themes.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
themes.consensus:
STRONG CONSENSUS: Bitso is the recommended platform for the first ~200 MXN live trade by a Mexico-based individual. 9 of 10 agents that addressed platform selection land on Bitso as primary. Convergent reasons: (1) Mexico-domiciled and CNBV-regulated under the 2018 Ley Fintech (IFPE license via its Nvio subsidiary), reducing counterparty/legal/account-freeze risk vs offshore venues; (2) native MXN funding via SPEI that is free and credits in ~1-2 hours, requiring only a CURP (which the owner has); (3) CRITICALLY, the API natively supports the exact required security stance — a TRADE-ONLY key ("Place orders" + "View balances" ON, "Make withdrawals" OFF) with an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses, up to 6 keys per account. This maps 1:1 onto the policy engine's blast-radius-containment design with zero workarounds.

TECHNICAL FIT (high confidence, multi-source): Bitso's REST API uses HMAC-SHA256 signing (signature = HMAC-SHA256(secret, nonce + HTTP_method + request_path + JSON_body)), implementable with Python stdlib only (hmac, hashlib, time, urllib.request, json) — consistent with the stdlib-only constraint. Orders go via POST /v3/orders (limit + market). A public sandbox exists at api-sandbox.bitso.com (Bitcoin/Ethereum testnet) plus read-only public ticker + authed balance endpoints, enabling the required read-only -> tiny-test-order validation. Per-book minimums are tiny (btc_mxn documented at minimum_value=5 MXN, minimum_amount=0.000075 BTC), so a 200 MXN order is ~40x the floor — but the executor MUST read GET /available_books live at startup rather than hardcode, since minimums are per-book and can change. Private rate limit is 300 req/min (requires completed KYC) — ample for a mechanical agent.

FEE DRAG (high confidence): At ~11 USD the dominant cost is fees+spread, not the fiat rail. Bitso entry-tier (lowest 30-day volume) maker/taker: MXN-quoted pairs 0.600%/0.780%; USD-stablecoin (USDT/USDC) pairs 0.300%/0.360%; BTC-quoted books ~0.075%/0.098%. Round-trip taker drag is ~1.56% on an MXN pair vs ~0.72% on a stablecoin book. Consensus mitigations: prefer LIMIT/maker orders; route through a USDC/USDT book to roughly HALVE fee drag; feed the realistic taker rate of the chosen pair into the cost-aware backtest (Deflated Sharpe) gate. CRITICAL ROUTING RISK flagged by multiple agents: the Bitso BASIC app "Quick Buy / convert" path uses a hidden SPREAD (~1.5-2%), 2-5x the Alpha/API maker-taker fees — the executor MUST hit the Trading API (POST /orders, Bitso Alpha) and NEVER the consumer convert endpoint.

CUSTODY (consensus): Leave the ~200 MXN on Bitso (exchange custody) for validation. Self-custody adds on-chain/withdrawal fees that dwarf an 11 USD position and needs the withdrawal-permission machinery the security stance avoids. The no-withdrawal key means the agent cannot move funds off-exchange; counterparty (Bitso insolvency) is the residual risk, acceptable at this size. Any rare manual withdrawal goes through the authenticated web UI with 2FA. Revisit only when balances grow materially.

ALTERNATIVES: Kraken and Binance are both genuinely accessible to a Mexican individual in 2026 (Kraken added native MXN/SPEI funding July 2025, 150 MXN min deposit, free, auto-converted to USD; Binance operates MXN fiat via the CNBV-regulated IFPE entity "Medá" launched ~Sep 2025). Both are viable documented fallbacks but secondary for THIS goal. Keep the Executor interface exchange-agnostic so a Kraken/Binance executor can be added later without touching the policy engine. Coinbase is the weakest fit — no native MXN/SPEI (card/FX friction) and ~0.40% maker / ~0.60% taker plus a stdlib-only tension (CDP keys require ECDSA/ES256 + JWT, which Python stdlib cannot cleanly sign).

TAX/LEGAL (LOW confidence, ALL secondary sources): Crypto is treated as intangible property; disposals (sell/swap/spend) are ISR-taxable enajenación for a persona física at progressive rates (cited 1.92%-35%); mere holding is not taxable. A 200 MXN gain is de minimis and almost certainly below any annual exemption. Crypto-to-crypto and MXN-to-stablecoin swaps are each taxable dispositions. CARF crypto reporting began Jan 2026 (exchanges report to SAT). Every cited number is blog-sourced and must be confirmed with SAT directly or a contador before scaling — but tax does NOT block the first trade; it creates a record-keeping obligation from trade #1.
themes.contradictions:
1. KRAKEN SPEI WITHDRAWAL FEE — DIRECT CONFLICT (unresolved). Bitso-comparison-sourced agents claim Kraken charges "0.5% with a 150 MXN MINIMUM withdrawal fee" (would consume ~75% of a 200 MXN balance, effectively disqualifying Kraken on off-ramp). The dedicated Kraken agent, citing Kraken's OWN support page, says MXN SPEI is 150 MXN minimum DEPOSIT and FREE, and did NOT confirm any 150 MXN minimum withdrawal FEE. The "0.5%/150 MXN" framing comes from Bitso, a competitor. A deposit minimum vs a withdrawal fee are different things. MUST be verified against Kraken's live withdrawal screen before trusting either. (Also: one aggregator claimed a 0.50% Kraken MXN DEPOSIT fee, conflicting with Kraken's official "Free" — trust the official page, verify in-app.)

2. BITSO MXN-PAIR BASE FEE — numeric spread, resolvable. Agents citing official bitso.com/fees give 0.600% maker / 0.780% taker for MXN pairs at the entry tier (<20,000 MXN/30d) — the authoritative figure. Others cited 0.5%/0.65% (that is the higher "up to 1.5M MXN" tier, not the lowest). Resolution: use 0.600%/0.780% for a permanently low-volume account, or fetch the live tier from GET /fees at runtime.

3. BITSO "0% TRADING FEE" — apparent contradiction, resolved. One agent cited "Bitso 0% basic trading." That refers to the BASIC app, which is the SPREAD path (~1.5-2% hidden), NOT zero cost. The API/Alpha path the executor uses pays explicit maker-taker fees. Not a real contradiction once the two surfaces are distinguished — and it is exactly why the executor must avoid the basic-app convert endpoint.

4. MINIMUM TRADE SIZE — soft conflict, resolvable. Aggregators cite "~$10 USD" (leaves 11 USD almost no headroom) vs "$1 USD"; the API-documented per-book minimum_value for btc_mxn is 5 MXN (huge headroom). The API figure is authoritative. Resolution: read GET /available_books live; do not trust aggregator minimums.

5. TAX EXEMPTION FIGURE — inconsistent, ALL low confidence. Cited annual enajenación-de-bienes exemption ranges from ~60,000 MXN to ~128,383 MXN (3x annual UMA), all from secondary blogs, none from primary SAT text. Only safe shared claim: disposals are ISR-taxable enajenación, holding is not, a 200 MXN gain is de minimis, keep records. The specific number must NOT be relied upon for filing.
blockers: NO HARD BLOCKER FOUND for a Mexico-based individual on Bitso. The first ~200 MXN trade is unblocked end-to-end (KYC via CURP/INE -> free SPEI funding -> trade-only IP-allowlisted key -> POST /orders). The items below are soft/conditional blockers to clear, not stoppers.

IP ALLOWLIST NEEDS A STATIC IP (conditional). The trade-only key's IP allowlist is the outer security wall, but the owner's backend runs on a residential MX IP (zen.chernandez.dev) that can rotate, which would break the allowlist and (on Binance) auto-expire the key. The executor must run from a host with a stable egress IP — the Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28) in memory is the right home. Until a static IP is pinned, either run from the VPS or accept no-IP-lock + rely on no-withdrawal + the policy engine (weaker).

KYC IS A PRECONDITION (owner gate, not a code blocker). Full 300 req/min rate limit AND MXN/SPEI funding both require completed KYC (CURP/INE + selfie + proof of address). Trivial for a Mexican resident but must be done before any live trade.

TINY-SIZE FEE/SPREAD DRAG IS A SOFT ECONOMIC BLOCKER, not a trade blocker. Round-trip cost is ~0.7% (USD/stablecoin maker) to ~1.6% (MXN taker), and far worse (~3-4%) if the basic-app spread path is hit by mistake. At 11 USD the first trade must be treated as a validation/learning cost, not a profit expectation; the mechanical edge must clear this hurdle in the backtest gate.

TAX RECORD-KEEPING OBLIGATION FROM TRADE #1 (not a blocker, an obligation). Every disposal is an ISR enajenación event; the agent must log per-fill timestamp/pair/side/MXN+USD notional/price/fee (and ideally DOF FX rate) so year-end ISR is mechanical. Confirm specifics with SAT/a contador before scaling.

UNVERIFIED RELIABILITY/UPTIME (caution, not a blocker). Bitso's never-hacked / monthly zk-proof solvency / CoinCover-insurance claims are largely self-reported; no primary status-page/incident history was located. Build defensively: handle HTTP 429 with backoff (never auto-retry into a lockout), treat 5xx as transient non-fills (re-query order state, do not blindly resubmit), and treat venue outage as a first-class failure path.

recommendations.recommendation: Choose Bitso as the Phase-3 platform and trade exclusively via the Bitso Alpha / Trading API (POST /v3/orders). NEVER use the consumer 'Quick Buy / convert' endpoint, which buries a ~1.5-2% spread (2-5x the maker-taker fee).
recommendations.why: 9/10 agents converge on Bitso for a Mexican individual (CNBV-regulated, free SPEI, CURP-only KYC, exact key-scoping match). The routing distinction is the single most expensive mistake to avoid at this size: hitting the spread path silently doubles-to-quintuples drag.

recommendations.recommendation: Mint the API key TRADE-ONLY: enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ optionally 'View account information'); explicitly leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF; enable 'Restrict use of API addresses' with the executor host's static IP. Use one dedicated key (of the 6 allowed).
recommendations.why: This is the outer hard wall the policy engine relies on: even a fully leaked key cannot exfiltrate funds, and IP-pinning caps it to the agent host. Natively supported by Bitso with zero workaround — high confidence across all agents.

recommendations.recommendation: Build BitsoExecutor with stdlib HMAC-SHA256 signing; persist the last-used nonce in the existing crash-safe journal so a restart cannot reuse or regress the nonce. Add a startup self-check (fail-closed) that asserts the key has NO withdrawal scope before any order is placed.
recommendations.why: Fits the stdlib-only constraint exactly (no SDK). Nonce regression after a crash would cause auth failures or replay risk; the journal already exists for crash-safety. The fail-closed key-scope check turns the security stance into an enforced invariant rather than an assumption.

recommendations.recommendation: Run the executor from a host with a STABLE egress IP — the Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28), not the rotating residential MX IP — so the IP allowlist holds. If a static IP is genuinely unavailable, fall back to no-IP-lock + no-withdrawal + policy engine, and document the weaker posture.
recommendations.why: The IP allowlist is half the blast-radius wall; a rotating residential IP breaks it (and on Binance even auto-expires the key). Memory already shows the VPS is the right home for the live executor.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement the smallest-safe validation sequence as gated steps: (1) public ticker GET, (2) authed GET /balance + GET /available_books + GET /fees with the trade-only key, (3) ONE tiny LIMIT order well inside the book and below a temporarily-lowered policy per-tx cap, (4) confirm fill via GET /order_trades and the audit log, (5) only then enable the autonomous loop. Refuse to place any order until the read-only probes succeed.
recommendations.why: This is the read-only-first, blast-radius-minimal path the project demands. A limit order gives deterministic price control (better for t+1 mechanical fills and the cheaper maker fee). Validating against the sandbox (api-sandbox.bitso.com) first removes real-money risk entirely before flipping to production.

recommendations.recommendation: Before the first order, call GET /available_books and read minimum_value / minimum_amount / minimum_price for the chosen book; assert order notional >= minimum (else the policy/executor aborts cleanly). Do NOT hardcode the 5 MXN or '$10' figure.
recommendations.why: Per-book minimums are live fields and can change; 200 MXN clears the documented 5 MXN btc_mxn floor by ~40x, but an aggregator-cited '$10' minimum on some pair could reject the order. Reading live makes the pre-trade check authoritative.

recommendations.recommendation: Encode entry-tier fees as the cost-model input and prefer LIMIT/maker orders. Set the cost-aware backtest (Deflated Sharpe) gate to the realistic TAKER rate of the chosen path: 0.780% for MXN pairs, 0.360% for USD/USDT pairs. Strongly prefer routing through a USDC/USDT book to roughly HALVE fee drag, treating the one-time MXN->stablecoin conversion as a fixed entry cost. Fetch the live tier from GET /fees rather than hardcoding.
recommendations.why: Fee drag is the binding constraint at 11 USD (~0.7% to ~1.6% round-trip). An honest cost gate prevents a false mechanical edge; stablecoin-book routing + maker orders is the concrete lever that cuts drag ~2x. This directly feeds the already-adopted Phase-2 cost-gate.

recommendations.recommendation: Build the executor defensively for unverified reliability: handle HTTP 429 with backoff and a conservative client-side rate limiter (well under 300 RPM, never auto-retry into a 1-min lockout that can escalate to 24h); treat 5xx as a transient non-fill by re-querying order state rather than blindly resubmitting; treat venue outage as a first-class failure (extend the existing staleness rule with an order-timeout/circuit path).
recommendations.why: Bitso's uptime/never-hacked claims are largely self-reported and not independently verified; absence of a found outage is not proof of reliability. Defensive handling avoids duplicate orders and lockouts.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep validation-phase funds in Bitso exchange custody; never enable the withdrawal scope on the bot key; have ZERO withdrawal code path in the executor. Do any rare manual withdrawal via the authenticated web UI with 2FA (+ address whitelist). Revisit self-custody only when balances reach the thousands of MXN.
recommendations.why: At 11 USD, on-chain/SPEI withdrawal fees dwarf the principal and self-custody adds attack surface the no-withdrawal stance deliberately avoids. The no-withdrawal key already bounds blast radius to 'funds already on Bitso'. Pragmatic-over-perfect for a single-user tiny trade.

recommendations.recommendation: Extend the HMAC-audited decision journal to double as the tax/SAT ledger: emit a per-disposal/per-fill record (timestamp, pair, side, MXN + USD notional, price, fee, and ideally the DOF FX rate at fill time). Do NOT rely on any cited exemption or bracket number — confirm crypto enajenación treatment, the exemption threshold, and any provisional-payment rule with a PRIMARY SAT source or a Mexican contador before scaling.
recommendations.why: Tax does not block the 200 MXN trade (de minimis) but the record-keeping obligation starts at trade #1, and CARF reporting to SAT began Jan 2026. All cited tax figures (60k-128,383 MXN exemptions, 1.92-35% ISR) are blog-sourced and low confidence; the journal makes year-end ISR mechanical without betting on unverified numbers.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep the Executor interface exchange-agnostic (SimExecutor -> PaperExecutor -> BitsoExecutor) so Kraken or Binance can be swapped in later without touching the policy engine. Document Kraken and Binance(Medá) as verified-accessible fallbacks; explicitly de-prioritize Coinbase (no native SPEI + ES256/JWT signing breaks the stdlib-only constraint).
recommendations.why: Both Kraken (native MXN SPEI since Jul 2025) and Binance (CNBV IFPE 'Medá' since Sep 2025) are real fallbacks if Bitso liquidity/coverage disappoints. Coinbase's CDP keys need ECDSA/ES256 + JWT, which Python stdlib cannot sign cleanly — a real architectural tension to avoid at this stage.

recommendations.recommendation: On the first live tiny order, MEASURE the realized effective cost (fill price + reported fee vs the public order-book mid) and reconcile against the assumed cost model. If effective cost >> 0.4%, you are on the spread path or a worse tier than assumed — switch routing before enabling the loop.
recommendations.why: This is the empirical verification step (verifiable-evidence stance): it catches the silent spread-path mistake and confirms the backtest cost assumption is honest, using the trade itself as ground truth rather than trusting config.

themes.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
themes.consensus:
All 10 agents converge on Bitso as the concrete, currently-real (2026) platform for a Mexico-resident individual: it is CNBV-supervised under the Ley Fintech (crypto as "activo virtual"; the IFPE/e-money authorization sits with its NVIO arm, while crypto trading sits under the broader virtual-asset framework — the normal status for all MX exchanges, not a Bitso gap), has native MXN/SPEI rails, accepts INE, and exposes an HMAC-signed REST trading API on PERSONAL (individual) accounts — no business account or company is required (a business account is for Payments/stablecoin APIs and is overkill).

KYC path (high confidence, multi-source): create account with email+phone, then complete the basic verified tier with INE front+back (passport/cedula also accepted) + a live selfie/liveness check. That tier's monthly limits (~150k MXN/month per several guides; another cites ~50k MXN for "Level 2") are 250x+ the 200 MXN size, so platform tier limits never bind. Proof of address (<3 months) and Constancia de Situacion Fiscal/RFC are only needed at the higher (Level 3) tier and are NOT required for the first trade. Verification is typically minutes to a few hours; budget up to 24h. The #1 avoidable rejection is a NAME/DATA MISMATCH against the government registry (CURP/RENAPO) — enter the full legal name exactly as on the INE (both apellidos + accents); other avoidable rejections are blurry document photos, expired ID, filtered/glasses selfies, and using a VPN during signup.

Funding: SPEI MXN deposit is free and near-instant via an in-app dedicated CLABE; no hard SPEI gateway minimum (some guides cite a practical ~100 MXN), and the smallest tradeable order is ~5 MXN per the available_books endpoint, so 200 MXN clears every floor with large headroom. TWO hard gates: (a) SPEI requires the verified tier + a CURP; (b) Bitso rejects THIRD-PARTY transfers — the funding bank/CLABE must be in the owner's OWN legal name matching KYC exactly.

Security stance is natively and exactly supported (high confidence, from Bitso's official docs.bitso.com): API keys have five independent permission toggles — Place orders, View balances, View account information, Perform security actions, Make withdrawals — plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 IPs, up to 6 keys per account. A TRADE-ONLY key = Place orders + View balances (+ View account information for read validation) ON; Make withdrawals AND Perform security actions OFF. With withdrawals off, a leaked key cannot exfiltrate funds — it can only churn trades inside the tiny balance, which the policy engine's rate-limit/cumulative-spend/notional/solvency rules further bound. 2FA via an authenticator app (Authy/Google Authenticator) is MANDATORY before any key can be created; SMS is only a weak fallback (SIM-swap vector) and must not be the primary factor. Bitso generates a one-time emergency restore code at 2FA setup and shows the API secret only once.

Validation sequence is fully supported: a documented SANDBOX (api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3) + HMAC-SHA256 signed requests (key + monotonically increasing nonce/Unix-timestamp). Smallest safe ramp: (1) sign successfully in sandbox; (2) production read-only GET account_status (returns transaction limits) + GET balance, asserting the key has NO withdrawal permission (fail-closed); (3) one tiny live order on an MXN pair; (4) read back the fill — gated behind the policy engine kill-switch + per-tx cap.

Economics (the real, non-procedural caveat): at ~11 USD the trade is a PLUMBING validation, not a profit event. A reported ~10 USD minimum order leaves little headroom (place near the full 200 MXN, on an MXN pair like BTC/MXN to avoid an extra MXN->USD spread). Taker fees ~0.65% / maker ~0.50% at the entry tier (the ~0.715%/0.550% figures are the BEST high-volume tier; small accounts pay more), so a round trip costs ~1.0-1.4%+ in fees plus bid/ask spread — material relative to edge but survivable for validation. Pull the ACTUAL fee tier from /v3/fees at runtime and feed real taker fee + observed spread into the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate; prefer maker/limit orders.

Blast radius via sub-accounts is NOT available to an individual: Bitso enforces one account per CURP (sub-accounts are a Business product); Binance sub-accounts are VIP-1/broker-gated. The correct and sufficient control at this size is the trade-only no-withdrawal IP-locked key + funding discipline (only the bot's risk capital on-exchange) + the existing policy engine. OKX is the only MX-available venue exposing true retail sub-accounts but is overkill at 11 USD.

Tax/legal is LOW confidence across agents and explicitly NOT verified against a primary SAT source: prevailing interpretation is general ISR on crypto disposals for a persona fisica (some cite an "enajenacion de bienes" annual exemption ~128,383 MXN; OECD CARF exchange-reporting from 2026). Immaterial at 200 MXN but must be confirmed with SAT/a contador before scaling. Individual crypto trading is legal in Mexico via a regulated ITF; banks themselves cannot hold crypto (why the ITF on-ramp is correct).
themes.contradictions:
1) Minimum trade size: sources conflict between ~10 USD and ~1 USD floors; separately the available_books endpoint reports a per-book ~5 MXN minimum_value. Net: 200 MXN clears whichever is true, but size near the full 200 MXN and read the live per-book minimum at startup rather than trusting any single figure.
2) Account tier limits: one agent reports the verified tier at ~150,000 MXN/month, another at ~50,000 MXN/month for "Level 2"; level numbering and peso figures come from secondary guides because Bitso's official help pages return HTTP 403 to automated fetch. All are far above 200 MXN, so it does not bind — but confirm the live number in-app.
3) Best-tier taker fee quoted as both 0.65% and 0.715% (and maker 0.50% vs 0.550%) — different sources/tiers; resolve by pulling /v3/fees for the actual account tier at runtime.
4) Practical minimum SPEI deposit cited as ~100 MXN by some, "no hard gateway minimum" by others; 200 MXN clears both.
5) "Verified users withdraw up to 6,000/month" appears in one review but is ambiguous (likely a USD/crypto sublimit) and irrelevant at this size.
No agent contradicted the core security model or the personal-account-is-sufficient conclusion.
blockers: HARD GATE (not a true blocker, but mandatory ordering): the verified KYC tier (INE front+back + live selfie) plus a CURP must be completed BEFORE any SPEI deposit or trade. Budget up to 24h for approval; do not schedule the first trade as time-critical.

HARD GATE: SPEI funding MUST come from a Mexican bank/fintech CLABE in the owner's OWN legal name matching KYC exactly. Third-party transfers (shared/family/different-name accounts) are rejected/returned. Blocks the timeline if the owner lacks a same-name MX bank account.

POTENTIAL/UNCONFIRMED 2026 BLOCKER: biometric CURP became the reinforced-identity standard for the Mexican financial sector during early/mid-2026 (press dates disagree; no primary source names crypto exchanges/ITFs explicitly). If Bitso enforces biometric CURP for new account opening and the owner lacks one, he must obtain it at RENAPO/registro civil first. Verify in Bitso's live onboarding + with RENAPO; do NOT treat press dates as binding. LOW confidence.

IP-ALLOWLIST FOOTGUN (operational blocker if mishandled): a dynamic/CGNAT residential Mexican IP will silently break an IP-allowlisted key. Either confirm a stable egress IP or run the executor from a fixed-IP host (the project already has a Netcup VPS 152.53.167.28). Note the residential MX IP is also the same kind of IP that has caused googlevideo 403s elsewhere — not relevant to Bitso REST but reinforces preferring a fixed VPS IP.

WITHDRAWAL-WHITELIST FOOTGUN (only if withdrawals are ever enabled later): Bitso's per-key, per-asset withdrawal address allowlist treats an EMPTY list as 'any address allowed' (default-allow, not default-deny). For treasuryforge this is moot because the key is no-withdrawal, but it must never be left empty if withdrawals are ever turned on.

NOT verified / do NOT rely on without primary-source confirmation: (a) Mexican tax treatment (ISR on disposals, ~128,383 MXN enajenacion exemption, CARF reporting) — LOW confidence, confirm with SAT/contador before scaling; (b) Bitso Terms-of-Service stance on bot/automated trading for personal accounts — not confirmed from a primary source, read bitso.com/legal before going live; (c) whether a Bitso ACCOUNT-LEVEL (non-API) withdrawal-address whitelist exists — not clearly documented (verify in-app/with support); (d) the secondary '$200M SIM-swap theft' figure.

recommendations.recommendation: Choose Bitso, PERSONAL (individual / persona fisica) account, as the Phase-3 platform. Do NOT open a Business account or form a company.
recommendations.why: Unanimous across all 10 agents: Bitso is the only CNBV-regulated, MXN/SPEI-native venue with an order-placement Trading API that works on individual accounts; a business account adds heavy KYC for zero benefit at 11 USD. Coinbase is a weak fit (limited MX functionality, no native MXN rail, US-regulated).

recommendations.recommendation: Before registering, assemble: current (non-expired) INE front+back, a phone with a working camera for the liveness selfie, your CURP (ideally the 2026 biometric version), and a same-name Mexican bank CLABE able to send SPEI. Type your full legal name EXACTLY as printed on the INE (both apellidos + accents); do not use a VPN during signup; photograph the INE flat, well-lit, all corners in frame, no glare.
recommendations.why: Name/data mismatch against the CURP/RENAPO registry is the #1 avoidable first-attempt KYC rejection for a Mexican individual; the same-name CLABE is required because Bitso rejects third-party SPEI funding. Getting these right makes a same-day first-attempt pass realistic.

recommendations.recommendation: At registration, verify in Bitso's live onboarding whether biometric CURP is now required for new account opening; if required and you lack it, obtain it at RENAPO first. Gate the live-executor enable flag behind 'KYC fully approved' confirmation rather than assuming instant approval.
recommendations.why: Biometric CURP became reinforced-identity-mandatory for the MX financial sector in 2026 (press dates conflict; no primary source confirms it applies to ITFs/Bitso). Treat as an unverified potential blocker to check live, not a settled fact.

recommendations.recommendation: Enable authenticator-app (TOTP) 2FA in Security settings BEFORE creating any API key, and never leave SMS as the primary factor. Save the one-time emergency restore code offline (separate from the authenticator) and seed the TOTP secret into a backup authenticator. Store the API secret immediately (shown only once) in the existing secret store.
recommendations.why: App 2FA is mandatory before key creation on Bitso and is the gate that unlocks the granular permission UI; SMS is a documented SIM-swap vector. Losing the 2FA device without the restore code means a support recovery delay that could strand an unattended agent.

recommendations.recommendation: Create a TRADE-ONLY API key: enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account information' for read validation); leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' OFF. Enable 'Restrict use of API addresses' and add the executor host's STATIC egress IP (prefer the fixed-IP Netcup VPS over a dynamic residential IP). Optionally create a second, read-only key for monitoring/reconciliation.
recommendations.why: This is the exact policy-engine blast-radius stance, natively supported and officially recommended by Bitso. With withdrawals off, a leaked key cannot exfiltrate funds. A dynamic/CGNAT residential IP would silently break the allowlist, hence the fixed-IP host.

recommendations.recommendation: Build the live Executor in staged, fail-closed steps: (1) HMAC-SHA256 signed-request success in the SANDBOX (api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3) with a strictly increasing Unix-timestamp nonce; (2) production read-only GET /account_status (assert limits cover the trade) + GET /balance, and a startup self-check that asserts the key has NO withdrawal permission (attempt a withdrawal-scoped call, require it to be denied); (3) at startup read per-book minimum_value/minimum_amount from GET /v3/available_books and reject any below-minimum intent in pre-flight; (4) place ONE order on an MXN pair near full 200 MXN, gated behind the policy engine kill-switch + per-tx cap; (5) read back the fill.
recommendations.why: Matches the requested smallest-safe read-only -> tiny-order validation, exploits the documented sandbox + read-only endpoints, and makes a misconfigured full-permission key fail closed. The fail-closed no-withdraw assertion is defense-in-depth on top of the policy engine.

recommendations.recommendation: Fund the account with ONE SPEI transfer of ~200 MXN from a same-name MX bank CLABE into the in-app generated CLABE (Wallet > MXN > Depositar). Put ONLY the bot's risk capital on-exchange; keep the rest off-exchange. Treat the funded amount as the hard solvency ceiling.
recommendations.why: SPEI is free and near-instant, but third-party funding is rejected. Because Bitso forbids a second personal account (one per CURP) and sub-accounts are Business-only, funding discipline + the single small balance IS the blast-radius cap — enforced in code by the solvency/cumulative-spend rules treating the funded amount as the ceiling.

recommendations.recommendation: Pull the ACTUAL account fee tier from GET /v3/fees at runtime (do not hardcode 0.65%/0.715%); feed the real taker fee + observed bid/ask spread into the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate, and prefer maker/limit orders. Treat the first 200 MXN trade as plumbing validation, not a profit event.
recommendations.why: At 11 USD, ~1.0-1.4%+ round-trip fees plus spread dominate any mechanical edge; the published best-tier fees understate what a tiny account pays. Using the live tier keeps the gate honest before greenlighting the strategy.

recommendations.recommendation: Have the audit/journal persist per-fill timestamp, price, amount, fee, cost basis and proceeds from trade #1 so a SAT-ready PnL/tax-basis ledger exists. Before scaling beyond the test, confirm ISR treatment of crypto disposals, any enajenacion exemption, and CARF reporting with a PRIMARY SAT source or a Mexican contador. Also read bitso.com/legal to confirm the ToS stance on bot/automated trading for personal accounts before going live.
recommendations.why: Tax treatment is LOW confidence across all agents (secondary blogs, no primary SAT citation) and the ~128,383 MXN exemption is unverified; immaterial at 200 MXN but must be confirmed before scaling. ToS-on-bots was not confirmed from a primary source. Logging from trade #1 is cheap and makes later compliance trivial.

recommendations.recommendation: Do NOT pursue sub-accounts for blast-radius isolation; do NOT plan around Binance sub-accounts; only consider OKX retail sub-accounts later if balances grow enough that funding isolation matters (verify OKX MX residency eligibility on okx.com first).
recommendations.why: Bitso is one-account-per-CURP (sub-accounts are Business-only); Binance sub-accounts are VIP-1/broker-gated. OKX is the only MX-available venue with true retail sub-accounts but adds KYC/custody/code surface for marginal benefit at 11 USD. The scoped key + small balance already achieve the isolation.

themes.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
themes.consensus:
Strong, near-unanimous convergence across all 10 agents on platform and key shape.

PLATFORM: Bitso is the recommended venue for a Mexico-based individual — MX-native, MXN/SPEI-funded, CNBV-regulated, and its API-key model maps one-to-one onto the project's security stance. Kraken is a credible fallback (trade-only/no-withdraw keys, IP restriction, SPEI MXN funding ~150 MXN min, standard individual KYC, plus defense-in-depth: withdrawals only to pre-whitelisted email/2FA-confirmed addresses). Binance and Coinbase are de-recommended (see contradictions/blockers).

KEY SHAPE (core answer): Bitso exposes 5 independent permission toggles — 'Place orders', 'View balances', 'View account information', 'Perform security actions', 'Make withdrawals'. Trade-only key = enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances'; leave 'Make withdrawals' OFF (no exfiltration possible) and 'Perform security actions' OFF (it can deactivate the account / block withdrawals — a non-obvious trap flagged by 3 agents). 2FA (authenticator app, not SMS) is MANDATORY before any key can be generated; up to 6 keys per account; keys are created/edited/deleted in the WEB UI only.

IP ALLOWLIST: Bitso supports 0-5 IPs per key, independent of permissions. Unlike Binance/OKX, Bitso has NO documented forced expiry for un-IP-restricted keys, so a residential dynamic IP is NOT a launch blocker — launch with zero IPs on the home connection (HMAC-SHA256+nonce auth still protects the key; no-withdraw caps blast radius), then harden by routing egress through the existing static-IP Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28) and allowlisting that one IP.

VALIDATION SEQUENCE (agreed): (1) read-only key (View balances) -> HMAC-signed balance call to prove signing/nonce/IP plumbing; (2) trade key (add Place orders); (3) ONE tiny ~200 MXN limit order on a LIQUID MXN pair, verify fill + audit log, confirm/cancel via open-orders. Gate the live path behind the policy engine kill-switch + per-tx notional cap so the first order physically cannot exceed ~200 MXN.

STDLIB FIT: Bitso uses plain HMAC-SHA256 (Authorization: 'Bitso key:nonce:signature', sig = hex HMAC-SHA256 over nonce+method+request_path+payload, nonce = strictly-increasing ms timestamp) — fully implementable in Python stdlib (hmac+hashlib), preserving the stdlib-only constraint.

RESIDUAL-RISK CONSENSUS (flagged by 4 agents): a trade-only/no-withdraw key is NOT zero-risk. A leaked trade-only key can still drain balance via self-trade/pump-and-dump on an illiquid pair (>$65M stolen this way Dec 2024-Jan 2025). Correct containment is exactly treasuryforge's policy engine — restrict the asset allowlist to LIQUID MXN majors (btc_mxn, eth_mxn, usdt_mxn), keep per-tx notional cap + cumulative budget tight, prefer limit orders with a slippage bound over naked market orders. At ~11 USD the attack is economically pointless, so it does not block the goal; it is an architectural guardrail for scale.

FEES: Bitso MXN spot order-book (API/Pro) fees ~0.60% maker / 0.78% taker per side -> ~1.2%-1.56% round trip in fees alone before spread. This is the relevant number for the executor (the consumer 'instant buy' wider spread is unverified and irrelevant to the API path).

TAX: Bitso does NOT withhold ISR; the individual self-reports using Bitso's downloadable annual statement (Feb/Mar); from 2026 CARF/automatic-exchange means exchanges report MX-client balances/gross transactions to SAT. Record-keeping (cost basis + proceeds + fee per trade) starts at trade #1.

CUSTODY: at ~11 USD, keep funds in Bitso exchange custody during the test phase; do not self-custody (on-chain fees dwarf principal) and add no external withdrawal address.
themes.contradictions:
1) Binance/OKX expiry constants — agents agree on the CONCLUSION (avoid Binance/OKX for an unattended agent without a static IP) but disagree on the exact number: one cites Binance's spot-permission auto-uncheck at 90 days, another cites a 30-day expiry plus 30-day idle auto-delete; OKX cited at 14 days. Both Binance figures trace to Binance announcements but the precise current value is UNVERIFIED here — confirm on Binance directly IF Binance is ever used. No conflict on the takeaway.

2) Coinbase viability — one agent lists Coinbase as a possible secondary venue (IP allowlist + documented rotation), while the stdlib-focused agent shows Coinbase Advanced Trade has moved to JWT with Ed25519/ECDSA signing, NOT implementable in Python stdlib — it would BREAK the stdlib-only constraint (only the legacy Exchange HMAC path is stdlib-compatible, and that is unlikely to be issued to a new MX retail individual). Resolution: Coinbase is effectively disqualified for THIS project's stdlib constraint; treat the 'secondary venue' framing as superseded.

3) IP allowlist necessity — posture split, not a factual conflict. Two agents call IP-pinning the 'single highest-value control' and recommend pinning to the VPS before launch; two others argue it is a FAST-FOLLOW, not a blocker, because no-withdraw + HMAC auth already cap blast radius at $11 size and Bitso allows zero-IP keys with no forced expiry. Reconciliation: launch is permitted with zero IPs at this tiny size; pin to the static VPS IP as the immediate next hardening step.
blockers: NONE are hard blockers for a Mexico-based individual on Bitso — the path is open. The items below are gating PRE-REQUISITES and CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY caveats, not blocks.

PRE-REQ: 2FA via authenticator app (Authy/Google Authenticator, NOT SMS) MUST be enabled on the Bitso account before the API-key page will issue any credentials. Without it, key creation is impossible.

PRE-REQ: KYC. The owner must complete standard individual KYC (full legal name, DOB, address, valid gov ID, likely CURP/proof-of-address for an MX resident) and fund ~200 MXN via SPEI before trading. Self-registerable; not a block, but it gates the first trade.

CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (tax/legal): exact MX ISR treatment of crypto gains is UNCERTAIN — there is NO dedicated crypto chapter in the ISR Law. The cited ~60,000 MXN/yr personal-asset exemption and the '20% in some cases' (PRODECON) framing are SECONDARY-source interpretations and must NOT be treated as settled law. Confirm with a primary SAT source or a contador before relying on any rate/exemption. (Amounts at 200 MXN are negligible, but record-keeping is real from trade #1, and CARF/automatic-exchange reporting to SAT begins 2026.)

CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (key longevity): Bitso docs are SILENT on whether an un-IP-restricted key auto-expires (unlike Binance/OKX which state limits explicitly). Absence of documentation is not proof of immortality — confirm with Bitso support before depending on indefinite key validity for an unattended agent.

CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (fees/funding): Bitso fee tiers are 30-day-volume based; verify the live 0.60%/0.78% tier shown in HIS account at trade time. If Kraken is used instead, verify the SPEI minimum (~150 MXN) and any ~0.50% SPEI funding fee on Kraken's own funding page (aggregator figures only).

OPERATIONAL (not a block, but causes silent failure if mishandled): if IP allowlisting is enabled on a residential dynamic MX IP, the key breaks on every ISP IP rotation. Mitigate by allowlisting the static Netcup VPS IP (152.53.167.28) instead, or launch with zero IPs at this size.

recommendations.recommendation: Use Bitso for the first ~200 MXN trade. Owner enables authenticator-app 2FA, completes KYC, funds ~200 MXN via SPEI. Create the trade key with ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' checked; leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF.
recommendations.why: Unanimous across 10 agents and verified against Bitso's official API docs. This is the exact least-privilege, no-withdrawal key shape the policy engine assumes; leaving 'Perform security actions' off closes a non-obvious account-deactivation/withdrawal-block trap. MX-native MXN/SPEI + CNBV regulation make it the lowest-friction real 2026 path for an MX individual.

recommendations.recommendation: Run the smallest-blast-radius validation sequence: (1) create a 'View balances'-only key, run an HMAC-signed balance call to prove signing/nonce/IP work read-only; (2) create the 'Place orders'+'View balances' trade key; (3) place ONE ~200 MXN LIMIT order on a liquid MXN pair, verify the fill + audit-log entry, then confirm/cancel via open-orders. Gate the live path behind the kill-switch + per-tx notional cap.
recommendations.why: Agreed read-only -> tiny-order ramp. Validating the signed balance call FIRST catches byte-exact path/payload and nonce errors (the #1 silent-401 cause) before any money can move; the per-tx cap makes it physically impossible for the first order to exceed ~200 MXN even if the strategy misbehaves.

recommendations.recommendation: Fix the executor fee model: SimExecutor defaults to fee_rate=0.001 (0.1%), but real Bitso MXN order-book fees are ~0.60% maker / ~0.78% taker per side. Make the BitsoExecutor and the cost-aware backtest gate (Deflated Sharpe + purged CV) model >=0.78% taker (or 0.60% maker) per side — a ~1.2-1.56% round-trip hurdle at this size — and prefer maker/limit orders.
recommendations.why: Load-bearing gap in C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\executor.py: the simulated 0.1% fee is ~6-8x lower than Bitso's real taker fee. A strategy that passes the backtest gate at 0.1% can be net-negative at 0.78%/side. The Phase-2 cost-aware gate must use the real fee to avoid greenlighting a losing strategy.

recommendations.recommendation: Add TWO new policy rules to contain the trade-only residual risk: (a) restrict the asset/pair allowlist to LIQUID MXN majors only (btc_mxn, eth_mxn, usdt_mxn) and reject any thin/non-allowlisted pair; (b) a price-deviation sanity guard that rejects any order whose limit price deviates more than X% from a fresh mid/reference price.
recommendations.why: A no-withdraw key is NOT zero-risk: a leaked trade-only key can still drain balance via self-trade/pump-and-dump on illiquid books (>$65M stolen this way Dec 2024-Jan 2025). Key scope alone does not stop this; the liquid-pair allowlist + price-sanity guard are the architectural mitigations and the correct place to contain it as size grows beyond $11.

recommendations.recommendation: Store the Bitso API secret in the OS keychain via Python `keyring` (Windows Credential Manager / DPAPI, per-user SID scope) through a thin SecretStore wrapper + a one-time seed CLI. Never put the secret in a .env/env var, never log it, never write it to the journal or HMAC audit log; discard the decrypted value after building each signed request. Strengthen the Windows login password and consider BitLocker.
recommendations.why: Env vars leak into child processes, logs, and crash dumps. keyring/DPAPI gives encrypted-at-rest + per-user isolation as a single near-stdlib dependency. Caveat: DPAPI does NOT stop same-user malware, so this complements (does not replace) the trade-only/no-withdraw key scope, which remains the real blast-radius cap. DPAPI strength derives from the Windows password, hence the password/BitLocker step.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement BitsoExecutor signing in pure stdlib (hmac+hashlib): sig = hex(HMAC-SHA256(secret, nonce+method+request_path+payload)), Authorization = 'Bitso {key}:{nonce}:{sig}', nonce = strictly-increasing epoch-ms. Add a golden-vector contract test reproducing Bitso's documented example, a strictly-monotonic nonce that never repeats across processes, and a server-time clock-skew guard.
recommendations.why: Bitso's HMAC scheme keeps the stdlib-only constraint intact (unlike Coinbase Advanced Trade's JWT/Ed25519, which would break it). Byte-exact prehash mismatch and nonce reuse are the top silent-401 causes; the golden-vector test and monotonic-nonce guard lock them down before the first live order.

recommendations.recommendation: Make the executor fail-closed and loud on auth/permission/IP errors: refuse to trade if the secret is missing or any auth error occurs, surface a clear 'IP not allowlisted' / 'permission denied' message (not a generic 401), and on a Bitso rate-limit lockout treat it as a HARD circuit-breaker (stop all outbound calls, capped exponential backoff with jitter, escalate to kill-switch) — never busy-loop retries.
recommendations.why: Bitso allows 300 private req/min (≈100x the agent's need), but continuous 1-minute lockouts can escalate to a 24-HOUR account block — a runaway retry loop during an open position is the real ban risk. Distinct, loud error paths make an IP rotation or expiry diagnosable instead of looking like a key compromise.

recommendations.recommendation: Self-meter API calls client-side with a sliding 1-minute window throttle capped well under the limit (e.g. <=60 req/min vs the 300 ceiling), budgeting <=5 reads + 1 write per decision cycle. Do not rely on server-returned quota headers on Bitso (it publishes none, unlike Binance's X-MBX-USED-WEIGHT).
recommendations.why: Bitso gives no used-quota response headers, so the only reliable defense is a client-side counter. A tight self-imposed cap means a bug cannot approach the lockout threshold, and order cancellations are exempt so safety cancels stay free.

recommendations.recommendation: Launch with a zero-IP trade-only key on the home connection IF the residential IP is dynamic; then immediately harden by routing the executor's egress through the existing static-IP Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28) and allowlisting that single IP on Bitso. Make outbound HTTP egress a config flag (proxy-aware), not a rewrite. Verify the live egress IP (curl ifconfig.me from the executor host) before enabling any allowlist.
recommendations.why: Bitso permits zero-IP keys with no documented forced expiry, so at $11 size (no-withdraw + HMAC auth already cap blast radius) IP-pinning is a fast-follow, not a launch blocker. Pinning a DRIFTING residential IP causes silent auth failures on every ISP rotation — reusing the known-static VPS avoids a new monthly cost (~11 USD/mo proxies would rival the whole first trade) and gives a stable allowlist target.

recommendations.recommendation: Add an export of a SAT-friendly per-trade ledger (CSV: timestamp, pair, side, qty, price, fee, MXN cost-basis + proceeds) from the existing HMAC audit journal. Owner downloads the Bitso annual statement each Feb/Mar and confirms the exact ISR treatment/exemption with a primary SAT source or a contador BEFORE relying on any specific rate. Do NOT treat the ~60k MXN exemption or '20%' figure as settled law.
recommendations.why: There is no dedicated crypto chapter in the ISR Law; the cited rates/exemptions are secondary-source interpretations (LOW confidence). The record-keeping obligation is real from trade #1 and CARF/automatic-exchange reporting to SAT starts 2026, so a clean per-trade ledger makes eventual filing trivial regardless of how the rate resolves.

recommendations.recommendation: Custody at this size: keep the ~200 MXN in Bitso exchange custody during the test phase — do NOT self-custody / withdraw to a cold wallet, and add NO external withdrawal address to the account. Treat any future funding/defunding as a manual, human-only, 2FA web-UI action on a SEPARATE key (with 'Make withdrawals' bound to an address allowlist of the owner's own wallet).
recommendations.why: At ~11 USD, on-chain withdrawal fees would dwarf the principal and self-custody adds key-management risk with no benefit. Adding no external withdrawal address means a leaked agent key (no-withdraw scope) has no exfiltration path even in the worst case. Keeping withdrawals as a separate human key prevents ever co-locating a withdraw-capable key with the agent process.

recommendations.recommendation: Document a key rotation + emergency-revoke runbook: create-new-key-before-deleting-old (avoid downtime); secret loaded once from the keychain (shown only once at creation, never re-retrievable); emergency revoke = delete the key in the Bitso web UI (hard revoke) + flip the local policy-engine kill-switch (soft revoke). Adopt a self-imposed ~90-day rotation cadence as a calendar reminder; record key-id + egress-IP per decision in the audit log for traceability.
recommendations.why: Across all venues the secret is shown only once and the only revocation primitive is delete, so the executor must load-once and support hot-swap. Bitso has no forced rotation (favorable for an unattended agent) but that shifts the rotation burden to the owner. Pairing exchange-side delete (hard) with the kill-switch (soft) gives a complete incident-response path.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep Kraken as the documented fallback venue only (do NOT use Binance or Coinbase Advanced Trade). If Bitso onboarding ever fails: Kraken supports trade-only keys (Query Funds + Query/Modify/Cancel Orders, Withdraw OFF), editable IP restriction, SPEI MXN funding (~150 MXN min), and adds a hard second control — withdrawals only to pre-approved email/2FA-confirmed addresses.
recommendations.why: Binance silently auto-expires/deletes un-IP-whitelisted trading keys (a foot-gun for an unattended agent) and Coinbase Advanced Trade moved to JWT/Ed25519 signing that BREAKS the stdlib-only constraint. Kraken is the only fallback that preserves both the security stance and (via HMAC signing) the stdlib constraint, with weaker-but-workable MX fiat fit.

themes.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
themes.consensus: All 10 agents independently converge on Bitso as the concrete, currently-real path for a Mexico-based individual: it is CNBV-regulated under the Ley Fintech (IFPE/ITF), MXN/SPEI-native, and its API natively supports the exact security stance Phase-3 demands. (1) PLATFORM/KEYS: Bitso keys expose five independent permission toggles — 'Place orders', 'View balances', 'View account information', 'Perform security actions', 'Make withdrawals' — so a true trade-only / no-withdrawal key is built by enabling ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account information' if reconciliation/limits endpoints are used) and leaving 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF. IP allowlisting ('Restrict use of API addresses', 0-5 IPs) and mandatory 2FA-at-creation are supported; up to 6 keys per account (use separate keys for stage vs prod). This makes a leaked key doubly contained: cannot withdraw (no permission) and cannot be used off-IP. (2) AUTH IS STDLIB-FRIENDLY: private calls are HMAC-SHA256 over the exact concatenation nonce+HTTP_method+request_path+JSON_body, header `Authorization: Bitso {key}:{nonce}:{signature}`, nonce a strictly-increasing unique integer (ms timestamp works) — implementable with Python stdlib hmac/hashlib/urllib, preserving the stdlib-only constraint, and the signed body bytes must be byte-identical to the body sent. (3) READ-ONLY-FIRST SEQUENCE is well-defined: Tier 1 (no auth) public market data (available_books / ticker / order_book) validates the HTTP client, JSON parsing, and symbol mapping with zero key risk; Tier 2 (auth, read-only) signed GET /api/v3/balance returns a 200 + parseable JSON and is the single best proof that signature/nonce/key all work without any order risk; only AFTER both pass do you place an order. (4) NEGATIVE TEST is concrete and deterministic: sign a withdrawal request with the trade-only key and ASSERT HTTP 401 with Bitso error.code '0202' ('API key is not authorized to execute the requested method') — distinct from 0201 (bad credentials/signature) and from any 2xx; a separate off-IP probe yields 0213. (5) PLACE != FILL: POST /api/v3/orders returns ONLY {success, payload:{oid}} with NO fill data, so the executor must be a place->oid->poll design; fill status comes from Look Up Orders (GET /orders/{oid}) with statuses queued/open/'partially filled'/completed/cancelled and filled = original_amount - unfilled_amount; lookup DROPS completed/cancelled orders after ~1h so a fast full-fill must be confirmed via user_trades or a balance delta, not mis-flagged as a reject. (6) IDEMPOTENCY: origin_id (client-supplied, <=40 chars, [A-Za-z0-9_-], rejected if it duplicates an ACTIVE order) makes retries safe; journal the id BEFORE POST, and on timeout query GET /orders?origin_ids=<id> before re-POSTing. (7) RECONCILE against exchange TOTAL (=available+locked, pending excluded) not available, plus user_trades for the real fee. (8) KILL-SWITCH: DELETE /api/v3/orders/all (cancel) is native and appears exempt from rate limits; 'flatten' = enumerate non-MXN balances and market-sell each to *_mxn (spot has no 'close position' call). (9) RATE LIMITS are HTTP 420 (not 429), ~60 rpm public / ~300 rpm private (300-tier needs full KYC) — vastly above this low-frequency agent's needs. (10) CUSTODY: keep the ~200 MXN on Bitso — the no-withdrawal key IS the custody safeguard; self-custody at 11 USD adds fees and key-management risk for no benefit. (11) TAX: BUYING crypto is NOT a taxable event in Mexico (only sale/swap/spend = enajenacion triggers ISR), so the first 200 MXN buy is clean; CARF/OECD auto-reporting starts 2026 so assume SAT sees all Bitso activity, and crypto-to-crypto swaps are each a taxable disposal — keep a per-fill cost-basis ledger. The recommended ladder is unanimous: in-repo PaperExecutor (capital-free) -> [optional sandbox] -> prod read-only key (validates IP allowlist + scope, zero spend) -> negative withdrawal probe (assert 0202) -> one minimum-size (~5 MXN) far-from-market or marketable test order with reconcile -> the full ~200 MXN order, all under the policy engine's per-tx cap.
themes.contradictions: Sharp, unresolved contradiction on whether a usable Bitso SANDBOX exists. Three agents assert high/medium confidence that stage/sandbox is real (https://stage.bitso.com/api/v3/ and/or https://api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/, fundable with BTC/ETH testnet, same signed endpoints, base-URL-swap-only). But the SAME research flags it as likely STALE/unreliable: (a) two different sandbox hostnames in Bitso's own docs (doc drift), (b) funding instructions reference Ethereum's ROPSTEN testnet, deprecated since Sept 2022 — a strong staleness signal, (c) one search source flatly asserted Bitso 'does not provide a sandbox', (d) no agent confirmed an individual can self-provision stage credentials (it may be partner-gated). Net: sandbox is an UNVERIFIED LEAD, not a dependable fact — must be live-probed before trust. Second contradiction — MINIMUM order size for btc_mxn: one agent cites minimum_value=5 MXN / minimum_amount=0.000075 BTC (so 200 MXN clears with ~40x headroom); another cites minimum_value=25 MXN / minimum_amount=0.003 BTC (where 0.003 BTC at 2026 prices is thousands of MXN, which would BLOCK a 200 MXN BTC order); a third cites a generic ~$10 USD floor. These per-pair minimums are read live from available_books, so the resolution is the same regardless: never hardcode, fetch minimum_value AND minimum_amount at runtime and pick a book where max(minimum_amount*price, minimum_value) <= notional. Third (minor): exact RPM numbers and whether a Retry-After header exists on 420 came partly from search summaries — verify on the live throttling-errors page before hardcoding backoff.
blockers: KYC is a HARD prerequisite: the 300-rpm private tier and signed private endpoints require a fully identity-verified Bitso account. The owner must complete Bitso KYC (CURP/INE-based) himself before any live key works. Not a code issue but gates everything.

No Kraken spot sandbox exists — Kraken's only official demo API is futures-only (demo-futures.kraken.com). This BLOCKS a Kraken-hosted spot-sandbox validation path but does NOT block the project (use Bitso + in-repo PaperExecutor). Do not design a Kraken-spot-sandbox executor.

Bitso sandbox/stage is UNVERIFIED and shows staleness (deprecated Ropsten testnet, conflicting hostnames, possibly partner-gated). Until live-probed it cannot be relied on as the validation environment; the safe fallback is in-repo PaperExecutor + prod read-only key + one tiny live order.

btc_mxn minimum-amount (0.003 BTC per one source) may vastly exceed 200 MXN at 2026 prices, which would hard-reject a 200 MXN BTC order. BLOCKS only if BTC is assumed; resolved by selecting the book at runtime from available_books. Do not assume btc_mxn works for an 11 USD order.

Fee/spread drag is structurally high at 11 USD size (not a hard blocker but a real economic floor): Bitso MXN taker fees reach ~0.65% (maker ~0.50%), so a buy+sell round-trip is ~1.3% (~2.6 MXN) BEFORE bid-ask spread. At 200 MXN this drag plus spread means the mechanical strategy's per-trade edge must exceed ~1.3-2% just to break even — confirm against the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate before going autonomous.

Tax/legal certainty is NOT verifiable from the sources gathered (all secondary blogs, not primary SAT text). The first 200 MXN BUY is non-taxable, so the first trade is unblocked — but before the strategy starts SELLING/swapping, ISR computation, the annual exemption figure (cited variously ~60,000 MXN or ~128,383 MXN / 3x UMA), and CARF reporting obligations MUST be confirmed with a contador or primary SAT source. Treat as a record-keeping obligation now, a confirm-before-scaling blocker later.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor the Executor interface from the current synchronous `execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill` (treasuryforge/executor.py:20-21) into a two-phase contract: place_order(intent) -> client_order_id/oid, then reconcile(oid, origin_id) -> Fill via polling. Bitso's POST /orders returns ONLY {payload:{oid}} with no fill data, so the current single-call model cannot represent a real fill. Keep SimExecutor conforming to the new interface (its fill is synchronous but should still return through reconcile()).
recommendations.why: The single most load-bearing design fact: place and fill-confirmation are SEPARATE on Bitso. The current Protocol assumes an atomic fill, which is structurally impossible against the real API. Every other live-executor recommendation depends on this split existing.

recommendations.recommendation: Build a stdlib-only BitsoReadOnlyExecutor.health_check() that signs GET /api/v3/balance and asserts HTTP 200 + parseable JSON + expected currencies present, and gate ALL order placement behind it passing this session. Implement the _sign(nonce, method, path, body) helper (HMAC-SHA256 over nonce+method+path+body, header `Bitso {key}:{nonce}:{signature}`, monotonic nonce = max(prev+1, time.time_ns())) and unit-test it against Bitso's documented example vector BEFORE any live call.
recommendations.why: GET /balance is the zero-risk canary that proves signature/nonce/key/IP-allowlist all work before any money moves; verifying the signer against the doc example vector offline removes the largest live-debugging risk. Stdlib hmac/hashlib/urllib preserve the stdlib-only constraint and reuse the existing audit.py HMAC pattern.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: when generating the production Bitso API key, enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (add 'View account information' only if reconciliation endpoints need it); leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF; enable 'Restrict use of API addresses' and add the agent host's current public IPv4; complete 2FA. Use a separate key for any stage testing.
recommendations.why: This IS the blast-radius containment the 8-rule policy engine assumes — a compromised key can neither withdraw nor be used off-IP. Directly satisfies the Phase-3 security stance with no code dependency.

recommendations.recommendation: Add a one-time validate_no_withdraw() pre-flight gate that signs a withdrawal request with a deliberately-invalid, impossible-to-settle payload (sub-minimum amount + empty/non-allowlisted destination) and ASSERTS HTTP 401 / error.code=='0202'. Treat any 2xx or other code as FAIL and refuse to arm the live executor. Wire it into startup self-checks alongside verify_chain().
recommendations.why: There is no permission-introspection endpoint; the only reliable proof a key cannot withdraw is to call the method and read the deterministic 0202 authorization error. Poisoning the payload makes the probe safe-by-construction even if scope were wrong. Distinguishes 'no permission' (0202) from 'bad signature' (0201).

recommendations.recommendation: Fix the Fill model's fee assumption before trusting any live reconciliation. Fill.quote_delta (treasuryforge/types.py:64-74) hard-codes the fee as paid in QUOTE, but Bitso charges a BUY fee in the BASE asset received (per user_trades fees_currency). Generalize Fill to carry fee_currency and debit the correct leg, and have the live executor read fees_amount/fees_currency from GET /user_trades after each fill.
recommendations.why: If unfixed, every live BUY will reconcile as a mismatch (btc.total lower than expected by exactly the base-denominated fee), permanently false-tripping the kill-switch. This is a latent correctness bug the live data will immediately expose.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement reconcile() against exchange TOTAL (=available+locked, pending excluded) per currency, comparing with PER-ASSET tolerance (BTC abs diff < 1e-8, MXN < 0.01) using decimal.Decimal quantized to each asset's scale — do NOT reuse SimWallet's blanket 1e-9 clamp (wallet.py:41-48), which is far tighter than MXN's 0.01 quantum. Reconcile within the ~1h lookup window with a user_trades/balance-delta fallback for fast full-fills that vanish from Look Up Orders. On any divergence, trip the existing kill-switch instead of placing more intents.
recommendations.why: Bitso returns decimal STRINGS while SimWallet uses float; an open/partial order parks funds in 'locked' so comparing against 'available' alone false-alarms; and Look Up Orders drops completed orders after ~1h so a fast fill must be confirmed elsewhere or mis-flagged as a reject. All three break naive reconciliation.

recommendations.recommendation: Make the executor base_url a single config constant (sandbox/stage vs prod) and add a CLI/runtime gate (e.g. stages: paper -> prod-ro -> prod-tiny -> prod-live) so each rung of the validation ladder is enforced in code, not just discipline. First live run STOPS after the read-only stage; the first real order = the exchange minimum (fetched from available_books), not the full 200 MXN.
recommendations.why: Same code path against every environment is high-fidelity (sandbox/prod differ only by base URL), and code-enforced staging prevents accidentally skipping straight to a real order. Smallest-possible first order bounds first-trade risk to a few MXN.

recommendations.recommendation: Read minimum_value AND minimum_amount live from GET /api/v3/available_books at startup and reject any book where max(minimum_amount*price, minimum_value) > order_notional. NEVER hardcode btc_mxn or its minimums — pick the smallest-minimum allowlisted book that fits 200 MXN. Mirror this as a low-end floor in the policy engine's notional check.
recommendations.why: Sources disagree on btc_mxn minimums (5 vs 25 MXN value; 0.000075 vs 0.003 BTC amount); 0.003 BTC at 2026 prices would hard-reject a 200 MXN order. Runtime fetch resolves the contradiction and prevents a silent sub-minimum reject leaving a position un-flattened.

recommendations.recommendation: Use origin_id as a deterministic idempotency key derived from the policy-approved intent's audit/journal decision id (encoded to <=40 chars in [A-Za-z0-9_-]); persist it in the crash-safe journal BEFORE the HTTP POST. On timeout/5xx, first GET /orders?origin_ids=<id> to check if the order landed before re-POSTing with the SAME id. Mark the intent terminally-executed in the journal as soon as a fill is observed, and gate retries on journal state — NOT on exchange rejection (dedup only covers ACTIVE orders, so a re-POST after fill creates a NEW order). Add a test for retry-after-fill proving no second order.
recommendations.why: Makes the at-most-once intent survive network retries/420s without double-spending the 200 MXN. The active-orders-only dedup window is a correctness trap: the journal, not the exchange, must remain the at-most-once source of truth.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: complete Bitso KYC (full identity verification) before generating any live key, and budget for fee/spread drag — confirm the chosen pair's live minimum and fee tier on the order book before the first order, expecting ~1.3% round-trip taker drag at 200 MXN. Keep the ~200 MXN on Bitso (custodial); do withdrawals only manually via the web UI.
recommendations.why: KYC is a hard prerequisite for any signed private call and the 300-rpm tier. The no-withdrawal key is the custody safeguard, so self-custody at 11 USD adds fees/key-risk for no benefit. Fee drag is the real economic floor the strategy must clear.

recommendations.recommendation: Wire the live kill-switch as two native actions: cancel via DELETE /api/v3/orders/all (parse the returned payload array as source of truth, then GET open orders to confirm none remain — a FILLED order is silently NOT in the cancelled array), and flatten via enumerate non-MXN balances + POST market sell (side=sell, type=market, book=<asset>_mxn, major=balance) skipping sub-minimum dust with an alert. Do not rate-limit-gate the cancel path; do backoff the GET-reconcile and flatten sells.
recommendations.why: The policy engine has a kill_switch flag (policy.py) but no live cancel/flatten action wired. Cancel-all appears exempt from the 300-rpm limit (ideal panic path); spot has no 'close position' call so flatten must enumerate-and-sell. Never assume cancel succeeded from a 200 alone.

recommendations.recommendation: Classify every API response as SUCCESS / DEFINITE-FAIL / INDETERMINATE: catch HTTP 420 (throttling, NOT 429) specifically with bounded exponential backoff + jitter; treat 420/5xx/timeouts as INDETERMINATE (the order may have landed server-side) and resolve via origin_ids lookup or reconciliation rather than blind-retry; treat 400-funds (insufficient balance) as a hard reject feeding the solvency rule. Throttle the reconcile poller to ~1 poll/sec with bounded max_attempts so it never tight-loops or blocks the policy engine.
recommendations.why: Bitso signals rate limits with HTTP 420, not the 429/418 most clients assume; a retry storm in the reconcile poller (not normal trading) is the real rate-limit risk. Branching on HTTP class before parsing payload lets the audit layer log WHY each tx was rejected.

recommendations.recommendation: Extend the existing audit.py HMAC hash-chain to the live executor: emit one append-only JSONL record BEFORE send (intent + nonce + request-body SHA-256 + policy decision-id) and one AFTER receive (status + latency + response body), NEVER logging the API secret or the Authorization signature. Add a post-run reconciliation pass that re-fetches Bitso's own /user_trades + /open_orders and diffs against the local log, tripping the kill-switch on any mismatch. Log the key fingerprint (not secret) so rotations are visible, and keep a one-command key-rotation runbook.
recommendations.why: A dropped/retried request can succeed server-side while the client thinks it failed; the exchange's own records are ground truth. The audit log's role is detection/forensics (the no-withdraw key is the real defense), and the per-fill records double as SAT cost-basis substantiation. Reuses the proven audit.py chain.

recommendations.recommendation: Serialize ALL signed Bitso calls (trade + reconcile + monitoring) through ONE monotonic nonce source persisted in the crash-safe journal, single-threaded. Do not share one API key across concurrent processes.
recommendations.why: Concurrent signed calls can emit out-of-order nonces and get silently rejected, breaking the reconcile poll at the worst moment. A single persisted monotonic counter is the only safe pattern.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: live-PROBE the Bitso sandbox/stage before depending on it — curl https://api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/available_books and a signed GET /balance against stage, and confirm with Bitso support whether stage credentials are self-serve for an individual and the CURRENT testnet-funding method (Ropsten is deprecated). If stage is dead/gated, fall back to: in-repo PaperExecutor + signature verified against the doc example vector offline + prod read-only key + one minimum-size live order.
recommendations.why: The sandbox is the single biggest contradiction in the research — claimed real by some agents, flagged stale (deprecated Ropsten, conflicting hostnames, possibly partner-gated) by others. It must be live-probed, not trusted from docs, and the project must not block on it.

recommendations.recommendation: For the first ~200 MXN order prefer a MARKET order specified in MINOR (MXN) — e.g. minor=200, side=buy — so the whole peso amount converts in one fill on a liquid book and partial-fill handling is near-moot; still run the bounded reconcile loop because placement returns no fill data. Keep a marketable-LIMIT/postonly path as the spread-control option. Log every fill with MXN market value + UTC timestamp + fee + fee_currency to build the cost-basis ledger.
recommendations.why: A 200 MXN market-in-minor order fills fully and instantly on a liquid book, making partial fills a near-zero edge case, while the cost-basis log is needed regardless for eventual ISR on sells.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: treat all tax claims as UNVERIFIED secondary-source guidance. The first 200 MXN BUY is non-taxable so proceed, but BEFORE the strategy starts selling/swapping, confirm with a Mexican contador or primary SAT (RMF 2026) source: ISR computation on enajenacion, the exact annual exemption figure (sources disagree: ~60,000 MXN vs ~128,383 MXN / 3x UMA), that each crypto-to-crypto swap is a separate taxable disposal, and CARF auto-reporting obligations (starts 2026 — assume SAT sees all Bitso activity). Prefer fiat(MXN)<->one-asset trades over crypto-to-crypto to minimize taxable-event count.
recommendations.why: All tax findings came from blogs, not primary SAT text, and the exemption figure conflicts across sources. The buy is unblocked, but selling without confirmed treatment is a real legal/record-keeping risk once the mechanical strategy generates many disposals.

recommendations.recommendation: Do NOT adopt the Kraken CLI paper mode or any Kraken spot path as a runtime dependency. Kraken has no spot sandbox (only futures demo at demo-futures.kraken.com), and the Kraken CLI is an experimental client-side Node/Go simulator that conflicts with the stdlib-only/local-first stance. Keep the in-repo PaperExecutor as the primary capital-free validation gate; treat Kraken CLI only as an optional offline cross-check if ever desired.
recommendations.why: Avoids a dead-end engineering path and a stdlib-violating external dependency for a money-moving agent. Confirms Bitso (not Kraken) is the spot venue for a Mexico individual.

themes.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
themes.consensus:
All 10 agents converge on a single coherent path, and the load-bearing numbers were re-verified live against the authoritative Bitso endpoint (GET https://api.bitso.com/v3/available_books/, fetched 2026-06-16) during this synthesis.

VENUE: Bitso is the unanimous choice for a Mexico-based individual. It is Mexico-born, the only exchange fully registered/regulated under CNBV (Ley Fintech), MXN-native (SPEI on/off-ramp), and exposes a documented HMAC-signed v3 REST trading API. Binance operates in MX only via a separate IFPE subsidiary and is not directly CNBV-registered; it is cheaper on paper (0.10% flat) but carries weaker MX legal footing and fiat-bridge friction, so it is a later optimization, not the first-trade venue.

SIZE CLEARS ALL MINIMUMS (verified live): every *_mxn book has minimum_value = 10 MXN. ~200 MXN is ~20x that floor. minimum_amount is tiny (btc_mxn 0.0000006 BTC, eth_mxn 0.000008 ETH, usdt_mxn 0.01 USDT). So order minimums are NOT the binding constraint — the full position is round-trippable and does not get trapped as dust.

THE REAL CONSTRAINT IS FEE DRAG (verified live): Bitso *_mxn flat_rate is maker 0.60% / taker 0.78% PER SIDE (NOT the 0.50%/0.65% several agents cited — those are stale API-doc sample values; the live flat_rate is 0.006/0.0078). A taker round-trip ≈ 1.56% (~3.1 MXN on 200 MXN); a maker round-trip ≈ 1.20%. Live top-of-book spreads are negligible (<0.02% on BTC/MXN; USDT/MXN tighter). At ~11 USD, order-book depth slippage is a non-issue (the order never walks the book). Consensus rule: the mechanical strategy must clear ~1.2–1.6% round-trip plus spread to be net-positive; prefer post-only maker (limit) orders, and feed this real cost floor into the Phase-2 Deflated-Sharpe / purged-CV cost-aware gate.

FRAMING: At 11 USD this is a paid REHEARSAL of the live executor, not an investment. Expect to end slightly down on a flat market (~3–4 MXN of fee "tuition"). Success = the executor placed, filled, and reconciled a real order within policy limits — something simulation cannot validate (real fills, real fee debits, real production-key permission enforcement, real KYC/account state).

SECURITY STANCE FULLY SUPPORTED: Bitso natively offers granular, independent API permissions — enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account info' if needed for solvency); leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF. IP allowlist supports up to 5 IPs ('Restrict use of API addresses'); up to 6 keys/account. This exactly matches the trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted blast-radius model. The no-withdrawal scope doubles as economic protection (it also prevents the executor from ever incurring an on-chain withdrawal fee that would strand an 11 USD balance).

CUSTODY: Unanimous — keep funds custodial on Bitso at this size. Self-custody's counterparty-risk benefit is marginal vs. added key-management risk and on-chain withdrawal cost; a hardware wallet costs more than the position. Exit (later, when larger) to MXN via SPEI, never on-chain at this size. The genuine total-loss tail is NON-market (Bitso counterparty/custody failure, or self-custody key loss) — price-driven total loss is near-impossible for BTC short-term (~54% annualized vol, ~1–2% typical daily moves) PROVIDED the pilot is confined to a top-cap allowlisted asset (BTC or ETH), NOT a microcap.

A FREE SANDBOX EXISTS: Bitso provides api-sandbox.bitso.com (personal accounts, dummy KYC, BTC/ETH testnet funding) that exercises the REAL API contract for $0. This belongs as a mandatory middle tier between SimExecutor and live. Validate the full intent→policy→execute→reconcile loop in sandbox BEFORE spending any pesos.

TAX (effectively zero at this size, but record-keeping is mandatory): Buying/holding is NOT a taxable event; each SELL or crypto-to-crypto swap is a taxable 'enajenacion' under ISR, valued at MXN FMV. Any realized gain at 200 MXN is orders of magnitude below the cited ~3x-annual-UMA exemption (~105k–128k MXN) and far below the ~645-UMA (~75,700 MXN) AML reporting trigger — so no practical ISR. BUT Art. 30 CFF mandates 5-year retention of dated, MXN-valued buy/sell records; if records are missing, SAT defaults cost basis to ZERO and taxes full proceeds. Platforms increasingly auto-report to SAT (CARF / 2026 Miscellaneous Resolution), so own records should reconcile against the exchange statement. This motivates a per-fill tax-lot ledger from trade #1 (reuse the existing HMAC-audited journal).
themes.contradictions:
Two numeric contradictions appeared across agents; BOTH were resolved live against the authoritative endpoint during this synthesis (https://api.bitso.com/v3/available_books/, 2026-06-16):

1) FEE RATE. Agents split between maker 0.50% / taker 0.65% (4 agents, citing the Bitso list-fees API DOC SAMPLE) and maker 0.60% / taker 0.78% (4 agents, citing bitso.com/fees and the live available_books flat_rate). RESOLVED: the LIVE flat_rate is maker 0.006 (0.60%) / taker 0.0078 (0.78%). The 0.50%/0.65% figures are STALE documentation-example values — do NOT use them. Correct round-trip: ~1.56% taker, ~1.20% maker. (At 11 USD the absolute peso difference is trivial, but the cost MODEL fed to the backtest gate must use 0.60/0.78, not the lower stale numbers, or it overstates edge.)

2) MINIMUM_VALUE. Agents split between 5 MXN (citing the list-available-books DOC SAMPLE, which shows btc_mxn minimum_value '5' / minimum_amount '0.000075') and 10 MXN (citing live available_books). RESOLVED: the LIVE minimum_value is 10 MXN on all *_mxn books, with minimum_amount 0.0000006 BTC (NOT 0.000075). The '5 MXN / 0.000075 BTC' values are stale doc-sample numbers. This does not change the conclusion (200 MXN is ~20x the live floor and clears easily), and it strengthens the mandate to NEVER hardcode minimums — the executor must read them live at startup.

3) Minor framing difference (not a true contradiction): one agent suggested USD-quoted books are ~half the fee (0.25–0.30%) and worth preferring; others correctly noted that for a SINGLE first trade, routing MXN→USDT→asset pays the book fee TWICE and the USD-book advantage only amortizes across MANY trades against a held USDT balance. Consensus for trade #1: trade a DIRECT *_mxn book (btc_mxn or eth_mxn), single leg, no USDT hop.

4) Pair recommendation nuance (complementary, not conflicting): some agents recommend BTC/MXN (top-cap, lowest market-total-loss risk, most liquid); one recommends USDT/MXN for the very first smoke-test (lowest precision/dust-drift risk because price is quasi-stable, so a residual can't silently fall below the 10 MXN floor). Both are valid: USDT/MXN for the plumbing smoke-test, BTC/MXN (or ETH/MXN) for the actual strategy trade.
blockers: NO HARD BLOCKER prevents a Mexico-based individual from making the ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso. KYC is the standard Mexican-resident flow (CURP/INE); ~200 MXN clears all live minimums (10 MXN floor) with ~20x headroom; the trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted key model is natively supported.

ECONOMIC (soft) BLOCKER, not regulatory: fee drag of ~1.2–1.6% round-trip (live maker 0.60% / taker 0.78% per side) sets a per-trade hurdle the mechanical strategy MUST clear to be net-positive. A high-frequency strategy at this size is eaten by costs. This blocks PROFITABILITY, not the trade itself — frame trade #1 as a validation rehearsal.

OWNER-ACTION GATE (avoidable trap): do NOT use Bitso's consumer 'Convert'/'instant buy' widget — it bakes in an opaque ~1.5% spread (one agent could not retrieve the exact figure; treat as higher and opaque). Execute only through the Bitso Alpha / API order book. This is a process discipline item, not a platform limitation.

DUST TAIL (avoidable, code-guardable): Bitso has NO public dust-convert/sweep feature (unlike Binance BNB-convert). A residual crypto balance worth <10 MXN after fees/partial-fills/adverse-price would be permanently un-sellable. Mitigate with an all-or-nothing / 'never leave <10 MXN residual' executor invariant. Not a blocker for a single full-exit order.
