TAX/LEGAL UNCERTAINTY (does not block 200 MXN, must confirm before SCALING): (a) the exact 2026 3x-UMA exemption peso figure must be confirmed from INEGI/DOF; (b) it is legally UNSETTLED (PRODECON characterization) whether crypto even qualifies for the Art. 93 'bienes muebles' exemption — it excludes titulos valor/inversiones. Do NOT hard-code an 'exempt below X' rule. Confirm with a Mexican contador / primary SAT source (LISR Art. 124, CFF Art. 30) before the FIRST SELL.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: register on Bitso (CURP/INE Mexican-resident KYC) and generate a TRADE-ONLY API key — check ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account info' if the executor reads limits); leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' UNCHECKED; add the builder's static/egress IP to the allowlist (up to 5 IPs). Store key/secret outside the repo.
recommendations.why: This is the single most important security control — it contains blast radius exactly as the policy engine intends; a leaked key can only place trades inside the policy caps, never move funds out. Natively supported by Bitso's granular, independent permission scopes.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: on startup the executor MUST fetch GET /v3/available_books and load per-book minimum_value, minimum_amount, minimum_price, and tick_size into the policy engine; reject any intent below minimum_value as a per-tx notional FLOOR (extend the existing per-tx notional cap to enforce a floor too). NEVER hardcode minimums.
recommendations.why: The two cross-agent numeric contradictions (5 vs 10 MXN min; 0.50/0.65 vs 0.60/0.78 fee) prove static doc values go stale. Reading live is the only correct source of truth (live: minimum_value=10 MXN, taker=0.78%). Prevents a rejected first order.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: build a PaperExecutor pointing at the FREE Bitso sandbox (https://api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/) with testnet funding, as a MANDATORY tier between SimExecutor and live. Validate the full auth-sign -> place -> fill-parse -> reconcile -> HMAC-audit loop at $0 before any real pesos. Adopt the 3-tier ladder: SimExecutor -> PaperExecutor(sandbox) -> live Executor.
recommendations.why: Sandbox exercises the REAL API contract for free, so the 200 MXN is spent only to validate what sandbox CANNOT (real fills/slippage, real fee debits, real production-key permission enforcement, real KYC state). Turns 'when to go live' into a concrete gate.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase + Owner: wire the LIVE per-book taker/maker fee (maker 0.60% / taker 0.78%, read from /available_books flat_rate) into the Phase-2 cost-aware Deflated-Sharpe / purged-CV gate and into the solvency/edge check, so the policy engine rejects intents whose expected edge < round-trip cost (~1.2% maker / ~1.56% taker + spread). Use the LIVE numbers, not the stale 0.50/0.65 doc sample.
recommendations.why: Fee drag — not order minimums or slippage — is the dominant, decisive cost at 11 USD. If the backtest uses the stale lower fees it overstates edge and would green-light a net-losing strategy.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner + Codebase: execute the smallest-safe validation ladder on first live day, each step gated by the policy engine — (1) read-only calls with the trade-only key (GET /available_books, /balance, /account_status) asserting the key has NO withdrawal permission; (2) ONE tiny post-only LIMIT test order near the 10 MXN floor (e.g. ~10-20 MXN) to confirm sign/place/fill/fee-accounting end-to-end; (3) then the real single ~195-200 MXN buy. Use a marketable/post-only LIMIT (capped worst price), NEVER a naked market order.
recommendations.why: This is the requested read-only -> tiny test order -> real order sequence. The 10 MXN floor lets the test order be genuinely tiny while exercising the full path; a capped limit order bounds worst-case fill and pays maker (0.60%) not taker (0.78%).

recommendations.recommendation: Owner + Codebase: for trade #1 do ONE single post-only maker buy of ONE top-cap allowlisted asset (btc_mxn or eth_mxn) directly in its MXN book, then HOLD. Do NOT split across assets, do NOT route MXN->USDT->asset (pays the fee twice), do NOT wire a rebalancer. Pin the allowlist to BTC/ETH only (no microcaps).
recommendations.why: Multi-asset allocation/rebalancing is dominated by fee+spread at this size (industry rule: rebalances under ~$50-100 aren't worth it; an $11 portfolio can't reach one increment). Direct single-leg MXN trade minimizes cost; top-cap-only keeps market-driven total-loss off the table.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: enforce an all-or-nothing / 'never leave a residual whose post-trade MXN value < minimum_value (10 MXN)' invariant in the executor on the SELL side, with a unit test for the 'residual below 10 MXN' case. Treat it as a policy-engine invariant, not just an executor nicety.
recommendations.why: Bitso has no public dust-convert sweep; an un-guarded fractional exit could permanently strand <10 MXN of value. The single full-exit order avoids it at 200 MXN, but the agent must be structurally prevented from creating sub-floor residuals.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: keep custody ON Bitso for the 11 USD pilot — do NOT self-custody, do NOT buy a hardware wallet, do NOT withdraw on-chain (network fee would be a large fraction of the balance; e.g. ~0.0001 BTC). Consciously accept Bitso counterparty risk at this trivial size; don't leave large idle balances later. Cash out (when larger) via SPEI to MXN, not on-chain.
recommendations.why: At 11 USD the rational tail-risk choice is to concentrate residual risk in the regulated exchange rather than incur on-chain cost + key-loss risk; the no-withdrawal key already aligns with this. On-chain exit would strand value.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: have the executor journal an immutable per-fill tax-lot record from trade #1 — UTC+local timestamp, pair, side, qty, fill price, MXN FMV at fill, fee, and Bitso trade_id — with FIFO lot-matching, INPC-adjustable acquisition cost (Art. 124 LISR), and a year-end realized-gain CSV export to reconcile against the exchange's CARF auto-report. Reuse the existing HMAC-audited decision journal. Do NOT hard-code any 'exempt below X' tax rule.
recommendations.why: Art. 30 CFF mandates 5-year record retention; missing records => SAT defaults cost basis to ZERO and taxes full proceeds. Records are load-bearing even at 11 USD, and platforms now auto-report so own records must reconcile.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: treat the Mexican tax position as UNCONFIRMED beyond trivial size — before the FIRST SELL (not the buy), confirm with a Mexican contador / primary SAT source: (a) the exact 2026 3x-UMA exemption peso figure from INEGI/DOF, and (b) whether crypto legally qualifies for the Art. 93 'bienes muebles' exemption at all (PRODECON characterization is unsettled; it excludes titulos valor/inversiones). The 200 MXN buy itself needs no tax confirmation (buying is not a taxable event).
recommendations.why: All specific tax figures in the research are from secondary blogs (low confidence) and the legal characterization is genuinely debated. Buying/holding is non-taxable, so this does not block trade #1 — but it must be settled by a primary source before realizing any gain.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: add a pre-trade cost/slippage guard that reads the PUBLIC order_book endpoint (no auth, 60 req/min/IP), simulates the fill for the exact ~200 MXN notional, computes (taker_fee + spread_cross) — NOT a depth-impact curve, since the order never walks the book at this size — and aborts if estimated effective cost exceeds a configured cap (e.g. >1.5%).
recommendations.why: Gives a deterministic, real-data pre-trade abort that protects against an unexpectedly wide spread (e.g. off-peak thinness on a local MXN book) without needing auth, and correctly models small-size cost as fee+spread rather than fictitious depth slippage.

themes.consensus:
Overwhelming, source-backed agreement across all 10 agents on the executor design for the first ~200 MXN (~11 USD) live trade:

PLATFORM: Bitso is the correct first target — Mexico-born, CNBV/Fintech-Law-regulated, SPEI peso deposits, native MXN order books (btc_mxn), and a documented REST trading API. Binance (available in MX, SAT "Vulnerable Activity"-registered entity) is the stdlib-compatible fallback. Coinbase Advanced is ruled OUT for the stdlib-only constraint because it mandates Ed25519/ECDSA JWT auth (no asymmetric signing in Python stdlib).

BUILD NATIVE, NOT CCXT: Bitso (and Binance) auth is plain HMAC-SHA256, fully implementable with stdlib hmac+hashlib+base64+json+urllib. CCXT 4.5.58 (2026-06-11) pulls in requests+cryptography (+async aiohttp/aiodns/yarl) and compiled wheels (a Windows-11 build-tool risk) — a large, harder-to-audit blast radius for one signed POST per trade. This directly contradicts the project's stdlib-only / auditable ethos. Verdict: native REST executor.

SECURITY STANCE IS NATIVELY SUPPORTED: Bitso API keys have independent permission toggles — 'Place orders' is separate from 'Make withdrawals' — plus a 5-IP allowlist and up to 6 keys per account. So the mandated trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted key is a real, supported configuration. Create the key with ONLY 'Place orders' (+ 'View balances' for the solvency check), withdrawals OFF, IP pinned. Auth errors prove the blast radius at the exchange: 0202 = trade-only key blocking a withdrawal call, 0213/0211 = off-allowlist IP rejection.

CODEBASE FIT: The existing Executor Protocol (executor.py:20-21, execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill) and frozen Intent/Fill dataclasses (types.py) — both verified in-repo — accept a drop-in BitsoExecutor(base_url, key, secret). The same class serves as PaperExecutor (base_url=https://stage.bitso.com — a REAL stage/sandbox with production-identical endpoints) and live Executor (base_url=https://bitso.com), giving true parity, not a mock.

ORDER STYLE: Default to a POST-ONLY LIMIT order (type=limit + time_in_force=postonly) to lock the maker fee and get price/slippage control at tiny size; the cost is it can fail to fill (Bitso cancels if it would cross), so the executor needs a RESTING/REJECTED-would-cross tri-state with bounded re-quotes. For a forced taker path use a marketable limit + immediateorcancel with a capped price (NOT a raw market order) so the policy notional cap bounds the worst fill. (One agent preferred IOC/market as the simplest fire-and-forget for the very first trade — see contradictions.)

FILL CONFIRMATION: POST /orders returns ONLY {success, payload.oid} — no price/fee — so execute() must POST then poll order/trades by oid to build the existing Fill, aggregating multi-trade executions into one VWAP price + summed fee. Own-order status is REST-poll only (Bitso websocket is public market-data only; there is NO private user-data fill stream).

IDEMPOTENCY / CRASH-SAFETY: Use the client-supplied origin_id (<=40 chars, [a-zA-Z0-9_-]) derived deterministically from the journaled intent id, persisted BEFORE the HTTP call. CRITICAL caveat: origin_id uniqueness is scoped to ACTIVE orders only, so it does NOT prevent a double-buy after an order fills and vanishes. Mandatory pattern: a 3-state outcome (CONFIRMED / REJECTED / UNKNOWN); every UNKNOWN reconciles via GET /orders?origin_ids AND trade/ledger history (GET /orders only shows open/partial; terminal states are visible ~1h) BEFORE any bounded, error-code-classified retry — never blind-retry, never rely on origin_id alone, and halt to an UNRESOLVED manual-review state if inconclusive.

NONCE/SIGNING: HMAC-SHA256 over nonce+method+path+body, header 'Authorization: Bitso key:nonce:signature'. Nonce v2 (13-digit epoch-ms + random salt) is now mandatory (v1 deprecated Nov 2025). Persist the last nonce in journaled state and use max(now_ms, last+1) behind a lock so an NTP step-back or two processes never regress it. Sign the EXACT byte string you send (single JSON serialization) — re-serializing after signing is the classic 401. Bitso has NO documented clock-skew window (unlike Binance, which enforces recvWindow / -1021 and added a 2026 percent-encode-before-signing rule / -1022).

ERROR TAXONOMY: Dispatch on HTTP status (401 auth / 400 validation / 420 throttle — note 420, NOT 429) then on string error.code. 0379 = INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS (non-retryable); 0377/0378 = 'submitted but not final' -> poll, never resubmit; rate-limit is 60 rpm public / 300 rpm private (cancels exempt), 1-min lockout escalating to a 24h block, no Retry-After -> mandatory >=60s exponential backoff, never tight-retry. Unknown 400 -> halt+log; unknown 5xx/timeout -> bounded retry + reconcile.

MIN-NOTIONAL / SIZING: 200 MXN clears the floor (btc_mxn ~25 MXN minimum, ~$10 general floor) but this is NOT a constant — fetch minimum_amount/minimum_value live from GET /available_books and add a min-notional policy check. A fixed-200-MXN market BUY maps trivially to Bitso minor=200 (quote amount); on Kraken volume is base-only and needs price-division.

FEES: Real drag at this size. Entry-tier Bitso MXN spot is maker 0.600% / taker 0.780% (one agent cites 0.50%/0.65% from docs examples). On 200 MXN, taker ~1.3-1.56 MXN/side, round-trip ~2.6-3 MXN (~1.3-1.5%). Post-only maker saves ~0.18 pp (~0.36 MXN). Pull the real taker rate from GET /api/v3/fees at startup — do NOT hardcode — and feed it into the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate.
themes.contradictions: 1. POST-ONLY-by-default vs IOC/market-by-default for the FIRST trade (resolved: marketable-limit+IOC for the inaugural trade, post-only-default after the resting state machine exists). 2. Exact entry-tier fee numbers (0.60/0.78 vs 0.50/0.65 vs 0.36-0.60) — all say pull live from GET /api/v3/fees. 3. Whether the 'postonly' time_in_force literal is verified — majority say yes (docs.bitso.com/place-an-order), one earlier agent unverified; confirm once live. 4. Min-notional figure (25 MXN vs ~5 MXN vs ~$10) — all agree read live from GET /available_books.
blockers: NO HARD BLOCKER prevents a Mexico-based individual from making the trade — every required primitive (self-registration + KYC, trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted key, stdlib HMAC auth, 200 MXN > minimum, SPEI peso funding) is real and currently supported on Bitso. The items below are design gaps and unverified facts that must be closed BEFORE go-live, not legal/access blockers.

CODEBASE DESIGN GAP (must fix before any live order): Intent has NO order-type, price, or post_only field, and NO quote_amount — verified in types.py (only symbol/side/base_amount/reason). It cannot express a post-only maker order or a 'spend exactly 200 MXN' (minor=200) buy. SimExecutor models a single immediate taker fill. The policy engine (policy.py, verified) sizes notional from base_amount*price and has no min-notional rule. Live orders REQUIRE choosing market vs limit. Must extend Intent with order_type (MARKET|LIMIT), limit_price, post_only, and an optional quote_amount, keeping MARKET default so the 55 existing tests + SimExecutor stay valid.

FILL-MODEL GAP: live fills are partial/multi-trade and a post-only order may NOT fill at all, but Fill (verified types.py) is single-price/single-fee and SimExecutor always returns exactly one Fill. Executor must VWAP-aggregate executions into one Fill and add an explicit 'resting/unfilled' outcome so only real executions mutate the wallet (preserving the 'Fill is the only thing allowed to mutate the wallet' invariant).

IDEMPOTENCY HAZARD (not a blocker but mandatory-to-handle): origin_id uniqueness is ACTIVE-orders-only, so it cannot prevent a double-buy after a fast fill vanishes. Requires the reconcile-before-retry 3-state machine (CONFIRMED/REJECTED/UNKNOWN) cross-checking trade/ledger history, or blind retries can double-spend the treasury.

TAX/LEGAL — UNVERIFIED, primary-source confirmation required: SAT treats crypto as a virtual asset and disposals (sell/trade/spend) can trigger taxable gains, but NO agent verified the exact 2026 SAT treatment from a primary source — all flagged it explicitly out-of-scope. This is NOT settled enough to assert numbers. The first 200 MXN trade is informational; confirm SAT/Fintech-Law treatment with SAT or a Mexican tax professional before scaling.

UNVERIFIED-BEFORE-GO-LIVE facts (close each against the live API/docs, do not trust aggregators): (a) exact live btc_mxn minimum_value via GET /available_books; (b) actual account taker/maker fee via GET /api/v3/fees; (c) verbatim time_in_force='postonly' literal; (d) Bitso stage/sandbox account requires emailing Developer Support to clear SMS verification — an onboarding friction step.

BINANCE-as-fallback caveat: a 2026-01-15 Binance change requires PERCENT-ENCODING the payload BEFORE HMAC (else -1022), and Binance enforces a clock-skew recvWindow (-1021) needing OS NTP sync — different signing rules than Bitso, so the fallback path is not a free swap.

recommendations.recommendation: Build a single stdlib-only BitsoExecutor(base_url, key, secret) implementing the existing Executor Protocol (execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill). One _sign(secret, nonce, method, path, body_str) helper using hmac+hashlib only; serialize the JSON body ONCE and sign the exact bytes sent. Same class = PaperExecutor (base_url=https://stage.bitso.com) and live (base_url=https://bitso.com). Do NOT add CCXT.
recommendations.why: Keeps the stdlib-only/local-first/auditable invariant; Bitso auth is plain HMAC-SHA256; the Protocol and stage/prod URL parity make it a clean drop-in. CCXT's requests+cryptography+async tree and Windows compiled-wheel risk is a large, harder-to-audit blast radius for one signed POST.

recommendations.recommendation: Create the Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' (+ 'View balances') enabled; leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' OFF; add the executor host's fixed egress IP to the 5-IP allowlist. Then deliberately trigger 0202 (attempt a withdrawal call) and 0213 (call from an off-allowlist IP) once during read-only validation to PROVE the blast radius holds.
recommendations.why: This is the exact trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-pinned posture the policy engine assumes; Bitso's permissions are independent toggles, so a leaked key cannot move funds off-platform. Actively triggering 0202/0213 verifies the containment rather than assuming it.

recommendations.recommendation: Extend Intent with order_type: OrderType (MARKET|LIMIT, default MARKET), limit_price: float|None, post_only: bool=False, and an optional quote_amount: float|None (maps to Bitso minor for a 'spend exactly 200 MXN' buy). Add a min-notional policy check that reads minimum_amount/minimum_value from GET /available_books (mirroring the existing per-tx notional cap and staleness rule).
recommendations.why: Verified gap: Intent (types.py) has no order-type/price/post_only/quote_amount, and policy.py has no min-notional rule, so it cannot express a maker order or a fixed-MXN buy that live exchanges require. MARKET default keeps the 55 tests + SimExecutor valid.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement a 3-state place() outcome (CONFIRMED/REJECTED/UNKNOWN) with reconcile-before-retry: journal a deterministic origin_id BEFORE the POST; on any UNKNOWN (timeout/5xx/drop) reconcile via GET /orders?origin_ids AND trade/ledger history before any bounded retry; halt to an UNRESOLVED manual-review state if inconclusive. Never blind-retry; never rely on origin_id alone (uniqueness is active-orders-only).
recommendations.why: A fast 200 MXN buy fills near-instantly; origin_id then becomes reusable, so a timeout+retry can double-buy. GET /orders only shows open/partial (terminal ~1h), so 'not found' does not prove non-execution — must cross-check trade history. This is the single most important correctness/safety control.

recommendations.recommendation: Make execute() POST /api/v3/orders then POLL order/trades by oid to build the Fill: aggregate multi-trade executions into one VWAP price + summed fee, and add an explicit 'resting/unfilled' outcome distinct from Fill so only real executions mutate the wallet. Use REST polling (every 2-5s, capped) for own-order status — Bitso has NO private user-data websocket.
recommendations.why: POST returns only {success, payload.oid} (no price/fee); live fills are partial/multi-trade and post-only may never fill, while Fill (verified types.py) is single-price and SimExecutor always returns one Fill. Preserves the 'Fill is the only thing that mutates the wallet' invariant.

recommendations.recommendation: Add executor.probe()/healthcheck(): one signed READ-ONLY call (GET /balance + GET /fees) asserting HTTP 200 and no 0201/auth errors, required as a precondition before execute() may POST. Go-live sequence: (1) full stage rung with testnet funds, (2) production read-only probe, (3) one real ~200 MXN order via marketable-limit+IOC with a capped slippage price.
recommendations.why: Nonce/signature/permission/IP bugs manifest identically on read and write but a read call has zero blast radius; this isolates auth from execution and de-risks the first peso of real money. Stage is production-identical, so passing stage gives high confidence for prod.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement Nonce v2 (13-digit epoch-ms + random salt) and persist the last-issued nonce in the journaled state; mint each nonce as max(now_ms, last+1) behind a process lock so an NTP step-back or two processes sharing a key never regress it. Document single-process-per-key. Add a signer unit test against a hardcoded (key, nonce, path, body) -> expected-hex vector before the first live call.
recommendations.why: Bitso has no documented clock-skew window; the real rejection (0201) is a non-increasing nonce. v1 was deprecated Nov 2025. Persisting in the existing crash-safe journal makes monotonicity survive crashes/NTP steps; the known-vector test catches the byte-identical-body gotcha before risking funds.

recommendations.recommendation: Build the Bitso-specific error taxonomy: dispatch on HTTP status (401/400/420) then string error.code -> {AUTH_FATAL: 0201/0202/0211/0213/0215/0216 halt+alert; NONCE: 0206/0207 single retry w/ fresh nonce; INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS: 0379 non-retryable; PENDING: 0377/0378 poll never resubmit; RATE_LIMIT: HTTP 420 + 0801/0802 >=60s exponential backoff+jitter, capped, NEVER tight-retry (24h-ban escalation); unknown-400 halt+log; unknown-5xx/timeout bounded-retry+reconcile}. Parse decimal strings to Decimal, never float.
recommendations.why: Rate-limit is HTTP 420 not 429 (generic 429-only handlers miss it) and tight-retrying risks a 24h account freeze. 0377/0378 resubmits double-fill. Branching on error.code (not message text) survives Bitso's frequent code additions.

recommendations.recommendation: Pull the actual taker/maker fee from GET /api/v3/fees at startup (do NOT hardcode 0.65%/0.78%) and feed it into the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate; reuse SimExecutor's fee_rate field populated from this so sim/paper/live agree. For the FIRST trade default to a marketable-limit + immediateorcancel with a capped price; adopt post-only LIMIT default only after the resting/re-quote state machine is built.
recommendations.why: At 200 MXN, round-trip taker drag is ~2.6-3 MXN (~1.3-1.5%) — real cost that must match across sim/paper/live. IOC+capped-price gives a bounded worst fill (the policy notional cap stays meaningful) without the open-order lifecycle risk a resting post-only order adds on the inaugural trade.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep the Executor interface venue-agnostic so a BinanceExecutor is a thin fallback (key in X-MBX-APIKEY, sign query+body with timestamp+recvWindow, percent-encode payload BEFORE HMAC per the 2026-01-15 change, keep recvWindow at 5000ms + OS NTP sync). Do NOT pick Coinbase Advanced under the stdlib-only constraint (Ed25519/ECDSA JWT is not in stdlib).
recommendations.why: Binance is MX-available and stdlib-HMAC-compatible as a fallback if Bitso onboarding stalls, but its -1021 clock-skew and -1022 percent-encode rules differ from Bitso. Coinbase would force a third-party 'cryptography' dependency, breaking the project's no-dependency posture.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: register on Bitso and complete full KYC (also unlocks the 300 RPM private tier and SPEI peso deposits). For stage testing, email Bitso Developer Support to clear SMS verification on the test account. Confirm SAT crypto-disposal tax treatment with SAT or a Mexican tax professional BEFORE scaling — treat the first 200 MXN trade as informational, not a tax strategy.
recommendations.why: KYC is required for the private rate tier and peso funding. Stage SMS does not work without Developer Support. SAT treats crypto as a virtual asset and disposals can be taxable, but no agent verified the exact 2026 rule from a primary source — it is explicitly unsettled and must be confirmed before scaling.

recommendations.recommendation: At this tiny size, keep custody on-exchange (Bitso) for the first trade — do NOT grant withdrawal or self-custody yet. Move funds off-platform manually (outside the agent) only if/when desired, never via the trade-only key. Defer Bitso modify/replace order endpoints to a Phase-4 optimization.
recommendations.why: Self-custody/withdrawal capability is the exact risk the security stance avoids at $11; leaving withdrawals OFF caps blast radius. Modify/replace adds complexity with no benefit for a single inaugural trade.

themes.consensus: Strong agreement across all 10 agents: a ~200 MXN (~11 USD) first live crypto trade by a Mexico-based individual is NOT blocked by fees, spread, minimums, or tax. Both Bitso (Mexico-domiciled, SPEI/USDC on-ramp) and Kraken Pro clear all minimums (Kraken's binding constraint is just a 0.50 USD cost minimum; Bitso BTC/MXN minimum is 10 MXN per the live API, ~20x below the trade size). The economics, not feasibility, are the real constraint: at low 30-day volume the realistic per-side taker fee is Bitso USDC book ~0.36% < Kraken Pro 0.40% < Bitso MXN book 0.78% (the widely-quoted Bitso '0.15% maker' figure is NOT the low-volume reality). Maker/post-only limit orders cut Kraken to 0.25%/side. Spread is a minor cost at this size and venue: live Bitso BTC/MXN spread was measured at ~0.074% (half-spread ~0.04%), so the explicit fee dominates; thinner alt/MXN books (ETH/MXN ~0.26%) are worse, so route on the deepest book (BTC/MXN or USD/MXN). Round-trip taker drag is roughly 0.7%-1.6% depending on venue/book, which the cost-aware backtest gate (Deflated Sharpe + purged CV) must net out as a hard edge bar (recommend requiring expected edge > 2x round-trip cost). On instant-buy: ALL agents agree the executor must use the Pro/Alpha order-book API and NEVER the instant-buy/convert path (Kraken App instant 1.0%-1.5%; Bitso app embedded undisclosed spread) — instant modes are both costlier and not controllable via the trade-only, IP-allowlisted key the policy engine needs. On tax: unanimous that ISR is effectively zero at this size — any realized gain is orders of magnitude below the annual enajenacion-de-bienes exemption (~3x UMA, cited ~128,383 MXN for 2026), and there is no IVA on the trade principal (only on the exchange fee, a fraction of a peso at 11 USD). The real, immediate obligation is RECORD-KEEPING, not payment: every disposal (including crypto-to-crypto and crypto-to-stablecoin swaps, not just MXN cashouts) is a taxable event that must be logged in MXN; records must be kept (>=5 years) to prove the exemption; the annual declaracion (due ~April 30) may be required even when the gain is exempt; and from 2026 exchanges report user activity to SAT under OECD CARF (plus Regla 2.9.21 RMF), so the owner's own ledger must reconcile with what the exchange reports. Concrete codebase consensus: build a crash-safe, per-fill, per-lot FIFO tax ledger inside the executor now (UTC timestamp, pair, side, qty, fill price in native quote, fee + fee currency, MXN-reference value at fill, exchange order/trade ID, computed realized gain/loss per disposal), wired into the existing HMAC-audited journal.
blockers: NONE are hard blockers for the ~200 MXN first trade. The following are conditions/drags, not blockers:

Economic drag (not a feasibility blocker): round-trip taker fee floor of ~0.7%-1.6% (Bitso MXN book ~1.56%, Kraken ~0.80%, Bitso USDC ~0.72%) plus live spread must be cleared by strategy edge in the cost-aware backtest gate; a mechanical strategy that cannot beat ~1.6% round-trip is uneconomic at this venue/size.

Executor-design constraint (must be respected, else cost balloons): the instant-buy/convert path is forbidden — Kraken App instant-buy is 1.0%-1.5% and Bitso app buy/sell embeds an undisclosed spread, and neither is controllable via a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted API key. The executor MUST use Kraken Pro / Bitso Alpha order-book API endpoints only.

Procedural/compliance obligation (cheap to satisfy, but real): record-keeping from trade #1 and likely an annual declaracion even with zero tax due. Flat CFF fines for an unfiled return (~$2,050-$25,360 MXN) or missing accounting records (up to ~$122,440 MXN) can dwarf the trade — neutralized for ~$0 by filing + keeping records.

Unverified tax specifics that must be confirmed with a contador/SAT BEFORE relying on net P&L or scaling: (a) the exact 2026 exemption figure and UMA-vs-salario basis; (b) whether any 20%-of-gross provisional ISR is withheld at source on small spot sales; (c) whether an autonomous high-frequency bot reclassifies the owner into 'persona fisica con actividad empresarial' (monthly declarations + electronic accounting + possible 16% IVA exposure).

CARF/Regla 2.9.21 visibility (not a blocker, a posture change): from 2026 the exchange reports the owner's gross operations to SAT, so 'too small to be seen' is not a valid long-term posture; the owner's ledger must be reconcilable with exchange exports.

recommendations.recommendation: Build the live/paper executor to place orders ONLY via the Pro/Alpha order-book API endpoints (Kraken Pro AddOrder / Bitso Alpha place-order), and explicitly forbid the instant-buy/convert path as a hard-coded forbidden route in the executor.
recommendations.why: Instant-buy is materially worse (Kraken App 1.0%-1.5%; Bitso app embedded undisclosed spread, can exceed the fee) AND is not controllable via the trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key the policy engine requires to contain blast radius. Order-book taker fees are 0.36%-0.78% vs 1.0%-1.5% instant.

recommendations.recommendation: Use a TRADE-ONLY, NO-WITHDRAWAL, IP-allowlisted API key for the live executor; validate the read-only path (GET ticker/balances/user_trades/fees) end-to-end FIRST, then place one ~15 MXN BTC/MXN taker order to confirm a live fill, then scale to the full ~200 MXN.
recommendations.why: The security stance: the policy engine contains blast radius only if the key cannot withdraw; full-permission keys are the exact risk to avoid. Bitso BTC/MXN minimum is 10 MXN (live API) and Kraken's cost minimum is 0.50 USD, so a sub-20 MXN smoke-test order is feasible within the budget and proves the executor before committing capital.

recommendations.recommendation: Add a crash-safe, per-fill, per-lot FIFO tax ledger to the executor/journal NOW: each BUY creates a FIFO lot (UTC timestamp, acquisition month, pair, qty, MXN cost, fee+fee currency); each SELL/SWAP consumes oldest lots first and emits a realized MXN gain/loss record; store exchange order/trade ID for reconciliation. Treat every crypto-to-crypto and crypto-to-stablecoin swap as a disposal, not only MXN cashouts.
recommendations.why: Unanimous across agents: the real obligation at pilot size is record-keeping, not payment. Every disposal is a taxable event valued in MXN; records must be kept >=5 years to prove the exemption; FIFO/PEPS is the audit-safe accepted method. From 2026 CARF makes the owner's ledger reconcilable against SAT-reported exchange data. Building it now turns a future SAT obligation into a solved logging problem and serves both 'enajenacion de bienes' and 'actividad empresarial' regimes.

recommendations.recommendation: Set the cost-aware backtest gate's per-side cost to the REAL low-volume taker rate of the chosen venue/book (0.78% Bitso MXN, 0.40% Kraken Pro, 0.36% Bitso USDC) PLUS a live half-spread estimate, and require strategy expected edge > 2x the round-trip cost before going live. Do NOT use the stale 0.15%/0.36% figures.
recommendations.why: The official bitso.com/fees page and live /v3/available_books confirm 0.78% taker on the MXN book at entry volume; third-party 0.15-0.60% claims are stale or for higher tiers/the USDC book. Round-trip drag is 0.7%-1.6%; a strategy that cannot clear this is uneconomic at 11 USD. The gate must net out the realistic cost or it will green-light a losing live strategy.

recommendations.recommendation: Choose the venue on TOTAL all-in cost (fee + live spread + MXN ramp), not fee alone. For a Mexico-based first trade, route on the deepest book — Bitso BTC/MXN or USD/MXN via SPEI — and confirm the live fee tier on the actual account dashboard before committing. Prefer the Bitso USDC book (0.36% taker) or Kraken maker/post-only (0.25%) if the strategy tolerates limit orders.
recommendations.why: Bitso is Mexico-domiciled with SPEI deposits and the deepest MXN book (BTC/MXN spread ~0.074% live; ETH/MXN ~0.26% is worse). Fee ranking (Bitso USDC 0.36% < Kraken 0.40% < Bitso MXN 0.78%) flips with maker orders (Kraken 0.25% best). Spread/liquidity/ramp are separate from fees and fees can change, so confirm on the live dashboard.

recommendations.recommendation: Add a pre-trade spread guard and a dynamic minimum-order check to the policy engine: fetch /v3/ticker (or Kraken ticker) before each order, reject if (ask-bid)/mid exceeds a configurable threshold (e.g. 0.5%), and read minimum_value/cost-minimum dynamically from /v3/available_books (Kraken AssetPairs) rather than hardcoding. Log effective_cost = taker_fee + 0.5*(ask-bid)/mid to the HMAC audit trail.
recommendations.why: Spread widens during volatility and on thin books; a live guard prevents submitting into a blown-out spread. Reading minimums dynamically prevents sub-minimum rejected orders (Bitso 10 MXN, Kraken 0.50 USD) and survives exchange parameter changes. Logging the realized cost makes the backtest cost assumption enforceable and auditable.

recommendations.recommendation: Model IVA at 16% on the FEE component ONLY (fee_iva = fee * 0.16, configurable, 0.08 for border zone) in the executor cost model; do NOT apply IVA to the asset principal. After the first real trade, read the actual fee receipt/CFDI to confirm whether IVA is already included in the quoted fee or added on top, then calibrate.
recommendations.why: No IVA is charged in practice on the crypto principal; only the exchange service fee may carry 16% IVA, which at 11 USD is a fraction of a peso (e.g. ~0.19 MXN on a 1.20 MXN fee). The legal basis for the principal exemption is contested (crypto = activo virtual, not divisa), so this is current-practice tolerance — model the fee IVA but do not over-engineer the principal.

recommendations.recommendation: Get a one-time written opinion from a Mexican Contador Publico (or confirm with SAT directly) BEFORE scaling trade frequency/volume, covering: (a) exact 2026 exemption figure + UMA-vs-salario basis; (b) whether any 20%-of-gross provisional ISR is withheld on small spot sales; (c) whether the autonomous bot's frequency/speculative intent reclassifies into 'persona fisica con actividad empresarial' (monthly declarations + electronic accounting + possible 16% IVA on foreign-sourced crypto). Confirm CARF/Regla 2.9.21 specifics against primary SAT sources.
recommendations.why: The most load-bearing tax facts (exemption figure, withholding, hobby-vs-business line, CARF timing) come from secondary blog sources that CONFLICT and were not verified against SAT/LISR primary text. None block the first trade (gains << any exemption), but relying on net P&L or scaling the bot without confirmation risks misclassification. A bot maximizes both habitualidad and especulacion, the two legal triggers for actividad empresarial — a genuine judgment call, not a bright-line rule.

recommendations.recommendation: File the annual SAT declaracion (due ~April 30) including the crypto activity even when the gain is exempt/trivial, and archive Bitso's free self-service 'Reporte de transacciones' (and a CSV pulled via GET /api/v3/user_trades + /fees) as the evidence base. Build a read-only report module in the executor that joins exchange fills to the decision journal and emits a tax-grade CSV.
recommendations.why: Flat CFF fines for non-filing (~$2,050-$25,360 MXN) or missing records (up to ~$122,440 MXN) can dwarf the entire trade even though the omitted TAX is ~$0. No exchange issues a CFDI or withholds ISR for crypto — compliance is 100% self-served. The read-only report path doubles as the read-only validation step before the first live order. CARF means the owner's records should reconcile with exchange-reported figures.

recommendations.recommendation: For the first trade, prefer a single MXN -> asset -> MXN round trip (one or two disposals) rather than a multi-leg crypto-to-crypto rotation, to keep the tax footprint trivial and the accounting one-line; add a trade-count-per-month metric to the audit output to surface when activity approaches the 'frequent' threshold.
recommendations.why: Every swap (including crypto-to-stablecoin) is a separate taxable disposal, so a multi-leg first trade multiplies the record-keeping for no benefit at pilot size. A monthly trade-count metric gives the owner an early-warning signal for the actividad-empresarial reclassification risk before it materializes.

themes.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
themes.consensus:
All 10 agents converge with high confidence: for a ~200 MXN (~11 USD) first live trade by a Mexico-based individual, KEEP funds in exchange custody on Bitso. Self-custody is wrong at this size for three independent reasons: (1) economically self-defeating — a hardware wallet costs 54-249 USD (5x-22x the position) and flat on-chain/dust fees can strand a tiny balance; (2) structurally incompatible with an autonomous t+1 trading agent, which MUST trade against an exchange-held balance via API (cold storage would require a manual signed transfer per trade); and (3) it inverts the project's core safety thesis — a raw private key cannot be made "no-withdrawal" the way a scoped exchange API key can, so a leaked key = 100% instant loss with no permission gate.

The custody decision directly LICENSES the project's existing security stance at zero functional cost: because funds stay on-exchange, the agent's API key never needs withdrawal scope. Bitso natively supports exactly the required controls (verified against Bitso's official API docs): up to 6 keys per account, 5 independently-toggled permissions (Place orders, View balances, View account information, Perform security actions, Make withdrawals), IP allowlist up to 5 IPs, and a withdrawal-address allowlist of up to 5 addresses per asset. The agent key = enable ONLY "Place orders" + "View balances"; leave "Make withdrawals" AND "Perform security actions" OFF; IP-pin it. The secret is shown ONCE.

Bitso is the correct custody home: CNBV-registered, MXN-native, free SPEI deposit/withdrawal, deepest MX liquidity, MXN fiat rails run through Nvio Pagos Mexico (an IFPE/EPI authorized under Ley Fintech). Security posture (per Bitso's own Trust page + reviews): segregated multi-sig cold storage, monthly zero-knowledge proof-of-reserves an individual can self-verify in-account, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, Coincover (Lloyd's-underwritten) protection, and a clean 10+ year no-major-hack record.

The blast radius is contained on two axes: KEY/agent compromise is bounded to fee/spread churn (a stolen trade-only IP-locked key cannot move funds out and is revoked instantly in the web UI with 2FA); CUSTODIAN/structural risk is bounded by keeping the on-exchange balance tiny (the policy engine's cumulative-spend budget + per-tx cap + solvency rules already enforce this, and double as a MAX_ON_EXCHANGE ceiling and a software "reserve floor"). At 11 USD the absolute loss ceiling is ~11 USD, so residual custodian/freeze/insolvency risk is real but financially negligible for the first trade.
themes.contradictions:
1) "Network fees make self-custody pointless at this size" — PARTIALLY CONTESTED. Most agents assert withdrawal fees are prohibitive; one agent verified directly against Bitso's official fee page that this is FALSE on the cheap rails: USDC-on-Base = 0.01 USD, USDT-on-Polygon = 0.01 USD, USDC-ERC20 = 0.02-0.05 USD (~0.1-0.3% on 11 USD). It is only TRUE if you naively pick Tron (USDT TRC20 = 3.2 USDT, ~29% of the stack) or Ethereum-mainnet gas. RESOLUTION: the fee objection is not the real argument; the real arguments against self-custody are (a) operational/irreversible-loss risk and (b) the no-withdrawal security model — both hold regardless of fees. The conclusion (stay custodial) survives even though the fee premise is partly wrong.

2) Stale source on Base USDC support — one web-search summary claimed Bitso only supports Ethereum/Solana for USDC withdrawal. The directly-fetched primary fee page (more authoritative) shows Base IS supported at 0.01 USD. Flagged as stale/wrong; trust the primary page and verify in the live withdrawal UI before sending.

3) "Bitso is CNBV-regulated" — interpretation contested. Most agents call it CNBV-regulated; one agent flags the wrapper is likely AML/KYC-grade (LFPIORPI 'vulnerable activities' + Ley Fintech for the MXN payment-institution leg via Nvio), NOT a bank-style deposit-insurance or client-asset-segregation guarantee. RESOLUTION: both are compatible — the FIAT (peso) rail is genuinely regulated; the CRYPTO holdings are not insured/segregated by statute. Do not represent exchange custody as 'bank-grade protected' in any project doc.

4) Confidence on Bitso's specific security claims (proof-of-reserves mechanics, Coincover coverage limits, 'never hacked', insurance scope) ranges from high to medium because several figures come from third-party 2026 reviews, not Bitso primary disclosures. Treat exact percentages/insurance limits as unverified pending Bitso's own Trust/security pages.
blockers: NO blocker prevents the ~200 MXN first trade. The items below are scoped, mostly self-inflicted-if-skipped, or scaling-gated.

SELF-INFLICTED RECOVERY BLOCKER (pre-trade, must do): At Bitso 2FA setup you must SAVE the emergency restore code offline BEFORE depositing. Losing BOTH the 2FA device and that code is the only scenario that forces slow, KYC-gated, text-only support recovery (Bitso has NO phone support; all recovery is Help Center ticket + customersupport@bitso.com).

BITSO PLATFORM DEFAULT-UNSAFE behavior (does not block, but must be respected): a key with 'Make withdrawals' ENABLED and an EMPTY whitelist fail-OPENS to ANY address. The platform fallback is permissive, not restrictive. Mitigated entirely by giving the agent a NO-withdrawal key (and no withdrawal code path).

ONE personal account per CURP/email: a Mexico individual CANNOT open a second personal Bitso account to physically separate trading vs reserve funds; duplicate-data detection can block one or both. This BLOCKS the 'two separate exchange accounts' separation pattern — replaced by logical separation (scoped keys + policy-engine reserve floor).

STRUCTURAL Mexico risks (do not block 11 USD, raise cost at scale): (a) NO IPAB/FDIC-equivalent deposit insurance covers crypto balances; (b) UIF can freeze accounts WITHOUT a judicial order/MP, and the Oct-2025 Amparo reform removes the usual injunction (suspension) defense in laundering-linked freezes — SPEI to/from the venue routes through this same banking surveillance; (c) Mexican law mandates NO client-asset segregation/bankruptcy-remoteness for non-bank crypto exchanges, and banks are barred (Banxico Circular 4/2019) from crypto custody. Mitigation costs nothing: fund via SPEI from the owner's OWN KYC-name-matched bank account in small explainable amounts, never route third-party money, keep the key trade-only so a freeze can't be amplified, and keep on-exchange balances tiny.

TAX/LEGAL UNVERIFIED (must confirm before scaling/realizing gains, NOT a blocker for the buy): No agent retrieved a PRIMARY SAT source. Consensus from secondary guides: holding and BUYING are NOT taxable; only enajenacion (sale, crypto-to-crypto swap, or paying with crypto) triggers ISR (progressive up to 35%); wallet-to-own-wallet transfers are generally not a disposal (standard inference, not primary-confirmed). An annual asset-disposal exemption ~3x UMA exists but the exact 2026 figure is UNCERTAIN (one guide ~128,383 MXN, another ~60,000 MXN). At 200 MXN gains fall far below any threshold so likely no ISR is owed, BUT records must be kept from day one. Under OECD CARF (effective April 2026) Bitso reports your balances and gross annual operations to SAT regardless of self-custody. CONFIRM all tax treatment with a Mexican contador or primary sat.gob.mx text before realizing gains.

recommendations.recommendation: First live ~200 MXN trade = funds held in Bitso exchange custody. Do NOT buy a hardware wallet or wire any self-custody/withdrawal path for Phase-3. Open ONE Bitso personal account (CNBV-registered, MXN-native, free SPEI); a second personal account is not allowed per CURP.
recommendations.why: Self-custody is economically irrational (device 54-249 USD = 5x-22x the position), structurally incompatible with an autonomous API trading agent, and inverts the no-withdrawal safety thesis. Exchange-custody is what makes a trade-only key sufficient. The executor should assume balance-lives-at-venue with no on-chain withdrawal path.

recommendations.recommendation: Provision the agent's Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' enabled; leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF; enable IP allowlist and pin it to the agent's egress IP (prefer the stable VPS IP 152.53.167.28 over a rotating residential IP — fail-closed if it changes). Store the once-shown secret encrypted at rest, never in git/plaintext config.
recommendations.why: Bitso natively supports 5 separable permissions, up to 6 keys, up to 5 IP allowlist entries. A trade-only IP-locked key bounds blast radius to fee/spread churn — a stolen key cannot move funds off-exchange. This is the custody decision licensing the security stance at zero functional cost. CRITICAL: a withdrawals-ENABLED key with an EMPTY whitelist fail-OPENS to any address (platform default-unsafe), so simply not enabling withdrawals is the safe path.

recommendations.recommendation: Make 'agent cannot withdraw' a property of the CODE, not just key config: the live/Paper Executor implements ONLY place-order / cancel / read-balance against Bitso REST and contains NO withdrawal code path. Add a startup self-check that asserts the configured key cannot withdraw (probe a withdrawal endpoint and assert it is denied / fail-closed if a withdrawal-capable key is detected). Treat a deleted/invalid key as a clean kill-switch halt, not a crash loop.
recommendations.why: Defense in depth: even if the key were misconfigured, the executor still cannot withdraw. Deleting a leaked key in Bitso's web UI (2FA-gated) should produce a graceful halt via the existing kill-switch path. This converts the no-withdrawal invariant into a hard, testable code property.

recommendations.recommendation: PRE-TRADE (blocking if skipped): at Bitso 2FA setup, save the emergency restore code offline; keep 2FA on a SEPARATE device from the trading PC; keep KYC docs (INE + proof of address) scanned and ready. Add docs/incident_response.md (RUNBOOK) to treasuryforge with the exact recovery steps: change password (this auto-triggers Bitso's 24h withdrawal freeze) -> change PIN -> change email password -> email customersupport@bitso.com; and 'delete leaked API key in web UI (2FA).' Store the runbook OUTSIDE the trading PC.
recommendations.why: Losing both the 2FA device and the emergency code is the ONLY scenario forcing slow, KYC-gated, text-only recovery (no phone support). Bitso's automatic 24h withdrawal freeze after a password change is a real anti-takeover buffer — the owner's first compromise move is to change the password to trigger it. A leaked trade-only key is revoked instantly with 2FA from a separate device.

recommendations.recommendation: Achieve trading-vs-reserve separation LOGICALLY, not physically: enforce a configurable reserve_floor and a MAX_ON_EXCHANGE ceiling via the existing cumulative-spend budget / per-tx notional cap / solvency rules (deployable capital = balance minus reserve_floor; never accumulate idle uninsured funds beyond loss tolerance). Keep the owner's own ledger of the split.
recommendations.why: A second personal Bitso account is forbidden per CURP and on-chain segregation of a few dollars is uneconomical, so physical separation is off the table. The policy engine already fences a reserve in software. This also caps custodian/freeze/insolvency blast radius (no IPAB insurance, no statutory segregation in MX) to the bounded working balance.

recommendations.recommendation: Fund via SPEI from the owner's OWN bank account whose name matches Bitso KYC, in small explainable amounts; never route third-party money; keep records. Realize results by selling on Bitso and SPEI-ing MXN out (free). Do NOT automate any fiat deposit/withdrawal in the executor.
recommendations.why: Mexico-specific freeze risk: UIF can freeze accounts without a judicial order, and the Oct-2025 Amparo reform removes the usual injunction defense in laundering-linked freezes; SPEI routes through the same surveillance. Clean, KYC-matched, small, documented flows minimize this. Keeping the executor trade-only ensures a freeze can never be amplified by the agent. The peso exit path is free, so self-custody is never on the critical path to realizing the first trade.

recommendations.recommendation: Run the smallest safe end-to-end validation sequence before any real order: (1) read-only first — call View balances / account info with the trade-only key and confirm the IP allowlist works (deliberately confirm a wrong-IP call fails closed); (2) verify proof-of-reserves in-account once; (3) place ONE tiny test order (~200 MXN), confirm the fill in the audit journal, then sell back and SPEI out. Capture per-fill cost basis (timestamp, asset, qty, price, fee, peso spot) for ISR.
recommendations.why: Validates key scoping, IP fail-closed behavior, executor read+trade paths, and the audit/journal pipeline at trivial risk before trusting automation. Per-fill peso records make future ISR computation trivial and are needed regardless because CARF auto-reports to SAT from April 2026.

recommendations.recommendation: Before the FIRST real order, get the exact Bitso MXN<->crypto trading spread/fee from the live trade ticket (NOT the fee page, which omits trading spread). At 11 USD, swap/trading fees are small in absolute terms but the round-trip buy+sell spread is the dominant drag — confirm it is acceptable for the validation experiment.
recommendations.why: Withdrawal/SPEI legs are free or ~0.01 USD, so the real cost of the experiment is the buy/sell spread, which the published fee page does not show. This must be read live before committing.

recommendations.recommendation: Confirm Bitso's CURRENT regulatory/insurance/segregation status from PRIMARY sources (Bitso Trust/legal pages + CNBV registry) before scaling beyond the trivial test; do NOT represent exchange custody as bank-grade/insured in any project doc. Treat Coincover as institutional theft/disaster-recovery mitigation, not deposit insurance for your balance.
recommendations.why: Several security/insurance figures come from third-party 2026 reviews, not Bitso primary disclosures. Crypto balances are NOT covered by IPAB/FDIC; Coincover and Binance-SAFU are discretionary/theft buffers, not insolvency insurance. The only real protection at this size is keeping the balance tiny.

recommendations.recommendation: Confirm Mexican tax treatment with a contador or primary sat.gob.mx text BEFORE realizing gains or doing any self-transfer at scale. Working assumption (secondary-sourced, NOT advice): buying/holding is not taxable; only sale/swap/spend is an ISR enajenacion; wallet-to-own-wallet transfer is generally not a disposal; an annual asset-disposal exemption ~3x UMA exists (exact 2026 figure uncertain). Keep acquisition cost/date and per-disposal peso records from day one.
recommendations.why: No agent retrieved a primary SAT source, so all tax claims are unverified secondary guidance. At 200 MXN no ISR is likely owed, but CARF auto-reporting (April 2026) means activity is visible to SAT regardless; the audit journal should already persist per-fill cost basis to make later reporting trivial. Do not rely on blog figures for the exemption threshold.

recommendations.recommendation: DEFER on-chain self-custody to a deliberate LATER rung, gated on (a) treasury size where flat fees are a small percentage (rough threshold: a few hundred USD+) and (b) a scoped-permission layer (MPC threshold / EIP-7702 session keys / agentic policy wallet) replacing the bare EOA key so the no-withdrawal guarantee survives on-chain. Keep the on-chain Executor as a documented interface stub only; do NOT ship an eth_account.from_key full-blast-radius executor as the first live path. When justified, the cheap native rail is Bitso USDC-on-Base at 0.01 USD (verify 'Base' appears in the live withdrawal UI; never use Tron TRC20 at small size).
recommendations.why: A raw private key cannot be made trade-only/no-withdrawal — a leaked key = 100% instant loss, violating the blast-radius thesis (industry 4B+ hot-wallet losses). On-chain also adds ETH-gas dust management, irreversible-transaction risk, and N taxable swap events. The Base rail is cheap and bridge-free when the time comes, but the security model must be solved first.

themes.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
themes.consensus:
Strong, near-unanimous agreement across all 10 agents on the core path:

PLATFORM: Bitso is the correct venue — Mexico-domiciled, CNBV-regulated, MXN-native funding and local KYC. Every agent converged on it independently. The owner registers himself, completes KYC (required from the first peso — unavoidable but not a per-tx constraint), and funds in MXN.

KEY SCOPING (security stance VALIDATED): Bitso supports exactly the project's least-privilege model. Five individually-toggleable permissions ("Place orders", "View balances", "View account information", "Perform security actions", "Make withdrawals"). A trade-only key = enable "Place orders" + "View balances", leave "Make withdrawals" OFF. Up to 6 keys/account, so issue TWO distinct keys: one for the agent (place+view), one for an independent watchdog/monitor (cancel/read only). IP allowlist (0–5 IPs) is OPTIONAL on Bitso — set it anyway for blast-radius, accepting that a residential dynamic-IP change is a fail-CLOSED (safe) outage. Unlike Binance/OKX, Bitso keys do NOT expire without an IP, so a residential IP is workable and the static-IP-VPS pressure disappears.

SIZE/COST REALITY: At ~11 USD, fees+spread DOMINATE — not regulation, not alpha. Bitso's lowest retail tier (<20k MXN/30d, exactly where this account sits) is ~0.60% maker / ~0.78% taker; a taker round-trip ≈ 1.56% before spread. Trade #1 must be treated as a COST-MEASUREMENT / plumbing-validation experiment, NOT a profit attempt — expect it to net-lose. P&L-based promotion gates are meaningless at this size; gate on operational criteria instead.

INFRASTRUCTURE: No VPS required for the first trade. A daily/low-frequency mechanical (t+1) strategy is latency-insensitive; a $5/mo VPS to manage $11 is economically inverted. Run on the existing Windows PC (NSSM) or the Debian VPS already in memory. Defer VPS to a scale-up decision.

MONITORING: Must be REST-poll-based. Bitso has NO authenticated/private WebSocket (only public order-book channels; the 2024–2026 changelog confirms none was added) and NO documented status/server-time/heartbeat/cancel-on-disconnect endpoint. Poll GET /balance, /orders, /user_trades (incremental marker). Rate limits are generous (60 RPM public/IP, 300 RPM private/user; cancellations EXEMPT; abuse escalates to a 24h block) so the 11-USD cadence is trivially within budget — the danger is a retry-storm self-inflicting the 24h ban.

THE REAL RISK is mechanics, not strategy: (1) silent failure (nonce/auth-rejected or stuck order with no push feed), (2) minimum-notional/dust rejection, (3) duplicate-order retry storms, (4) clock skew on the residential Windows host, (5) state drift between journal and exchange truth. All agents touching incident response agree the existing 8-rule policy engine covers Layer-1, but the LIVE executor must add: startup reconciliation (exchange = ground truth), origin_id idempotency, a client-side dead-man-switch (Bitso has no server-side one), and a manual phone-based key-DELETE as the ultimate kill.

KILL PATH: Bitso exposes DELETE /api/v3/orders/all (cancel-all, returns canceled IDs) — wire it as the executor's panic()/cancel_all(). Cancellations are rate-limit-exempt, so the halt channel always lands.

TAX/LEGAL: All agents flag this UNVERIFIED and defer to primary SAT/CNBV sources. Consensus context only: Bitso KYC is mandatory from peso one; a 200 MXN trade is ~280x below the LFPIORPI AML reporting line (56,000 MXN) and far under the persona-física annual-gain exemption, so AML/tax is non-binding at this size; but crypto-to-crypto swaps and sells are taxable "enajenación" events and CARF reporting to SAT begins in 2026, so log a per-fill tax-lot ledger from day one. RECOURSE BLOCKER: self-inflicted bot losses are NOT reversible via CONDUSEF/CNBV (crypto sits outside Mexican consumer-finance protection) — treat the 200 MXN as fully at-risk burn-in capital.

CODE GROUNDING (verified by reading the repo): executor.py defines `class Executor(Protocol): execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill` and `SimExecutor(fee_rate=0.001, slippage_bps=5.0)` — the swap point is exactly as the findings describe. policy.py confirms all 8 rules, frozen PolicyConfig, the staleness gate is INERT (`data_age_ns` defaults to None and the sim supplies none), the breaker is anchored to `_starting_equity` (not trailing), latched state is snapshot/restore-able, and register_fill debits budget on fills only. Every recommended config value (max_notional_per_tx, max_notional_per_window, max_tx_per_window, max_drawdown_pct, fee_rate, max_staleness_ns, kill_switch) already exists as a field — NO schema change needed, only values + two new behaviors (cancel_all on the executor, real data_age_ns into evaluate()).
themes.contradictions:
1. EXCHANGE MINIMUM ORDER VALUE — the one material data conflict. Agent #1 cites btc_mxn minimum_value=5 MXN (minimum_amount=0.000075 BTC); Agent #9 cites minimum_value=10 MXN (minimum_amount=0.0000006 BTC, tick_size=10) from a LIVE fetch of https://api.bitso.com/v3/available_books and reports the same 10 MXN floor for eth_mxn and usd_mxn. RESOLUTION: trust the live-fetched 10 MXN figure (Agent #9 actually hit the endpoint; Agent #1's 0.000075 BTC vs 0.0000006 BTC numbers also diverge, suggesting #1 quoted a stale/cached doc value). PRACTICAL UPSHOT: moot if the executor fetches available_books at startup and reads minimum_value/minimum_amount/tick_size LIVE per book (both agents independently recommend this pre-flight gate). 200 MXN clears either floor 20–40x over.

2. FEE TIER PRECISION — Agent #1 pins 0.600% maker / 0.780% taker for the lowest MXN tier; Agent #9 says exact Bitso fee numbers were "not pinned" and must be read live before the 200-MXN rung. Not a true conflict — #1 is more specific, #9 appropriately cautious. RESOLUTION: use fee_rate=0.0078 as a conservative placeholder but read the live fee schedule before scaling.

3. "BITSO DOES NOT SUPPORT BOTS" — Agent #4 surfaces a third-party REVIEW claiming Bitso "does not support bots, social/copy trading, DCA," which conflicts with Bitso's own documented private Trading API and third-party integrations (WunderTrading, Jinca, AmeliaBot). RESOLUTION: the "no bots" phrasing is from a review, not Bitso's ToS; API-driven order placement is technically supported and used. Low-risk for a solo individual at $11, but read the current Bitso ToS/API terms before RECURRING automation (not before the one-off test).

4. PER-TX CAP / FIRST RUNG SIZING — Agent #1 recommends max_notional_per_tx=25 MXN as the 200-MXN-rung config; Agent #9 argues the safest FIRST live rung is a single 10–20 MXN smoke order (the absolute floor) before 200 MXN, inserting a sub-200 rung. Not contradictory — they target different rungs; Agent #9's 4-rung framing (PAPER → 10-20 MXN SMOKE → 200 MXN → LARGER) subsumes Agent #1's caps as the 200-MXN-rung config.

No other substantive contradictions — agents are remarkably aligned on platform, key scoping, poll-based monitoring, fail-closed design, and the dominance of fee drag.
blockers: KYC IS MANDATORY FROM THE FIRST PESO: Bitso (CNBV-regulated) requires customer identification/KYC before any virtual-asset activity. Not a per-tx constraint, but a hard registration gate the owner must clear before any trade. Cannot be bypassed.

NO EXCHANGE-SIDE DEAD-MAN-SWITCH ON BITSO: unlike Kraken (cancelAllOrdersAfter), Bybit, BitMEX, Binance Futures, Bitso has NO server-side cancel-on-disconnect/countdown. If BOTH the agent and a client-side watchdog die, resting orders are NOT auto-cancelled. Mitigation: market-only orders at this size leave nothing resting (near-zero residual risk) + build a client-side watchdog. BLOCKS a true server-side DMS but is worked around, not fatal.

NO REGULATORY RECOURSE FOR SELF-INFLICTED LOSSES: crypto sits outside CONDUSEF/Banxico consumer-finance protection in Mexico; bot-made (authorized) trades cannot be reversed by any regulator. The 200 MXN must be treated as fully at-risk burn-in capital. CONDUSEF's fintech portal covers account-TAKEOVER/unauthorized-access only, not the owner's own key's bad trades.

TAX/LEGAL TREATMENT IS UNVERIFIED: no agent fetched an authoritative SAT/CNBV primary source. The persona-fisica 2026 gain-exemption figure CONFLICTS across secondary sources (60,000 vs 128,383 MXN). At 200 MXN any gain is immaterial and tax does NOT block the test trade, but the exact exemption, ISR rates, provisional-withholding mechanics, and CARF (operational 2026) reporting MUST be confirmed with SAT or a contador before scaling. Treat all cited tax figures as UNCONFIRMED.

DYNAMIC RESIDENTIAL IP vs STRICT IP-ALLOWLIST: a residential rotating public IP will 403 the key on change if a strict allowlist is set. This is FAIL-CLOSED (safe) but presents as an outage. Workaround: run from the fixed-IP Debian VPS and allowlist that, OR re-add the IP on change, OR skip the allowlist (Bitso doesn't force it) and rely on trade-only/no-withdrawal scoping for blast radius.

CLOCK SKEW ON THE RESIDENTIAL WINDOWS HOST: Bitso auth signs requests with a nonce/timestamp; local clock drift surfaces as intermittent SILENT auth rejections (order never placed -> agent thinks it's flat). Must verify Windows NTP (w32time) sync and refuse to trade if drift > ~1-2s. NOTE: Bitso's exact HMAC/nonce/timestamp signing rules ('Nonce v2', 2025-03-14) were NOT fully verified in this research and MUST be read from Bitso's auth docs before coding the signer.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: register on Bitso, complete KYC, and mint TWO trade-only keys — Agent key ('Place orders' + 'View balances'), Watchdog/monitor key (cancel-only or read-only). Leave 'Make withdrawals' OFF on both. Optionally set the IP allowlist to the trading host's egress IP (or run from the fixed-IP Debian VPS and allowlist that). Store each secret OUTSIDE the repo.
recommendations.why: Prerequisite for any live trade and the single biggest blast-radius control. A no-withdrawal key bounds the worst case to churning the 200 MXN; it can never be withdrawn. Two keys mean a compromise of one doesn't expose the other, and the monitor/watchdog survives a kill-switch on the trade key. Verified against Bitso's official credentials doc.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: add a BitsoExecutor implementing the existing Executor Protocol (execute(intent,tick,wallet)->Fill) with: (a) a startup loader that fetches /v3/available_books and caches minimum_value/minimum_amount/tick_size per book; (b) a pre-flight gate that REJECTS (never silently truncates) any intent below live minimums or within ~10% of the floor after fee+slippage buffer; (c) a deterministic journaled origin_id (<=40 chars, [a-zA-Z0-9_-]) reused on every retry; (d) an explicit cancel_all() = signed DELETE /v3/orders/all. Keep policy.py untouched.
recommendations.why: Minimum-notional/dust rejection is the most likely HARD failure at 11 USD, and origin_id is the primary defense against duplicate-order retry storms (Bitso enforces origin_id uniqueness across ACTIVE orders). The live minimum is 10 MXN (live /v3/available_books) — reading it live makes the 5-vs-10 doc conflict moot. The Protocol swap point is verified in executor.py.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: add a STARTUP + PRE-ORDER reconciliation gate. On boot and after any timeout/restart, query Bitso open orders + recent user_trades (match by origin_id), merge into the journal treating EXCHANGE as ground truth, and if exchange balance/positions diverge from journaled state beyond epsilon, trip the kill-switch and refuse to trade until manually cleared. Never blind-retry a submit — reconcile first.
recommendations.why: State drift is described across multiple agents as 'the single most dangerous failure mode in automated trading' and is the #1 live-only failure a SimExecutor never exercises — MOST likely precisely after a watchdog/crash restart. The project is already crash-safe/journaled, so this is the highest-value single live change.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: instantiate PolicyConfig for the 200-MXN rung with max_notional_per_tx=25.0, max_notional_per_window=100.0, max_tx_per_window=4, max_drawdown_pct=0.05, fee_rate=0.0078 (conservative top-tier taker). Add a startup assertion that max_notional_per_tx >= the LIVE exchange minimum_value. CRITICAL: bind the tick-based windows (window_steps, spend_window_steps) to real 24h wall-clock in live mode — the engine measures windows in ticks via _prune, so 'daily' is meaningless until a tick maps to wall-clock.
recommendations.why: All values use EXISTING PolicyConfig fields (verified in policy.py) — no schema change. Bounds worst-case run loss to ~10 MXN (5% breaker, outside BTC's ~2%/day noise but leaving room for ~3 round-trips of fee drag) and daily fee bleed to ~1.6 MXN. The tick-to-wallclock binding is a genuine prerequisite or the daily caps stay inert. The 5%/100-MXN/4-tx thresholds are engineering judgments (medium confidence) — tune them, but the structure is sound.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: ACTIVATE the currently-inert staleness gate by passing a real data_age_ns into PolicyEngine.evaluate() in live mode (it defaults to None and the sim supplies none, so the rule never fires today). Add freshness fields (last_market_ts, last_account_ts); when data age exceeds ~2-3x the poll interval, propose only no-op. Set max_staleness_ns accordingly.
recommendations.why: Verified in policy.py (line 97): the staleness gate is genuinely inert (requires BOTH max_staleness_ns AND data_age_ns non-None). Stale data is the top silent failure; without this the agent could trade on a frozen price during a Bitso outage. Small, high-value live-only wiring change.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: build a standalone watchdog.py (separate process, separate trade-only key) implementing a client-side dead-man-switch — read the agent's journaled heartbeat; if heartbeat age > N seconds, fire DELETE /v3/orders/all and set the journaled kill-switch flag so the agent refuses to re-arm on restart. Bitso cancellations are rate-limit-EXEMPT so this halt channel always lands.
recommendations.why: Bitso has NO server-side cancel-on-disconnect (confirmed BLOCKER vs Kraken/Bybit/BitMEX/Binance), so the only equivalent is client-side. At 11 USD with market-only orders nothing rests, so residual risk is near-zero TODAY (hence medium impact) — but the watchdog should exist before live funds and becomes essential if the strategy ever uses resting limit orders.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase + Owner: build the smallest safe E2E validation sequence and the live secret loader. Sequence: (1) read-only GET balances/open-orders with the agent key; (2) verify NTP/clock drift < 1-2s and fetch live minimums; (3) place ONE ~10-20 MXN market BUY (smoke rung, BELOW the 200 target); (4) immediately call cancel_all() to prove the kill path; (5) reconcile fill price/fee from /user_trades against what SimExecutor would have recorded and log the 'sim vs actual' delta to the HMAC audit. Secret loader: read keys via keyring (Windows Credential Locker) first, then env-var fallback to stay stdlib-pure, then fail loudly — never from a tracked file.
recommendations.why: This is the cautious read-only -> tiny-order ramp the goal asks for, making trade #1 a COST-MEASUREMENT experiment (the decisive paper-to-live divergence is slippage/fees the sim hides, which dominate at 11 USD). A 10-20 MXN smoke order clears the live ~10 MXN floor and de-risks the jump before the full 200. keyring is the ONE conscious break of the stdlib-only rule; .env+gitignore+gitleaks is the pure-stdlib fallback. NTP check pre-empts silent auth rejections.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: add a stdlib poll-loop Monitor (urllib + hmac) — GET /balance, /orders, /user_trades with incremental marker, ~10-30s cadence (well under 300 RPM private cap) — that reconciles exchange state vs journal and alerts on divergence. Add exponential backoff WITH JITTER on 429/5xx, honor Retry-After, cap self-imposed RPM well under the limit, and treat repeated 429/5xx as fail-closed (stop proposing, alert ONCE) — never retry-storm into Bitso's escalating 24h block. Wire a stdlib-only Telegram Bot Notifier (urllib to sendMessage; smtplib email fallback) for fills/errors/breaker trips.
recommendations.why: Bitso has no private WebSocket, so monitoring MUST be poll-based; silent failure (not throughput) is the operational risk. A retry storm self-inflicting the 24h ban is the one thing to engineer against. Telegram-via-urllib matches the stdlib-only / local-first / zero-cloud stance with no new dependency or cost and gives human-in-the-loop visibility for the first live trade.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase + Owner: emit a per-fill tax-lot ledger (timestamp, pair, side, qty, price, fee, MXN cost basis) from day one — the existing HMAC-audited journal already captures most of this. Owner: CONFIRM the 2026 persona-fisica gain exemption (sources conflict: 60k vs 128,383 MXN), ISR/provisional-withholding mechanics, and CARF reporting obligations directly with SAT (sat.gob.mx) or a contador BEFORE scaling beyond token size. Do NOT rely on any secondary tax figure cited in this research.
recommendations.why: Tax does NOT block the 200 MXN test (gain immaterial, ~280x below AML lines), but every agent flags the legal treatment as UNVERIFIED and the exemption figure as conflicting. CARF makes Bitso's activity SAT-visible from 2026, and crypto-to-crypto swaps are taxable enajenacion — so the recordkeeping habit matters now even when the amounts don't. This is the explicit primary-source-confirmation item.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: run the agent on the EXISTING Windows PC under NSSM (boot-start + auto-restart with restart-storm throttle + log rotation, exit=Exit) — or the Debian VPS under systemd Type=notify+WatchdogSec via stdlib sd_notify. Do NOT buy a VPS for the first trade. Subscribe to status.bitso.com. Pre-practice the phone-based password-change + API-key-DELETE flow once on day 1 so it's muscle memory; pre-save customersupport@bitso.com. Treat the 200 MXN as fully at-risk burn-in capital with NO regulatory recourse.
recommendations.why: A $5/mo VPS to manage $11 is economically inverted; the strategy is latency-insensitive. Reliability here is software correctness (idempotent journaled state + reconciliation), not hardware. NSSM matches the owner's documented prior experience (the AppEnvironmentExtra-needs-restart and ASCII-only-.ps1 gotchas apply). The self-service phone key-DELETE is the fastest, most certain hard-stop if the host is compromised — and the only recourse, since CONDUSEF won't reverse self-inflicted bot trades.

recommendations.recommendation: Codebase: poll Bitso's free, auth-free Atlassian Statuspage JSON (https://status.bitso.com/api/v2/summary.json) every 30-60s as an OUT-OF-BAND health input; map indicator!='none' OR 'API'/'Trading Engine' component != operational OR a non-empty scheduled_maintenances window -> a new policy input venue_healthy=False that makes the engine reject all proposed intents and alert once, resetting only when status returns operational AND reconcile() is clean.
recommendations.why: Agent #6 fetched this endpoint LIVE and confirmed valid component-level JSON with dedicated 'API' and 'Trading Engine' components — a true out-of-band signal that distinguishes 'my key/IP is throttled' from 'Bitso is actually down,' which the REST API alone cannot. Free, zero-key, read-only. Low impact because freshness/reconciliation gates already fail safe on errors, but it's a cheap, high-signal addition.

recommendations.recommendation: Owner: before any RECURRING automation (not before the one-off test), read Bitso's current ToS / API terms and confirm programmatic/automated order placement by an individual is permitted; if ambiguous, get written confirmation from Bitso support. Codebase: before coding the signer, verify Bitso's exact HMAC/nonce/timestamp ('Nonce v2', 2025-03-14) signing rules from Bitso's auth docs — these were NOT fully verified in this research.
recommendations.why: A third-party review claims 'Bitso does not support bots,' conflicting with its documented Trading API and live third-party integrations — likely review hype, not ToS, and low-risk for a solo $11 trade, but worth resolving before scaling. The signing details are an unverified coding-prerequisite: a wrong nonce/timestamp causes SILENT auth rejection (the exact failure the monitor must surface).

themes.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
themes.consensus: Strong, near-unanimous consensus across all 10 agents: NOTHING in Mexican law blocks a persona fisica from making a ~200 MXN (~11 USD) own-funds crypto trade in 2026, and no personal license, CNBV/Banxico authorization, AMIB certification, or SAT anti-lavado (SPPLD) registration is required. The reasoning is consistent: (1) Crypto = "activos virtuales" under the Ley Fintech (LRITF, 2018, amended 2025) — legal intangible property, not legal tender, but not banned for individuals. (2) The restrictive rules (Banxico Circular 4/2019; the LFPIORPI "actividad vulnerable" regime; ITF authorization) bind BANKS, FINTECHS/ITFs, and PROVIDERS/VASPs — NOT the retail end-user trading his own funds. This bank-vs-individual "gray zone" is precisely why consumer exchanges operate under the AML provider regime with mandatory KYC. (3) Running an automated/algorithmic bot on one's OWN account does not change legality — algo order entry on own funds is not separately regulated. (4) The only individual-side obligations are: complete the exchange's KYC, and pay ISR on realized gains (tax, separate from licensing). (5) Bitso is the unanimous concrete platform recommendation: a CNBV-supervised ITF (the MX fiat/payment leg via NVIO Pagos Mexico, an IFPE in SIPRES; crypto leg under Gibraltar GFSC), native MXN/SPEI on-ramp, INE/CURP+selfie KYC, never reported hacked. (6) Bitso natively supports the EXACT requested security stance: granular API permissions (Place orders / View balances / View account / Make withdrawals / Perform security actions — each independently toggleable) plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses. A TRADE-ONLY, withdrawals-DISABLED, IP-pinned key is directly creatable, reinforcing the policy engine's blast-radius containment. (7) AML reporting thresholds (210 UMA ≈ 24,635 MXN identification/aviso post-July-2025 reform; 645 UMA ≈ 75,665 MXN historical aviso; 2026 UMA = 117.31 MXN/day) are provider obligations and are ~100x above a 200 MXN trade, so the trade triggers no report. (8) CARF / standardized exchange-to-SAT reporting is arriving (Mexico, ~2026-2027) — assume full SAT visibility; keep clean per-fill MXN cost-basis records from trade #1. (9) Custody at $11: leave funds on-exchange — self-custody would require enabling withdrawal permission, directly contradicting the no-withdrawal stance, and on-chain fees dwarf the position. (10) Mexico is NOT OFAC-embargoed; the regulated CEX runs OFAC/EU/UN screening on deposits/withdrawals, and a CEX-internal-only + no-withdrawal executor is structurally incapable of touching an SDN-listed address.
themes.contradictions: Few hard contradictions; mostly figure-level variance flagged as low/medium confidence by the agents themselves. (1) AML aviso/reporting threshold: sources split between 645 UMA (~75,665 MXN, historical/SAT-Bitso) and 210 UMA (~24,635 MXN, post-July-2025 reform). Likely both are real but apply to different duties (210 UMA = identification/lowered reporting trigger; 645 UMA = older aviso figure) — irrelevant at 200 MXN either way, but the exact peso figure is unverified against primary DOF/SAT text. (2) ISR movable-property exemption ~128,383 MXN (3 UMA annual) gain: cited by several agents as "likely covers" the trade, but at least two agents flag LOW confidence that this Art. 93-XIX-b exemption cleanly applies to CRYPTO specifically — it is reported by tax blogs, not a primary SAT ruling. One agent adds the Art. 150 LISR nuance: if total annual income from all sources exceeds 500,000 MXN, even exempt amounts must be reported. (3) CARF Mexico go-live timing: disputed (~April 2026 "real-time SAT access" per several MX guides vs OECD-aligned "first exchanges 2027"). Direction (full transparency) is agreed; exact date is not. (4) Bitso fee/spread figures (MXN market ~0.36-0.60% vs USD-pair ~0.04/0.05% maker/taker) come from review/aggregator sources, not Bitso's live fee schedule — flagged medium confidence, must verify on the official page. (5) The "no bot-specific rule" point is high-confidence for the conclusion but rests on absence-of-rule reasoning (no source addresses bots directly), so it is an inference, not a cited prohibition's absence. (6) Two agents could not load primary SAT pages (HTTP 403/79706), so the provider-vs-user distinction, while corroborated by Hacienda PLD docs and GLI, is not quoted from SAT primary text.
blockers: NONE that legally stop the ~200 MXN first trade. Every agent classifies the regulatory/legal dimension as ENABLES for an own-funds individual.

SOFT/PROCEDURAL GATE (not a legal blocker): mandatory exchange KYC must be PASSED before any order — INE/CURP + selfie/liveness, possibly proof of address; budget minutes-to-hours. Trading is gated until verification completes.
