BRIGHT LINE TO NEVER CROSS (would convert legal hobby into regulated/illegal activity): never accept, pool, hold custody of, or trade ANY third-party funds, and never offer habitual/professional individualized paid recommendations or market the agent as a money-management/advisory service — that triggers CNBV 'Asesor en Inversiones' registration (LMV Art. 225) and/or the LFPIORPI 'actividad vulnerable' provider regime. Keep treasuryforge strictly single-user/own-funds.

TAX OBLIGATION (does not block the trade, but is a real individual-side duty): ISR on realized gains; every crypto-to-crypto swap AND every sell-to-MXN is a taxable disposal. Negligible at 11 USD, but the obligation and record-keeping start at trade #1. Exact exemption/threshold figures are UNVERIFIED against primary SAT text — confirm with a contador before any trade large enough to realize meaningful gains.

CUSTODY/RECOURSE CAVEAT (not a blocker at this size): exchange balances are UNINSURED — no IPAB coverage for crypto, Bitso does not insure fiat balances (only a private Coincover/Lloyd's crypto-THEFT policy, not insolvency/fiat); CONDUSEF reaches only the CNBV-registered fiat leg (NVIO Pagos Mexico) and is non-binding mediation. Acceptable at 11 USD; re-evaluate before scaling balances.

recommendations.recommendation: Proceed with the ~200 MXN first trade as a persona fisica on Bitso (CNBV-supervised ITF, native MXN/SPEI on-ramp). No personal license, CNBV/Banxico/AMIB registration, or SAT anti-lavado (SPPLD) filing is required for own-funds trading. Register personally, complete KYC (INE/CURP + selfie, possibly proof of address), fund ~200 MXN via SPEI.
recommendations.why: Unanimous high-confidence consensus across all 10 agents: the legal path is green for an own-funds individual; the restrictive regime binds banks/fintechs/providers, not the retail user. KYC is the only gate and it is a cooperate-with-onboarding step, not a state filing.

recommendations.recommendation: Create the Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (and optionally read-only 'View account information') ENABLED; leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' DISABLED; set the IP allowlist to the agent host's static IP; enable 2FA. Store key/secret outside the repo (env/secret file). Validate first with read-only calls (balances/ticker) BEFORE any test order.
recommendations.why: Bitso natively supports each permission as an independent toggle plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses — this exactly satisfies the trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted security stance and reinforces the policy engine's blast-radius containment. A withdrawals-disabled key makes the agent structurally incapable of sending funds to any external (including SDN-listed) address.

recommendations.recommendation: Keep treasuryforge strictly single-user / own-funds. Add a SCOPE.md / README legal note asserting third-party funds, pooling, custody-of-others, and offering signals/management as a service are explicitly OUT OF SCOPE pending Mexican legal counsel + CNBV 'Asesor en Inversiones' registration. Optionally encode an own-account-only assertion in policy/config.
recommendations.why: This is the single bright line: LMV Art. 225 triggers CNBV registration only when managing THIRD-PARTY funds, HABITUALLY, and PROFESSIONALLY (all three). Even registered advisors are barred from custody of clients' money. Documenting the non-goal keeps the project provably on the legal side and makes the boundary auditable.

recommendations.recommendation: Extend the executor/journal to log every fill (timestamp, side, pair, qty, price, fee, and MXN-equivalent value in/out) as a tax-grade ledger with CSV export, capturing per-disposal MXN cost basis from trade #1. The HMAC-audited decision log already provides the evidence trail; add the fill-level economic detail.
recommendations.why: Every crypto-to-crypto swap and sell-to-MXN is a taxable enajenacion under ISR; CARF / standardized exchange-to-SAT reporting (~2026-2027) means assume full SAT visibility. A mechanical strategy generates many taxable events even at tiny size, so the ledger must be reconcilable against what Bitso reports. Building this now is cheap; reconstructing later is not.

recommendations.recommendation: Before any trade large enough to realize meaningful gains (NOT required for the 200 MXN test), confirm with a Mexican contador / primary SAT (Ley del ISR) source: (a) whether the ~128,383 MXN (3-UMA) movable-property exemption applies to crypto disposals, (b) the annual-declaration trigger, and (c) the Art. 150 'report all income incl. exempt if total >500k MXN' rule. Do NOT rely on tax-blog peso figures as exact.
recommendations.why: Tax treatment is the area with the most low/medium-confidence, secondary-source figures and at least one agent explicitly flagging LOW confidence that the exemption applies to crypto. At 11 USD the gain is negligible so this does not block the first trade, but it must be settled before scaling.

recommendations.recommendation: Hard-constrain the tradable-asset allowlist to high-liquidity majors (e.g., BTC, ETH) quoted against MXN; keep the quote/settlement leg as MXN or exchange-custodied spot — NOT self-custodied on-chain USDT/USDC. Hard-exclude privacy coins (XMR/ZEC/DASH). Restrict the executor to the CEX-internal order book only — never send to/receive from external addresses, P2P, or mixers.
recommendations.why: Centralized stablecoins are freezable at contract level and innocent 'tainted-coin' holders have been caught (Tether ~$3.3B / Circle ~$110M frozen); privacy coins are delisted across major exchanges. Keeping everything CEX-internal + MXN-quoted neutralizes freeze risk, the cartel-FTO 'material support' line (Feb-2025 designations; OFAC has added cartel-linked ETH addresses to the SDN list), and the entire mixer-sanctions question.

recommendations.recommendation: Verify Bitso's CURRENT MXN-pair fee and live book spread on the official Bitso fee page before sizing the first order; prefer maker/limit orders and avoid unnecessary round-trips. Have the executor read live fee + spread and feed real cost into the policy engine's notional/solvency checks.
recommendations.why: At 200 MXN, round-trip cost at the MXN-market rate is ~1.5-2.5 MXN — material but not prohibitive. Fee figures in the research are from aggregators (medium confidence) and thin MXN-book spread can exceed the stated fee at this size; this is consistent with the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate. Must verify against the live schedule before relying on it.

recommendations.recommendation: For the validation phase, leave the ~200 MXN of crypto on Bitso (exchange custody) with withdrawals DISABLED on the trading key; keep only working capital on the venue and move pesos in at the moment of buying. Document a future self-custody threshold as a backlog item and treat on-venue balances as uninsured at-risk capital.
recommendations.why: At 11 USD, self-custody requires enabling withdrawal permission (contradicting the no-withdrawal stance) and on-chain fees dwarf the position. But there is no IPAB/deposit insurance and CONDUSEF reaches only the fiat leg with non-binding mediation, so the right posture is minimal on-venue balance + uninsured-capital awareness, re-evaluated before scaling.

recommendations.recommendation: As a ~5-minute pre-flight before going live: verify Bitso/NVIO Pagos Mexico's current SIPRES registration at condusef.gob.mx, confirm which legal entity holds the account (NVIO for MX fiat vs Bitso/Gibraltar for crypto) in the in-app terms, and skim Bitso's ToS 'Prohibited Uses'/sanctions section plus CNBV/UIF AML guidance.
recommendations.why: These primary-source confirmations were flagged low confidence (some SAT/ToS pages returned 403 and could not be quoted directly). None is expected to block a clean individual, but verifying the live registration, the responsible entity per leg, and the ToS prohibited-uses clause is cheap insurance before committing real funds.

verdicts.recommendation: Choose Bitso as the Phase-3 platform and trade exclusively via the Bitso Alpha / Trading API (POST /v3/orders), never the consumer Quick Buy/convert path which buries a variable spread.
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation holds up on every load-bearing technical claim, but two of its specific numbers are softer than stated and one regulatory phrasing is imprecise.

CONFIRMED (primary/official sources):
1. Endpoint is real and current. POST /api/v3/orders exists, takes book/side/type/major|minor(+price for limit), supports market and limit orders (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/place-an-order). This is the correct programmatic path and matches the SimExecutor->Executor swap-in design.
2. The spread-vs-fee distinction is real and correctly directional. Bitso's own help center confirms the app Quick Buy/convert charges a percentage SPREAD, while Alpha uses maker/taker fees. Routing through Alpha is genuinely the cheapest path and the single most expensive mistake to avoid. The core thesis is sound.
3. API key scoping exactly matches the security stance. Bitso's create-credentials docs list 5 INDEPENDENT permission toggles: Place orders / View balances / View account info / Perform security actions / Make withdrawals. A TRADE-ONLY, NO-withdrawal key is fully supported (enable Place orders, leave Make withdrawals off). IP allowlist ('Restrict use of API addresses') supports up to 5 addresses. The blast-radius-containment design is implementable as written.
4. CNBV regulation is real (with a caveat — see below). KYC for a Mexican individual is CURP-based and the platform is the dominant MX on-ramp with free SPEI.
5. Tax treatment maps to actual law. Crypto gains = enajenacion de bienes muebles intangibles under LISR; personas fisicas get the Art. 93 Frac. XIX exemption of ~128,383 MXN/yr (3x annual UMA) for 2026. At ~11 USD trade size, any gain is orders of magnitude below the exemption, so tax is a NON-BLOCKER for the first trade (record-keeping only). The LISR is published by SAT (sat.gob.mx).

WEAKNESSES / OVERSTATEMENTS:
A. The '~1.5-2% spread' and '0.15%/0.19% maker/taker' figures are NOT Bitso-published for this user's situation. Bitso explicitly says spread 'varies by region and currency' and does not publish a number — the 1.5-2% is a reasonable estimate, not a citable fact. More importantly, Bitso's OFFICIAL fee page shows MXN-market fees are HIGHER than the global 0.15/0.19: MXN maker 0.100%-0.600%, taker 0.130%-0.780%, with a first-trade individual sitting at the WORST (highest-volume-discount-free) tier. So real Alpha cost on a BTC/MXN first trade is closer to ~0.6-0.78% taker, not 0.19%. The spread is still worse, so the recommendation's CONCLUSION stands, but the quantitative gap ('2-5x') is overstated for MXN pairs — it's more like ~2-3x. Using a USD/USDC-quoted book (maker 0.04-0.30 / taker 0.05-0.36) would actually be cheaper than MXN books.
B. Regulatory phrasing is imprecise. The CNBV IFPE license is held by NVIO Pagos Mexico (a Bitso subsidiary), not 'Bitso' the trading brand directly. Practically fine, but the claim 'Bitso is CNBV-regulated' is a simplification.
C. '9/10 agents converge' is an appeal-to-consensus, not evidence — irrelevant to whether it's correct (it happens to be).

OPERATIONAL RISK for the stated security stance: IP allowlisting (up to 5 static IPs) collides with this builder's KNOWN dynamic residential Mexican IP (documented in his own memory: googlevideo home-IP rotation saga). If the bot runs from his PC on a rotating residential IP, the allowlist will intermittently lock the key out. Mitigation: run the executor from a fixed-IP VPS (he already operates Netcup 152.53.167.28) and allowlist that, OR accept no-IP-restriction but keep the no-withdrawal scope as the primary blast-radius control. Do NOT skip the no-withdrawal toggle.

COST REALITY at 11 USD: minimum trade size on Bitso is ~10 USD, so the ~11 USD target sits right at the floor — a sell-then-rebuy validation cycle could dip below minimum after fees. Round-trip drag (taker in + taker out on MXN book ~0.78% x2 = ~1.5%, ~0.17 USD) plus SPEI-free funding is acceptable for a validation trade but means this size is for VALIDATION ONLY, not for any expectation of strategy edge surviving costs.
verdicts.caveats: 1. Verify the exact entry-tier fee live in your own Bitso account before the first order (MXN books can be ~0.6/0.78% not the quoted 0.15/0.19; consider a USDC-quoted book which is cheaper). 2. The 1.5-2% spread figure is an estimate — confirm the actual spread on the app's order-summary screen if you ever compare. 3. CNBV license is held by NVIO (Bitso subsidiary), not Bitso S.A. directly — accurate enough but know the structure. 4. IP allowlist + your dynamic residential IP will cause lockouts — run the executor from your fixed-IP VPS and allowlist that, or rely on no-withdrawal scope alone. 5. ~10 USD is the trade minimum; an 11 USD trade is at the floor — keep it a single validation trade, don't expect a round-trip to clear costs. 6. Tax is a non-blocker at this size (far below the 128,383 MXN exemption) but keep CSV records from day one. 7. Live-validate the smallest sequence: read-only balance call -> single limit order well away from market (won't fill) to confirm order acceptance -> cancel -> then one tiny real market order.

verdicts.recommendation: Mint the API key TRADE-ONLY: enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ optionally 'View account information'); explicitly leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF; enable 'Restrict use of API addresses' with the executor host's static IP. Use one dedicated key (of the 6 allowed).
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED AND CURRENT (2026). Every mechanical claim checks out against Bitso's own docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials) and 2026 sources:

1) The five permission scopes are named exactly as stated and confirmed verbatim: "Place orders", "View balances", "View account information", "Perform security actions", "Make withdrawals". Place orders = trade + view trade history; View balances = balances per currency; the two left OFF are the exact fund-exfiltration vectors. Correct.
2) "Restrict use of API addresses" IP-allowlist exists (0 to 5 IPs). Confirmed.
3) Up to 6 API keys per account. Confirmed.
4) Trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-pinned is NATIVELY supported, zero workaround. The core claim is true. Leaving "Perform security actions" OFF is genuinely correct because that scope can modify withdrawal restrictions/account settings.
5) No regulatory blocker for a Mexican individual: Bitso operates under the Ley Fintech as a CNBV-regulated path (NVIO Pagos Mexico SAPI is the CNBV-authorized IFPE entity). An individual can register and KYC himself.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS THAT FAILED: I tried to break the permission-name accuracy, the IP-whitelist existence, the key count, and the regulatory availability — all held against Bitso primary docs and multiple 2026 reviews.

GENUINE WEAK POINT (the one thing to pressure-test before acting): the recommendation assumes "the executor host's static IP." Per the owner's own infra notes, his executor candidates are (a) a residential MX home IP, which is dynamic/CGNAT and rotates — IP-allowlisting it will SILENTLY break the key on every rotation and cause auth failures mid-trade — or (b) the Netcup VPS 152.53.167.28, which IS static. The blast-radius guarantee only holds on a genuinely static IP. Pin to the VPS, not the home PC. This is an operational caveat, not a refutation of the security posture.

SCOPE CAVEATS (don't falsify the rec, but matter for the Phase-3 path): (a) the ~11 USD trade sits right at Bitso's $10 minimum order floor — viable but razor-thin; treat it as a validation trade, not an investment. MXN-market taker fees run ~0.13%-0.65% plus spread, so a buy+sell round-trip burns ~1-2%+ at this size. (b) Tax claims gathered (LISR Art. 126 20% provisional on disposal; 2026 digital-platform withholding 1%->2.5%; ~128,383 MXN / 3x-UMA annual exemption that easily covers an $11 trade) all came from SECONDARY blogs, not SAT/DOF primary text — per the owner's standing rule these must be downgraded until verified against SAT/DOF directly. The recommendation itself makes no tax claim, so it is not falsified by this.
verdicts.caveats: CRITICAL before acting: do NOT IP-allowlist a dynamic residential home IP — it will silently brick the key on IP rotation. Pin to the VPS static IP (152.53.167.28) where the executor actually runs, or skip IP-restriction only if you cannot guarantee a static IP (but then the blast-radius claim weakens — keep withdrawals OFF as the hard wall). Also: ~11 USD is at the $10 Bitso minimum, so this is a validation trade with ~1-2%+ fee drag, not a cost-efficient investment. Tax figures (Art.126 20% provisional, 2.5% platform withholding, 128,383 MXN exemption) are from secondary sources and must be confirmed against SAT/DOF primary before being relied on — but they do not block the $11 first trade.

verdicts.recommendation: Build BitsoExecutor with stdlib HMAC-SHA256 signing; persist the last-used nonce in the existing crash-safe journal so a restart cannot reuse or regress the nonce. Add a startup self-check (fail-closed) that asserts the key has NO withdrawal scope before any order is placed.
verdicts.assessment:
Core platform + signing claims VERIFIED and current (2026). Bitso is the correct venue for a Mexico-based individual: it is CNBV-supervised (filial NVIO was the first authorized IFPE under Ley Fintech, 2020), MXN-native with SPEI fiat on-ramp and BTC/MXN, ETH/MXN books — ideal for a ~200 MXN first trade. The API auth is exactly as the recommendation states and fully stdlib-implementable: HMAC-SHA256 over the concatenation nonce + HTTP method + request path + JSON payload, hex-encoded, sent as header "Bitso key:nonce:signature" (docs.bitso.com create-signed-requests + authentication). Nonce must be a unique strictly-increasing integer; Bitso recommends Unix-ms. No SDK required — fits the stdlib-only constraint cleanly. The security STANCE is fully supported by the platform: trade-only is real (separate "Place orders" / "View balances" / "Make withdrawals" scopes), and IP allowlisting is real (up to 5 IPs) — both set at key creation in the Bitso UI.

THREE caveats that move this from do-now to do-with-care:

1) The startup fail-closed key-scope self-check is NOT implementable AS WRITTEN. There is no documented Bitso endpoint that returns an API key's own permission scopes; GET /account_status returns account-level info (limits, KYC, status), not per-key scopes. The literal "assert the key has NO withdrawal scope" cannot be done by querying scopes. Reframe it: (a) enforce no-withdrawal at key-creation in the UI (this is the real control), and (b) for a runtime fail-closed proxy, issue a deliberately safe-to-fail withdrawal-scoped probe at startup and assert it returns an authorization/permission error rather than a validation error — designed so it can NEVER move funds. This is more delicate and must be coded carefully, not asserted as a simple scope read.

2) Nonce persistence is sound but partly redundant: a Unix-ms nonce already increases monotonically across restarts on a healthy clock. Persisting it is good defense against NTP step-back and accidental dual-instance, not a strict crash-replay necessity. Keep it — it is cheap insurance — but do not oversell it as the primary safety mechanism.

3) Fees/spread drag at ~11 USD: Bitso maker/taker fees plus BTC/MXN spread are a material fraction at this size; the cost-aware backtest gate (Deflated Sharpe + purged CV) from Phase 2 must include Bitso's actual fee schedule (queryable via GET /fees) before any live order is sized.

Tax/legal (Mexico individual, primary-source-anchored): crypto sale = enajenacion de bienes under LISR (Art. 124 inflation adjustment; activo virtual per Art. 30 Ley Fintech). Bitso does NOT withhold ISR on gains — self-reporting is the individual's responsibility. The 20% provisional retention some secondary sources cite is the digital-intermediation-platform regime (LISR 113-C area), not crypto spot sales on an exchange, so it does not apply here. There is an annual exemption for asset-disposal gains (~3x annual UMA, ~128k MXN cited for 2026), so an ~11 USD trade is far below any tax due — but records must be kept, and CARF reporting (OCDE, 2026) means the exchange reports activity to SAT, so keep clean cost-basis records from trade #1. None of this BLOCKS the first trade.

Custody at this size: leave funds custodial on Bitso. Self-custody/withdrawal infrastructure is unjustified blast-radius and cost for ~11 USD, and the whole point is a no-withdrawal key — withdrawals (if ever) stay manual via the UI/2FA.

Nothing blocks a Mexico-based individual from registering, KYC'ing, and executing this path today. The minimal safe sequence holds: register + KYC on Bitso, fund via SPEI, create a trade-only + IP-allowlisted key (no withdrawal), validate read-only (GET /balance, /account_status) end-to-end, then place one tiny market order well inside the policy engine's per-tx notional cap.
verdicts.caveats: The single load-bearing inaccuracy: the "assert key has NO withdrawal scope" self-check has no Bitso API to read scopes — it must be reframed as a UI-level control plus a carefully-designed safe-to-fail withdrawal probe, never a scope query. Secondary: nonce persistence is defensive-redundant (Unix-ms already monotonic), and fee/spread drag at ~11 USD must be wired into the cost gate using GET /fees before live sizing. Tax/legal does not block the trade but cost-basis records must start at trade #1 (CARF 2026). Verify Bitso's current fee schedule and the exact private-endpoint paths live against your own account before the first real order, since docs were read but not exercised against a live key.

verdicts.recommendation: Run the live executor from the stable Netcup VPS egress IP and lock the Bitso API key to it (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted); if a static IP is truly unavailable, fall back to no-IP-lock + no-withdrawal + policy engine and document the weaker posture.
verdicts.assessment:
The operational conclusion is sound and the security primitives it depends on are real and current for 2026. Bitso's API supports granular permissions: "Place orders" is separable from "Make withdrawals," and IP allowlisting accepts up to 5 IPs (confirmed in both cited Bitso docs). So a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-locked key is genuinely achievable for an individual on Mexico's CNBV-regulated ITF. The thesis that the IP allowlist is meaningful even with a no-withdrawal key holds up better than the recommendation argues: a leaked trade-only key is NOT fully safe because attackers run wash-trade/self-cross attacks on illiquid pairs to siphon value without any withdrawal right, so binding the key to a stable VPS IP is legitimate defense-in-depth. The fallback (no-IP-lock + no-withdrawal + the 8-rule policy engine, with the weaker posture documented) is responsible and correct.

However, three defects keep this from do-now. (1) Platform-mismatch in the justification: the "why" claims the key would "auto-expire ... on Binance" — that IS a real Binance rule (keys without an IP whitelist are disabled after 90 days / deleted after 30 days inactive, and withdrawals require an IP), but every cited source is Bitso, and Bitso's docs do NOT document any auto-expiry for keys lacking an IP restriction. The conclusion survives, but one of its two stated reasons is imported from the wrong exchange and should not be relied on for a Bitso decision. (2) Unverified geo/account-origin: there is no documented Bitso geo-block (IP restriction is user-config, not geo-fencing), but I could not confirm live that a German VPS IP (152.53.167.28) is accepted for API trading on a Mexico-resident ITF account — this needs a read-only probe before committing. (3) Bigger latent blocker for the Phase-3 goal: Bitso's own onboarding docs state "many APIs require a Bitso business account, even for testing," while only "some ... work with individual accounts" — so an individual personal-KYC account placing spot orders via API must be confirmed live before assuming the platform is even usable. Tax is moot at this size (movable-property gains exemption is ~128k MXN/yr for 2026 per secondary sources; no SAT/CNBV primary located, but $11 cannot breach it), and fee drag is fine IF the executor uses the order book (taker ~0.19%, ~US$0.02) and avoids the spread-based "quick buy" convert path — neither addressed, but both are outside this recommendation's narrow scope.
verdicts.caveats: Scope mislabel: titled "Platform Selection for Mexico" but only answers WHERE to host the executor; it presupposes Bitso is chosen and that an individual can trade via API. Before acting, do a live read-only probe from the VPS IP confirming (a) individual/personal-KYC account can call private trading endpoints, and (b) the German IP is accepted. Drop or rewrite the Binance auto-expiry justification — it does not apply to Bitso and the cited sources don't support it. Verify minimum order size ($10 reported) leaves headroom at ~$11 after fees, and force the executor onto the order book, not the spread/convert path. Tax claims rest on secondary sources only; immaterial at $11 but get a SAT primary before scaling. Start the end-to-end sequence read-only (balances) -> tiny test order, exactly as the project intends.

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the Bitso read-only-first gated sequence, but probe the sandbox live (do not trust it), correct the fee framing, and define no-fill handling before arming the loop. Keep the trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted key.
verdicts.assessment: Spine correct and current for a Mexico individual in 2026. Bitso is CNBV-regulated via subsidiary NVIO Pagos Mexico (IFPE, authorized Jan 2020 under Ley Fintech); a Mexican individual can legally register and KYC. Demanded security stance is achievable: granular key scopes allow a TRADE-ONLY no-withdrawal key, plus IP restriction (up to 5 IPs) and write-once secret. Read-only-first sequence is sound; 200 MXN sits under the per-tx cap and clears the per-book minimum_value (~25 MXN) from GET /available_books. NOT HOLDING UP: (1) sandbox does not remove real-money risk entirely - docs still cite dead Ropsten testnet, must be probed live; (2) fee framing understated - BTC/MXN entry tier is maker ~0.5%/taker ~0.65%, ~1.0-1.3% round-trip plus spread at 11 USD; (3) a resting limit order may not fill, breaking deterministic t+1 mechanical fills - unflagged, needs no-fill handling gated on GET /order_trades; (4) silent on Mexican tax - SAT taxes each disposal/swap as ISR (up to 35%), CARF reporting begins 2026, start record-keeping at trade 1. Corrections and gaps, not refutations: platform, KYC, key-scoping, and validation path are real and safe for a tiny first trade if sandbox is probed and no-fill handled.
verdicts.caveats: Could not directly fetch the Bitso help-center fee page (HTTP 403) or run a live probe of api-sandbox.bitso.com; fee figures from official-help-center snippets plus third-party corroboration; Ropsten staleness inferred from docs, not a confirmed outage; btc_mxn minimum_value (~25 MXN) from third-party client/snippet. Owner must run read-only probes himself (sandbox reachability + live GET /available_books and GET /fees) before ordering. Codebase: policy.py (per-tx cap is the lowerable gate), executor.py (where a real BitsoExecutor with no-fill handling must be built).

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the fee-encoding rec, but pin the cost gate to live GET /fees values and correct the "halve fee drag" framing: stablecoin-book routing halves the PER-TRADE rate (0.360% vs 0.780%) only AFTER amortizing a one-time MXN<->USDT conversion leg that is itself charged at the 0.780% MXN-book taker (or an app spread). For a single ~11 USD round trip the net savings are small/negative; the lever pays off across many trades, which is the autonomous-agent case. Keep maker/LIMIT preference. Verdict: do-with-care.
verdicts.assessment: The load-bearing numbers are CORRECT and CURRENT against the primary source. Official bitso.com/fees confirms the entry tier exactly: MXN pairs maker 0.600% / taker 0.780% (under 20,000 MXN volume); USD/USDT/stablecoin pairs maker 0.300% / taker 0.360% (under 1,000 USD volume). The much lower 0.15%/0.19% figures floating around review sites are a different (higher-volume / Alpha pro) tier, NOT what a first-time individual hits, so the rec is right to use the entry numbers. GET /fees is real (api/v3/fees) and returns per-book live maker/taker plus next-tier projections, validating "fetch live rather than hardcode." Security stance is fully achievable: Bitso API keys have independent "Place orders" vs "Make withdrawals" permissions, so a trade-only / no-withdrawal key is possible, and IP allowlisting (up to 5 IPs) is supported. The round-trip drag estimate (~0.72% USD to ~1.56% MXN, taker-taker) is internally consistent. Regulatory/registration: a Mexican individual CAN register and KYC on Bitso (CNBV-supervised under the Fintech Law/Ley Fintech as an ITF); this is not a blocker. The recommendation itself makes NO tax/legal claim, so it carries no unsourced fiscal risk - that is correct scoping. ADVERSARIAL WEAKNESSES: (1) "roughly HALVE fee drag" overstates the single-first-trade case - the MXN->USDT entry leg is itself a 0.780% MXN-book taker (or app spread), so for one tiny 11 USD round trip the conversion overhead can eat or exceed the savings; the halving only materializes when many trades amortize the fixed conversion, which is the agent's steady-state, so the framing is directionally right but should be labeled as amortized, not per-first-trade. (2) The rec says route through "USDC/USDT" - on Bitso the deep MXN-quoted book is USDT/MXN; USDC liquidity/spread in MX is thinner, so pin to USDT specifically and confirm book depth at 11 USD before assuming the 0.360% USD-book rate is reachable. (3) Minor: confirm the taker/maker tier the live key actually returns (Bitso app quick-buy uses an embedded SPREAD, not the maker/taker schedule - only Bitso Alpha order-book orders, which the API uses, get the cited fees). None of these refute the core; they are calibration cautions.
verdicts.caveats: Sources verified against primary: official bitso.com/fees (entry-tier 0.600/0.780 MXN, 0.300/0.360 USD-USDT confirmed verbatim), docs.bitso.com GET /fees (live per-book fees confirmed), Bitso API credential docs (Place-orders independent of Make-withdrawals; IP allowlist up to 5 confirmed). Fix before coding: (a) reword "halve fee drag" to "halve the per-trade rate, amortized across trades after a one-time conversion leg charged at MXN-book taker/spread" - do not promise halving on the single first trade; (b) specify USDT/MXN (not generic USDC/USDT) and verify book depth at ~11 USD; (c) ensure the agent uses Bitso Alpha order-book API (maker/taker schedule) and never the app quick-buy path (hidden spread); (d) maker/LIMIT preference is correct and cuts 0.780->0.600 (MXN) or 0.360->0.300 (USD) but only if orders rest, which a mechanical taker-style fill model may not achieve - reconcile with the Phase-2 t+1 fill assumption. Tax note (not part of the rec, but for the build): Mexican individuals owe ISR on each crypto enajenacion event (gains added to annual income, progressive to 35%); Bitso under Ley Fintech is CNBV-supervised; CARF auto-reporting to SAT phases in 2026. This is FYI for the owner, not a defect in this cost-model rec.

verdicts.recommendation: Choose Bitso, PERSONAL (persona fisica) account, for the Phase-3 platform; do NOT open a Business account or form a company.
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation survives adversarial scrutiny on every load-bearing claim, but two supporting justifications are imprecise and the tax angle is uncited.

WHAT VERIFIED TRUE (2026, individual in Mexico):
1. Bitso has a real order-placement Trading API (POST /orders, limit+market) usable on personal accounts. Bitso docs show no account-type gating for order placement; setup references a standard personal web account (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/place-an-order, /api-overview, /2-generate-your-api-credentials). CONFIRMED.
2. The exact security stance is supported by the platform: API keys have GRANULAR permissions — Place orders / View balances / View account info / Perform security actions / Make withdrawals are independently toggleable, so a TRADE-ONLY, NO-WITHDRAWAL key is creatable. AND Bitso supports an IP allowlist ("Restrict use of API addresses", up to 5 IPs). Both pillars of the stated security stance are real and available on individual accounts. This is the strongest part of the recommendation and it checks out.
3. Crypto trading by an individual in Mexico is legal. Global Legal Insights (2026 MX chapter) confirms it is "entirely legal for the public to own, hold, transact, and exchange cryptoassets." Business account adds KYC for zero benefit at this size — CORRECT.
4. Funding works: SPEI deposit minimum ~100 MXN, free of charge; 200 MXN funds fine.
5. Coinbase is genuinely a weak MX fit: no native MXN/SPEI rail (confirmed by FXEmpire/Bitget 2026 reviews) — so the "Bitso over Coinbase" call is sound.

REFUTATIONS / CORRECTIONS the recommendation should absorb:
A. REGULATORY FRAMING IS LOOSELY STATED. The "why" implies Bitso-the-exchange is itself the CNBV-regulated entity placing it above peers. The reality is more nuanced: it is Bitso's subsidiary NVIO Pagos Mexico that holds the CNBV IFPE (electronic-payment) authorization (granted 2020) — and an IFPE license covers e-money/payments, NOT a bespoke "crypto-exchange" license. Per Global Legal Insights, crypto-exchange activity itself currently faces "no specific licence requirement" in Mexico (it sits in a regulated-AML-but-not-specifically-licensed gray zone). So the conclusion (pick Bitso) is right and Bitso IS the most-regulated/SPEI-native venue, but the phrase "only CNBV-regulated venue with a Trading API" overstates the precision of what CNBV actually licenses. This is a framing downgrade, not a refutation of the choice.
B. COST DRAG IS UNDERSTATED. Bitso's own fees page shows the MXN-market standard low-volume tier at maker 0.600% / taker 0.780% (higher than the 0.36-0.60% figure floating in secondary sources). A market round-trip (buy+sell) at ~11 USD is ~1.4-1.6% in fees alone, before spread. Not a blocker, but the executor must treat fees as ~0.78%/side, use LIMIT (maker) orders where possible, and the cost-aware backtest gate (already adopted in Phase 2) must use the realistic taker number.
C. "$10 MINIMUM" IS A NON-ISSUE / was a false alarm. The order-BOOK minimum (List Available Books) for btc_mxn is ~5 MXN minimum_value, so 200 MXN clears comfortably. The "$10 min" some reviews cite is a fiat-purchase UI floor, not the API order-book floor. No blocker.
D. TAX CLAIM HAS NO PRIMARY SOURCE in the recommendation (its 3 sources are Bitso API docs, a Bitso blog, and an MEXC news page — none authoritative for SAT). Independent secondary sources consistently say SAT treats crypto disposal as enajenacion de bienes under LISR (income tax, progressive 1.92%-35%), and crucially that there can be a 20% provisional withholding on the gross disposal amount — but this needs a SAT/LISR primary citation before being relied upon. At ~11 USD the tax liability is trivially small/likely below any exemption threshold, so it does not block the first trade, but the Phase-3 doc should NOT present tax treatment as settled without a CNBV/SAT/LISR primary source.

NET: The platform/account choice is correct and the security stance (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key) is fully supported by the live platform. Proceed, but (1) tighten the regulatory wording to "Bitso is the most-regulated MXN/SPEI-native venue (subsidiary NVIO holds a CNBV IFPE; crypto-exchange activity itself sits in an AML-regulated gray zone)", (2) budget fees at 0.78%/side taker and prefer limit orders, (3) get a SAT/LISR primary cite before publishing any tax treatment.
verdicts.caveats: Verifications are from Bitso's own docs/fees pages and reputable 2026 legal/exchange reviews, not from a logged-in Bitso account — confirm the exact API-key permission toggles and IP-allowlist UI live during registration (KYC and feature availability can vary by account verification level). The 20% SAT provisional-withholding figure is from secondary sources and MUST be confirmed against LISR/SAT primary text before being treated as fact. Fee tiers can change; re-read bitso.com/fees at execution time. The regulatory gray-zone characterization is current as of 2026 but Mexican Fintech Law secondary provisions on virtual assets remain in flux.

verdicts.recommendation: Registration & KYC prep for a Mexico individual's first ~200 MXN Bitso trade: assemble valid INE (front+back), camera for liveness selfie, CURP, same-name CLABE; enter legal name exactly as printed (both apellidos + accents); no VPN; photograph INE flat/well-lit/all-corners/no-glare. Two corrections: drop the "ideally biometric CURP" steer (use your traditional alphanumeric CURP) and soften "#1 cause" to "a leading documented cause."
verdicts.assessment:
The actionable core is true, current for 2026, and safe. CONFIRMED: (1) INE vigente + selfie/liveness is the real Bitso flow (Bitso official blog confirms "a selfie to confirm identity"; verification typically under 5 min). (2) Exact-name entry incl. both surnames + accents is correct — finantres explicitly says "respetalo tal cual" and warns that abbreviating "Jose Luis" to "Jose" causes a problem; "Nombre no coincide" is the first-listed rejection reason. (3) Flat/well-lit/all-corners photo guidance maps directly to documented rejection causes "Foto borrosa, Documento recortado." (4) No-VPN is correct — VPN/IP-country mismatch raises Bitso's risk score and can trigger rejection. (5) Same-name CLABE / third-party SPEI rejection is real — Bitso does not permit third-party transfers; sender and receiver first+last name must match. (6) CURP-vs-RENAPO mismatch is a genuine failure mode (Bitso's own help article attributes CURP validation failures to the RENAPO registry).

TWO REFUTATIONS, neither fatal: (a) "ideally the 2026 biometric version" of CURP is MISLEADING and works against the same-day-pass goal. Biometric CURP is VOLUNTARY in 2026; the traditional alphanumeric CURP (126M holders) remains valid and is exactly what Bitso validates against RENAPO. No source shows Bitso requires or prefers the biometric version. Steering a first-timer to obtain it risks an unnecessary government-office errand that DELAYS registration. Correct to: a valid traditional CURP whose data exactly matches the INE is sufficient. (b) The "#1 avoidable first-attempt KYC rejection" RANKING is NOT supported by the cited didit.me page (it documents the validation capability, not rejection statistics). Name mismatch is demonstrably A leading, well-documented cause (finantres lists it first), so the direction is right but "#1" is an unsourced embellishment.

REGULATORY/TAX/SECURITY/COST: No problems created. Bitso is CNBV-regulated under the Ley Fintech. At ~200 MXN the user is far below every SAT/CARF reporting threshold (200,000 MXN balance / 50,000 USD tx) and the ISR-free annual capital-gains allowance (~128,383 MXN = 3x annual UMA for 2026), so the tiny trade creates no tax liability or reporting trigger. This step is owner-action PREP only — no API keys, no funded wallet, nothing live — so it is fully upstream of and compatible with the trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted key stance the policy engine relies on.
verdicts.caveats: Fix before acting: (1) Drop "ideally the 2026 biometric version" — use your existing traditional CURP; only its data matching your INE matters. Do NOT make a government-office trip for biometric CURP just to register; that contradicts the same-day-pass goal. (2) Treat "#1 cause" as "a leading documented cause" — the cited didit.me link does not substantiate a ranking. (3) The strongest same-name-SPEI confirmation came from search corroboration and a finance blog; the official Bitso help page returned 403/302 (paywalled), so verify the same-name CLABE rule live in-app at funding time before sending SPEI. (4) Tax thresholds (200,000 MXN / 128,383 MXN ISR-free) are 2026 figures from secondary guides citing SAT/Ley Fintech, not pulled from a primary SAT/CNBV page in this pass — they only matter far above this trade size, so they do not gate the action, but confirm against SAT before any materially larger position.

verdicts.recommendation: Enable authenticator-app (TOTP) 2FA before creating any Bitso API key; save the one-time emergency restore code offline and seed the TOTP secret into a backup authenticator; store the API secret immediately (shown once). Verdict: do-now.
verdicts.assessment: Adversarial check confirms the core claims against Bitso's own documentation (2026). (1) 2FA is a HARD prerequisite to API key creation: Bitso docs state "To generate API credentials, you must enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)" — without it you cannot reach key creation. (2) The supported API 2FA method is an authenticator app (TOTP); Bitso docs name Authy / Google Authenticator and a 30s rotating token. SMS is NOT an API-gating method, and Bitso explicitly still allows SMS for account-login 2FA ("through SMS or your authenticator of choice"), so the "do not leave SMS as primary / SIM-swap" warning is apt and correctly scoped. (3) The emergency restore code is real — Bitso shows it once during 2FA setup, validating the "save offline" advice. (4) Critically for the project's blast-radius stance, permissions are INDEPENDENT toggles (Place orders / View balances / View account information / Make withdrawals), so a TRADE-ONLY, NO-withdrawal key is genuinely supported; IP allowlisting (up to 5 addresses) also exists. The recommendation makes NO tax/legal claims, so no primary-source (CNBV/SAT) downgrade is triggered. Minor imprecision: "gate that unlocks the granular permission UI" is loose — permission checkboxes render after clicking Add-new-key and the 2FA token is entered at final confirmation — but the practical chain (no 2FA -> no key -> no permissions) holds. Action is owner-only, reversible, zero-cost, and prerequisite to the safe key-scoping the policy engine depends on. It REDUCES security risk and introduces no regulatory, tax, or cost problem. Cited sources (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials and the 2FA support article) are authoritative and current.
verdicts.caveats: Two imprecisions, neither fatal: (a) "gate that unlocks the granular permission UI" overstates the flow — 2FA is a prerequisite to key creation; permission selection appears after clicking Add-new-key and the token is entered at confirmation; reword to "2FA is mandatory before any key can be created." (b) Bitso still permits SMS for login 2FA, so the user must affirmatively switch login 2FA to TOTP, not assume it is already TOTP-only. Downstream Phase-3 items NOT covered by this rec but to flag before the live trade: Bitso minimum trade size is ~$10 USD, so a ~$11 (200 MXN) order sits at the floor; MXN-pair fees (e.g., BTC/MXN ~0.50% maker / ~0.65% taker) far exceed the 0.15%/0.19% headline, making round-trip spread+fee drag material at this size. Also re-verify exact permission labels and the IP-allowlist field against the live UI at registration time, since exchange UIs drift.

verdicts.recommendation: {"recommendation":"Create a TRADE-ONLY API key: enable ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account information' for read validation); leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' OFF. Enable 'Restrict use of API addresses' and add the executor host's STATIC egress IP (prefer the fixed-IP Netcup VPS over a dynamic residential IP). Optionally create a second, read-only key for monitoring/reconciliation.","theme":"Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)"}
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED CORRECT and current for 2026. Refutation attempt failed on the substance; only minor wording/citation issues found.

WHAT HELD UP (official sources):
1. Exact permission names match docs.bitso.com verbatim: 'Place orders', 'View balances', 'View account information', 'Make withdrawals', 'Perform security actions'. The trade-only subset is exactly right; withdrawals-off means a leaked key cannot exfiltrate funds (correct blast-radius logic).
2. 'Restrict use of API addresses' IP allowlist exists (up to 5 IPs) - confirmed in official docs.
3. Bitso DOES publish least-privilege + 2FA + key-regeneration guidance (blog.bitso.com 'API Key Security Tips'), so the security stance aligns with Bitso's own recommendations.
4. LEGAL (primary/authoritative): Individuals in Mexico may freely trade small crypto amounts; no licensing or amount restriction on individuals (only VASPs face AML obligations). Bitso operates lawfully under Ley Fintech. Crypto is NOT legal tender (Art. 30 Fintech Law) but trading is not prohibited. Source: Global Legal Insights Mexico 2026 + Banxico Circular 4/2019. Nothing blocks a Mexico-based individual.
5. The fixed-IP VPS choice is sound: a dynamic/CGNAT residential IP would silently break the allowlist, and a wrong IP fails CLOSED (requests rejected) - the safe failure mode.

WHERE I COULD WEAKEN IT (do not block, but fix):
1. CITATION GAP: the 2nd cited source (support.bitso.com/.../4415097148948) returned HTTP 403 - could not verify it is live/current. Substance is corroborated by docs.bitso.com + blog.bitso.com, so swap that citation for https://blog.bitso.com/blog/how-keep-api-key-safely (official API key security tips) and https://docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials.
2. OVERSTATEMENT: 'officially recommended by Bitso' is too strong for IP-allowlisting specifically - docs frame it as OPTIONAL ('If you want the API to only validate requests...'). Bitso explicitly recommends least-privilege + 2FA; IP-allowlist is supported/offered, not mandated. Soften to 'natively supported, consistent with Bitso's least-privilege guidance.'
3. SURROUNDING-PLAN CAVEATS (this KYC rec is fine, but Phase-3 must carry these): (a) Bitso minimum order is ~$10 USD, so 200 MXN (~$11) sits BARELY above the floor - a 'tiny test order' below ~$10 will be REJECTED; size the validation order accordingly. (b) Cost drag: MXN-market taker 0.65% / maker 0.5% + spread => ~1.5-2%+ round-trip on 200 MXN; fine for a one-time validation, not for frequent rebalancing at this size. (c) TAX (downgrade per primary-source demand): every sell/swap is an ISR disposal (enajenacion de bienes, LISR Title IV Ch IV); the ~128k MXN annual individual gains exemption means a one-time tiny trade owes ~0 ISR; BUT IVA on crypto disposal is genuinely UNSETTLED (some firms read crypto->MXN as in-principle VATable on an intangible; SAT has not enforced on retail). These are tax-firm secondary reads, NOT a primary SAT ruling - so the plan must say 'treat sells as ISR disposal events; consult SAT/contador' and must NOT assert 'no IVA' as settled fact.","caveats":"Sources verified: docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials (permission names + IP allowlist, official); blog.bitso.com/blog/how-keep-api-key-safely (Bitso security guidance, official); bitso.com/fees + support.bitso.com Maker-Taker article (0.5%/0.65% MXN, $10 min order); globallegalinsights.com Mexico 2026 + Banxico Circular 4/2019 + Art 30 Ley Fintech (individual trading legal, crypto not legal tender). UNVERIFIED: support.bitso.com/.../4415097148948 (HTTP 403 - swap citation). TAX SOURCES ARE SECONDARY (tax-firm blogs, not a SAT primary ruling) - downgrade any IVA/ISR assertion to 'consult SAT/contador'; IVA-on-crypto-disposal is unsettled, do not claim 'no IVA' as fact. Verify the VPS EGRESS IP is the static one (not the inbound tunnel IP) and confirm Netcup does not rotate it before relying on the allowlist."

verdicts.recommendation: Theme: Build the live Bitso Executor as staged fail-closed steps (sandbox HMAC -> read-only account_status/balance + no-withdraw self-check -> read per-book minimums -> one ~200 MXN order behind the policy kill-switch -> read back the fill).
verdicts.assessment:
The shape of the plan is right (staged, fail-closed, read-only before tiny order, policy-gated) and the platform choice (Bitso, a CNBV-supervised Mexican ITF) is correct for a Mexican individual at ~200 MXN. KYC/registration as an individual is real and unblocked. But several load-bearing specifics are wrong or stale and break a literal implementation:

1. NONCE IS WRONG / WILL BE REJECTED. The recommendation hardcodes "a strictly increasing Unix-timestamp nonce." Bitso deprecated Nonce v1 in November 2025; production now requires Nonce v2 = a 13-digit millisecond epoch PLUS a 1-6 digit random salt (16-19 digits total). A seconds-resolution Unix nonce as written will be rejected by prod. This is concrete and current (Bitso changelog 2025-03-14 "Nonce v2"; docs "Nonce v2 Rollout"). Must build to Nonce v2.

2. SANDBOX IS UNVERIFIED / LIKELY STALE. The sandbox (api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3) is still documented, but the docs still tell you to fund it with "Ethereum's Ropsten Testnet" — Ropsten was shut down in 2022. The changelog (actively maintained through 2026-05) has ZERO sandbox entries since 2020. So step (1) "prove HMAC works in the sandbox" may fail or test a divergent surface. Treat the sandbox as unverified: probe it live before depending on it; do not let a green sandbox imply prod-readiness, and have a fallback (sign-test against a prod read-only GET) if the sandbox is dead.

3. THE NO-WITHDRAW SELF-CHECK IS A SECURITY ANTI-PATTERN AND PROVES LESS THAN CLAIMED. Step (2) proposes "attempt a withdrawal-scoped call, require it to be denied." Issuing a live withdrawal request to prove it fails is dangerous: if the key is misconfigured WITH the "Make withdrawals" permission AND Bitso's address whitelist is empty (Bitso default: empty whitelist = withdraw to ANY address), the call could SUCCEED and move funds. The correct fail-closed check is to (a) create the key with only "Place orders" + "View balances"/"View account information" and NEVER "Make withdrawals", (b) verify permission scope by reading key metadata / catching the permission-denied error WITHOUT a real destination+amount, and (c) if you must probe, send a withdrawal with a deliberately invalid/zero target so it cannot execute. Do not "test" withdrawal by firing a real one.

4. IP-ALLOWLIST IS OPTIONAL, NOT ENFORCED, AND THE OWNER IS ON A RESIDENTIAL IP. Bitso's "Restrict use of API addresses" supports "from none up to five addresses" — it is optional, so the security stance only holds if the owner actively configures it. On a dynamic residential MX IP (per memory, this builder runs services from home), an allowlisted IP can rotate and silently lock the key out mid-run; plan for that (re-allowlist flow, or pin via the VPS already in the fleet).

5. $10 MINIMUM CROWDS THE FLOOR. Bitso min trade is $10 across pairs and ~$11 is right at it. Step (3) reading per-book minimum_value is correct, but step (4) "place ONE order near full 200 MXN" leaves almost no headroom for the policy engine's per-tx notional cap or rounding, and after a buy fee you hold <200 MXN — a later SELL of that position can fall under the $10 floor and be rejected on exit. Size with explicit floor margin, not "near full."

6. COST DRAG IS NON-TRIVIAL AT THIS SIZE ON MXN PAIRS. Entry-tier MXN taker fee is 0.65% (vs 0.05% USD). A buy+sell round trip is ~1.3% + spread on 200 MXN (~2.6-3 MXN of pure friction). Fine for a one-shot validation, but this is a validation/plumbing exercise, not a profitable trade — the recommendation should say so.

7. TAX/LEGAL CLAIMS LACK PRIMARY SOURCES (the recommendation cites none for tax). Crypto = activo virtual under Ley Fintech art. 30 (primary: LRITF / diputados.gob.mx); Banxico Circular 4/2019 (mod. 37/2020) bars banks/ITFs from offering crypto to the public except authorized internal ops — does NOT block an individual trading on Bitso. SAT treats disposal as enajenacion de bienes (LISR); a one-shot 200 MXN trade has negligible tax. But the specific rate/exemption figures circulating are from secondary blogs (beincrypto, MEXC, mexc), NOT SAT/CNBV primary text — downgrade and verify against SAT before relying on any threshold. Also note CARF data-sharing begins 2026 (platforms report to SAT), so assume reportability even at small size.</assessment>
<parameter name="caveats">Fee/fiscal figures (0.65% MXN taker, $10 min, ISR thresholds) come from Bitso help pages and secondary tax blogs, not always primary docs — re-confirm live, especially anything tax-rate-specific against SAT directly. The sandbox status is inferred from stale docs + an empty changelog, not from a live probe; a quick signed GET against api-sandbox would settle it. Bitso's exact API-key permission names and whether key metadata exposes scope read-only should be confirmed in the live key-creation UI.</caveats>
<parameter name="verdict">verify-live

verdicts.recommendation: do-with-care: The recommendation's facts hold for a Mexico individual in 2026 and the security stance is fully implementable, but proceed only after live-verifying the minimum order size and using maker/limit (not market/taker) orders to control the ~1.5%+ round-trip fee drag at 11 USD size. Fund via ONE same-name SPEI into the in-app NVIO/CLABE; treat the funded amount as the hard solvency ceiling.
verdicts.assessment:
The factual claims are TRUE and CURRENT for a Mexico-based individual in 2026, confirmed against Bitso's own help center.

CONFIRMED:
- One account per CURP: Bitso states "only one active account is allowed per customer," cites Terms of Use clause 14; duplicate-CURP registration is blocked (help.bitso.com/.../11000093667).
- Third-party funding rejected / same-name required: Bitso states it does not accept third-party transfers; both accounts must match first and last name. Matches the recommendation exactly.
- In-app CLABE via NVIO Pagos Mexico, a CNBV-authorized IFPE — genuinely regulated in MX; leaving the tiny balance on-exchange is reasonable given the regulated wrapper.
- Sub-accounts Business-only; single small balance as blast-radius cap is sound and well-sourced.
- SECURITY STANCE IS IMPLEMENTABLE (strong positive): Bitso API keys offer separate permission toggles (View balances, Place orders, Make withdrawals, Perform security actions) PLUS IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs) and withdrawal-address allowlisting. Trade-only + no-withdrawal + IP-allowlisted is exactly buildable (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). NOTE: a residential MX IP is often dynamic — IP allowlisting may break the key on IP rotation; plan for that.

GAPS (why do-with-care, not do-now):
1. COST DRAG UNMENTIONED + UNDERSTATED. Bitso MXN-market fee at this user's tier (<20,000 MXN/30d) is 0.600% maker / 0.780% taker PER SIDE (bitso.com/fees), NOT the 0.15/0.19% headline. Taker round-trip ~1.5%+ before spread. Use limit/maker orders.
2. MINIMUM-ORDER-SIZE RISK (the real potential blocker). Sources conflict: $1 USD vs $10 USD min trade. At only 11 USD capital this is decisive — if the floor is ~$10 the read-only -> tiny-test-order -> real-order sequence is barely feasible. Verify live before funding.
3. "Near-instant" SPEI is slightly optimistic: Bitso documents crediting "within 24 hours."
4. TAX: enajenacion-de-bienes/ISR treatment (progressive 1.92-35%, ~128,383 MXN 2026 exemption, crypto-to-crypto = taxable disposal, CARF reporting from Jan 2026) is consistent across sources and the legal frame (Fintech Law Art. 30) is real, but no PRIMARY SAT/CNBV ruling URL was retrievable — treat tax specifics as directionally correct, confirm with SAT/a contador.
verdicts.caveats: No primary SAT/CNBV document obtained for tax treatment (only secondary tax guides + the genuine Fintech Law Art. 30 frame) — confirm tax with SAT or a contador. Live source conflict on minimum trade size ($1 vs $10 USD) materially affects feasibility at 11 USD and must be checked in-app before funding. Bitso support pages 403/redirect on direct fetch; CURP and same-name claims confirmed via help-center search snippets rather than a clean page fetch. Residential MX IP is typically dynamic, which can break IP-allowlisted keys.

verdicts.recommendation: do-with-care
verdicts.assessment:
The core recommendation survives adversarial scrutiny and is current for 2026, but it has one real security omission and understates cost.

WHAT HOLDS UP (verified against primary/official sources):
1. API permission model is EXACTLY as stated. Bitso's official docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials) confirm the five permissions: Place orders, View balances, View account information, Perform security actions, Make withdrawals. 2FA is mandatory to create a key. The "Perform security actions" trap is real and correctly described: it "enables modifying account settings, including deactivating your account or blocking withdrawals." Leaving it OFF is correct and the reasoning is sound. The least-privilege shape (Place orders + View balances only) matches what the policy engine assumes.
2. Bitso as the MX-individual platform is correct for 2026. It is Mexico-born, CNBV-registered (its NVIO subsidiary holds an IFPE authorization), MXN/SPEI-native, and dedicated crypto exchanges remain legal for individuals (the 2026 "gray zone" restrictions apply to banks/fintechs, not to dedicated exchanges). No KYC blocker found for a resident individual.
3. SPEI deposits/withdrawals are fee-free; KYC + SPEI funding path is real and low-friction.

GAPS / REFUTATIONS (why not do-now):
1. SECURITY OMISSION — IP allowlisting is dropped from the action steps. The Phase-3 security stance explicitly requires "IP-allowlisted" keys, and Bitso DOES support it ("Restrict use of API addresses," up to 5 IPs, confirmed in official docs). The recommendation's action steps only mention the permission checkboxes and never tell the owner to enable IP restriction. This is a real blast-radius gap that contradicts the stated security stance. Caveat: a residential MX IP is typically dynamic, so the owner must use a static-IP egress (VPS/Cloudflare tunnel egress) or be prepared to re-allowlist on IP change — a known operational wrinkle, not a blocker.
2. COST UNDERSTATED — Phase-3 explicitly asked for "fees/spread drag at ~11 USD size," and the recommendation is silent on it. The relevant fee for an MXN-funded trade is the MXN-market tier, NOT the headline 0.15%/0.19%. Bitso's official fee page shows the entry tier (<20,000 MXN volume) MXN maker/taker = 0.600%/0.780%. A market-order round trip is ~1.56% in fees alone (before bid/ask spread), i.e. ~3 MXN per side on a 200 MXN trade. Acceptable for a one-time validation trade, but materially erosive for a repeated autonomous strategy — worth surfacing now.

TAX/LEGAL (primary source demanded): Plausible and well-grounded but not from a single primary citation in this recommendation. Secondary/professional sources consistently anchor to LISR Art. 141 (enajenacion de bienes) and the Art. 93 fr. XIX movable-property exemption (3 UMA), with the Fintech Law Art. 30 classifying crypto as a virtual asset. Bitso issues a CFDI for its service but does NOT withhold ISR on gains — the individual self-declares (annual, by Apr 30, in Anexo of the annual return). New for 2026: OECD CARF reporting means exchanges report balances/operations to SAT. At 200 MXN with realistic gains far under the ~128,383 MXN (3 UMA) annual exemption, no tax is practically owed on a first trade, but the declaration/record-keeping obligation still exists. The recommendation makes no tax claim, so nothing to downgrade there — but the project's tax sub-question should cite LISR Art. 141 / Art. 93-XIX directly rather than rely on blog summaries.

NET: The recommendation is true, current, and safe for a first tiny real trade. It is not wrong — it is incomplete on two points the Phase-3 brief explicitly demanded (IP allowlist, fee/spread drag).</assessment>
<parameter name="caveats">1) Action steps MUST add: enable "Restrict use of API addresses" (IP allowlist) on the key — the stated security stance requires it and Bitso supports it; omitting it leaves a blast-radius gap. Residential MX IP is dynamic, so route the executor through a static egress or plan to re-allowlist. 2) Real cost at ~11 USD is the MXN-market tier 0.60%/0.78% (entry), ~1.56% round-trip before spread — fine for one validation trade, erosive for repeated automated trades; size this into the backtest cost gate. 3) Confirm current minimum order size live (sources conflict $10 vs $100; ~200 MXN may be near the floor for some pairs) as part of the read-only -> tiny-order validation sequence. 4) Tax: practically no ISR owed at this size (well under 3-UMA exemption) but a declaration/record obligation exists and 2026 CARF means SAT gets exchange-side reporting; cite LISR Art.141 / Art.93-XIX as primary, not blogs. 5) Custody at 11 USD: leaving funds on Bitso (exchange custody) is the pragmatic choice; no self-custody needed at this size, but it is counterparty risk to acknowledge.</caveats>
</invoke>

Gate the live path behind the kill-switch + per-tx notional cap.","theme":"API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)"}
verdicts.assessment: CORE CLAIMS VERIFIED CURRENT (2026) for a Bitso MX individual. Confirmed against Bitso docs: (a) granular permissions exist as separate toggles — 'View balances', 'Place orders' (includes retrieving trades + open orders), and a SEPARATE 'Make withdrawals' — so a trade-only / no-withdrawal key is genuinely creatable; (b) IP allowlist exists (up to 5 IPs); (c) signing is HMAC-SHA256 over nonce+HTTP method+request path+JSON payload, header 'Bitso key:nonce:signature' — exactly matching the rationale that a signed balance call first catches byte-exact path/payload + nonce errors before money moves; (d) btc_mxn minimum_value = 5 MXN, so a 200 MXN order clears the per-book minimum with ~40x headroom and will NOT be rejected for size. The read-only -> tiny-order ramp + per-tx cap blast-radius logic is sound and the security stance (trade-only, no-withdraw, IP-allowlisted) is correct.\n\nREFUTATIONS / CORRECTIONS: (1) FEE FIGURE: MXN pairs (btc_mxn) are 0.50% maker / 0.65% taker per Bitso's own fees doc, NOT the 0.15%/0.19% headline (that is the USD/BTC-denominated markets). Round-trip on a MXN pair is ~1.0-1.15% + spread; at 11 USD that is ~0.11-0.13 USD/round-trip plus a typically wide MXN-pair spread. Tolerable for one validation, but ~3x the naive reading — the executor CostModel must use the real MXN-pair fees. (2) LIMIT != GUARANTEED MAKER: a limit order priced through the book fills as TAKER (0.65%); guaranteeing maker requires post-only inside the spread, which may rest UNFILLED and defeat 'verify the fill'. The recommendation conflates 'place a limit order' with 'deterministic fill at maker fee' — pick marketable-limit (taker, fills) OR post-only (maker, may not fill). (3) IP-ALLOWLIST + RESIDENTIAL DYNAMIC IP: this user is on a residential MX IP that has already caused silent failures elsewhere in his stack; an allowlisted home IP that rotates produces silent 401s. Pin egress via the existing VPS 152.53.167.28 or a static IP, not the drifting home IP — a concrete MX-individual trap the recommendation omits. (4) TAX/LEGAL: DOWNGRADED — only tax-software blogs (bleap/mexc/beincrypto/waltio), no primary SAT/LISR/CNBV citation. The recommendation itself makes no tax claim (sources are only the two Bitso API docs) so it does not over-claim; at 200 MXN single-trade scale realized gain is negligible and tax is not a first-trade blocker, but MXN cost-basis tracking from trade #1 is a real obligation. (5) PHASE-2 DRIFT: Phase-2 concluded the live rung is Kraken paper -> Coinbase AgentKit/Base testnet; this silently pivots to Bitso/CEX-MXN. The security/blast-radius core is correct and current."}

verdicts.recommendation: Fix the executor fee model: SimExecutor defaults to fee_rate=0.001 (0.1%), but real Bitso MXN order-book fees are 0.600% maker / 0.780% taker per side (tier 0). Make the BitsoExecutor and the cost-aware backtest gate (Deflated Sharpe + purged CV) model >=0.78% taker per side (1.56% taker round-trip; 1.2% maker round-trip), and prefer maker/limit orders.
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED against the primary live source bitso.com/fees: the lowest-volume tier for MXN markets (e.g. BTC/MXN) is exactly 0.600% maker / 0.780% taker per side, matching the recommendation verbatim. The competing "0.50%/0.65%" figures seen in Bitso's API docs example and some review sites are stale/example values, not the current live tier-0 schedule, so they do not refute the claim. The code claim is also true: C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\executor.py line 25 sets fee_rate: float = 0.001, i.e. 0.1% — roughly 7.8x below the real taker fee, consistent with the recommendation's "6-8x" framing. The round-trip math (1.2% maker / 1.56% taker) is correct.

Adversarial pressure-test on each axis:
(1) Safety for a first tiny trade: This change is strictly safer. Raising the modeled fee only makes the cost-aware gate MORE conservative; it cannot greenlight a losing strategy. The risk it prevents (a strategy passing the Deflated-Sharpe/purged-CV gate at a fictitious 0.1% then bleeding out live at 0.78%/side) is real and material at ~11 USD.
(2) Order-size blocker: NONE. Bitso btc_mxn minimum value is 25 MXN, so a 200 MXN first trade clears the minimum comfortably (also leaves room for fractional round-trip sells above 25 MXN).
(3) Security stance: NOT undermined. Independently confirmed that Bitso API keys support granular permissions (Trade without Withdraw) plus an IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs), so the trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted blast-radius stance is implementable on the chosen platform. This change touches only a fee constant and the cost model — no keys, custody, KYC, or withdrawal surface.
(4) Tax/legal: The recommendation makes NO tax/legal claim, so there is nothing to downgrade on that axis. (Separately, the MX SAT/CNBV treatment of this trade is a real open question for Phase 3, but it is out of scope for THIS fee recommendation.)

If anything the recommendation UNDER-states total drag: SimExecutor also models only 5 bps (0.05%) adverse slippage per side, which is optimistic for an ~11 USD order on a potentially thin MXN book where spread can dominate. So the fix is directionally conservative and safe to adopt as-is.
verdicts.caveats: "Prefer maker/limit orders" is correct as a fee strategy (0.60% vs 0.78%), but a limit order can fail to fill — which breaks the Phase-2 point-in-time t+1-fill assumption the backtest is built on. Do not model the 0.18% maker discount as free; model it as fill-uncertainty, or keep the gate on the 0.78% taker assumption (conservative) and treat any maker fill as upside. Also: do NOT let the cheaper BTC-denominated books (~0.075%/0.098%) tempt the model — the FIRST MXN->crypto trade must use the MXN order book (the 0.60%/0.78% tier); the cheap BTC-quoted pairs require already holding BTC and are irrelevant to the first fiat entry. Finally, surface to the owner that a 1.2-1.56% round-trip hurdle plus spread on an ~11 USD ticket demands a large per-trade edge for a mechanical strategy to be net-positive — a viability flag, not a reason to reject the fee fix. Fee tier is volume-based and could change; re-read bitso.com/fees at go-live and ideally pull GET /fees per book at runtime rather than hardcoding.

verdicts.recommendation: Add TWO policy rules to contain trade-only key residual risk: (a) restrict the pair allowlist to liquid MXN majors (btc_mxn, eth_mxn, usdt_mxn); (b) a price-deviation sanity guard rejecting orders whose limit price deviates >X% from a fresh reference mid. SHIP (a) now as a tightening of existing rule-4; GATE (b) to ship together with the limit-order executor (it is inert today); and drop the fabricated $65M citation, replacing it with the real 3Commas (~$22M, 2022) and Viacoin (2018) precedents.
verdicts.assessment:
CORE CLAIM TRUE, EVIDENCE FABRICATED. The threat is real and well-documented: a leaked trade-only/no-withdrawal key can drain a balance by contra-trading liquid holdings (BTC/ETH/USDT) into illiquid altcoin books at a markup. Canonical case = 3Commas leak (Dec 2022, ~$22M), the exact mechanism; also Binance/Viacoin (Mar 2018, ~70x). All three cited sources corroborate the MECHANISM. HOWEVER the load-bearing statistic '>$65M stolen this way Dec 2024-Jan 2025' appears in NONE of its three citations: hackernoon cites Viacoin 2018, vantixs cites no figure and never mentions illiquid pairs, apisecurity.io cites 'over a million dollars.' The $65M/2024-25 figure is unsupported/fabricated; real precedent is ~$22M (2022).

PLATFORM FIT (Bitso, the real MX path) CONFIRMED: granular permissions (place-orders separate from withdrawals), IP allowlist up to 5 IPs, withdrawal-address allowlisting; Bitso supports limit AND market orders plus a sandbox (api-sandbox.bitso.com). Trade-only/no-withdraw/IP-allowlist stance is sound and platform-supported; a liquid-pair allowlist genuinely removes the destination books the attack needs.

TWO REFUTATIONS ON THE PROPOSED RULES: (1) Rule (b) is currently INERT/architecturally mismatched: the Intent dataclass (types.py:27-42) has no price field; intents are market-style base_amount quantities evaluated against tick.price. There is no limit price to clamp until the executor places LIMIT orders. As written (b) is a no-op against today's architecture and only becomes meaningful bundled with a limit-order executor. (2) Rule (a) is a TIGHTENING of the EXISTING rule-4 symbol allowlist, not a new rule; and at the $11-$200 first-trade size the EXISTING per-tx notional cap (rule 5), spend budget (rule 7), and solvency (rule 8) already hard-bound blast radius to the tiny balance, so the 'high impact / first trade' framing is overstated. The mitigations are correct for 'as size grows' (recommendation says so) but are not load-bearing for the first tiny trade.

No tax/legal claim is made by THIS recommendation, so no primary-source downgrade applies to it. Relevant files: C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\policy.py (rule 4 allowlist at lines 104-106; caps at 108-133), C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\types.py (Intent has no price field, 27-42), C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\executor.py (SimExecutor, swap point).
verdicts.caveats: Drop the $65M Dec 2024-Jan 2025 claim before reusing this justification; it is in none of the cited sources and looks fabricated. Replace with verifiable 3Commas (~$22M, 2022) and Viacoin (2018) precedents. Rule (b) must NOT ship before the executor places limit orders or it silently does nothing; confirm Bitso place_order limit-price params and crypto-to-crypto book availability in the live API/sandbox before wiring it. Any tax reliance (progressive ISR 1.92-35%, ~60k MXN / 3x-UMA exemption, SAT real-time access from Apr 2026) needs SAT/CNBV primary sources, not the secondary blogs surfaced here.

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the recommendation as written, with one edit: change "IP-allowlisted" from an assumed always-on control to a conditional ("enable Bitso IP restriction only with a stable egress IP; otherwise rely on trade-only/no-withdrawal scope plus the policy engine's notional/budget caps"). Everything else (keyring/DPAPI per-user storage, never env/log/journal, discard-after-sign, strengthen Windows password + BitLocker, treat key scope as the real blast-radius cap) is verified, current, and safe for the first ~11 USD trade.
verdicts.assessment: VERIFIED and current for a 2026 MX individual. (1) Bitso trade-only, no-withdrawal key is REAL: official docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials) confirm five granular permissions; enable "Place orders" + "View balances", leave "Make withdrawals" OFF -> no fund-transfer capability via that key. This validates the core security stance and the blast-radius argument. (2) keyring/DPAPI claim is TECHNICALLY ACCURATE: keyring's WinVaultKeyring uses win32cred -> Windows Credential Manager, encrypted at rest by DPAPI, scoped to the user SID (confirmed in jaraco/keyring backends/Windows.py). The "DPAPI does NOT stop same-user malware" caveat is correct and important. (3) Both cited sources are real and credible (the keyring GitHub file exists; comcomponent.com/KomuraSoft article is a genuine, non-SEO technical piece that correctly enumerates DPAPI's same-user/in-memory limits). ONE FACTUAL CORRECTION: the recommendation's "IP-allowlisted" framing implies an always-on control, but Bitso's official docs say IP restriction ("Restrict use of API addresses") is OPTIONAL, not mandatory (one search snippet wrongly called it mandatory). On a residential MX connection with a dynamic ISP IP (a recurring pain point in this owner's history), an IP allowlist can silently lock the bot out when the IP rotates -- so it is good advice but operationally brittle and must be paired with a known-static egress or quick re-whitelist step. CONTEXT (not a refutation, since the rec is about key storage not tax/regulation): Mexican tax treatment is crypto-as-enajenacion-de-bienes (LISR Art. 141/Cap. IV; 3x-UMA-annual exemption under Art. 93 frac. XIX) -- at ~200 MXN any realized gain is sub-peso and far below any threshold, so no practical tax/cost problem on the first trade; the specific peso figure and 20% provisional rate came from secondary tax-blog sources, not a primary SAT bulletin, so do not hard-code them. Regulatory nuance to know: Bitso's CRYPTO activity is NOT CNBV-regulated as such -- it runs under the LFPIORPI "actividad vulnerable" AML regime; only its NVIO subsidiary holds an IFPE/CNBV e-money license. Banxico never authorized crypto for fintechs. This does not block an individual but means crypto held at Bitso lacks the regulatory protection one might assume -- reinforcing keeping custody tiny at this stage.
verdicts.caveats: 1) FIX the IP-allowlist framing: Bitso IP restriction is OPTIONAL not mandatory; enable it only if you have a stable egress IP, otherwise a dynamic residential IP rotation will lock out the bot -- have a fast re-whitelist path or skip it and lean on trade-only scope + low notional caps. 2) keyring needs pywin32 (not pure stdlib) -- already an accepted Phase-2 concession; keep it quarantined behind the SecretStore adapter. 3) DPAPI does not protect against same-user malware or an admin/full-machine compromise, nor the plaintext-in-memory window -- correctly stated; the trade-only/no-withdraw scope remains the actual cap. 4) Do not hard-code MX tax numbers (128,383 MXN / 20% provisional) from secondary blogs; they are immaterial at ~200 MXN but cite a primary SAT/LISR source before relying on them later. 5) Know that Bitso crypto custody is under the AML vulnerable-activity regime, not CNBV crypto regulation -- keep custody minimal at this size.

verdicts.recommendation: Implement BitsoExecutor signing in pure stdlib (hmac+hashlib): sig = hex(HMAC-SHA256(secret, nonce+method+request_path+payload)), Authorization = 'Bitso {key}:{nonce}:{sig}', nonce = strictly-increasing epoch-ms. Add a golden-vector contract test, a strictly-monotonic cross-process nonce, and a server-time clock-skew guard.
verdicts.assessment:
Core is verified against Bitso's primary docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/create-signed-requests and /understand-bitsos-auth-mechanism). CONFIRMED TRUE and current for 2026: (1) signature = hex(HMAC-SHA256(secret, nonce+method+request_path+payload)), no '+' separators; (2) Authorization header format 'Bitso key:nonce:signature' is byte-exact correct; (3) pure stdlib hmac+hashlib suffices — no JWT/Ed25519 unlike Coinbase Advanced Trade, so the stdlib-only constraint holds; (4) Bitso supports the exact security stance — granular Trade-only permission with Withdraw OFF, IP allowlist up to 5 IPs, and per-asset withdrawal-address restriction; (5) Bitso is a CNBV-registered ITF under the Ley Fintech, open to MX individuals self-registering with KYC, min trade ~1-10 USD, so the ~11 USD first trade is feasible. The recommendation's diagnosis of the top silent-401 causes (byte-exact prehash mismatch + nonce reuse) is correct and the monotonic non-repeating nonce is the genuinely load-bearing safeguard.

REFUTATIONS / OVERSTATEMENTS: (a) 'golden-vector contract test reproducing Bitso's documented example' is partially false — Bitso publishes NO concrete numeric golden vector (no fixed key/secret/nonce -> expected-hash tuple); both auth pages contain only templated placeholders plus a Bash/openssl snippet with variables. You can and should build a golden-vector test, but with your OWN fixed fixture cross-checked against the openssl one-liner, not against a nonexistent Bitso-published hash. (b) nonce='epoch-ms' and 'server-time clock-skew guard' are over-specified: docs require only a unique strictly-increasing integer (Unix timestamp recommended) and document NO clock-skew tolerance window; the ms precision and skew guard are the recommender's defensive inventions, fine to keep but not 'documented' failure modes. (c) Cost drag is material though out of the recommendation's stated scope: Bitso MXN-book taker is ~0.13-0.65% plus spread, so a buy+sell round trip at ~11 USD costs roughly 1-2%+ (~$0.10-0.25); the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate must use the MXN-book taker rate, not the cheaper USD-book rate. (d) Tax/legal context (3-UMA exemption, ISR 1.92-35%, claimed real-time SAT data access from April 2026) comes only from secondary blogs (MEXC/BeInCrypto), NOT primary SAT/CNBV — downgrade until verified against SAT and Ley Fintech Art. 30; not a blocker since a single ~11 USD trade is de-minimis. The recommendation makes no tax claim, so this is context, not refutation.
verdicts.caveats: Two sub-claims must be corrected before implementation: (1) there is NO Bitso-published numeric golden vector to 'reproduce' — generate your own fixed-input fixture and validate it against an independent openssl HMAC computation; (2) the epoch-ms nonce and clock-skew guard are sensible defensive extras, not Bitso-documented requirements — do not block on or assume a documented skew window. Keep the real load-bearing guarantee: nonce strictly monotonic and never-repeating across process restarts (persist last nonce to crash-safe state, consistent with the journaled architecture). Validate end-to-end read-only first (GET /api/v3/balance) before any order; set the API key to Trade-only, Withdraw OFF, IP-allowlisted to the executor host. For the cost gate, use Bitso MXN-book taker rate (~0.19%+) plus spread. Verify-live the actual signing against the running endpoint (sign one real GET) since the only true contract is the server's 200/401 response, and downgrade all MX tax figures until checked against primary SAT/CNBV sources.

verdicts.recommendation: Make the Bitso executor fail-closed and loud on auth/permission/IP errors: refuse to trade on missing secret or any auth error, surface distinct messages for "IP not allowlisted" (code 0213) and "permission denied" rather than a generic 401, and treat a rate-limit lockout as a HARD circuit-breaker (stop outbound calls, capped exponential backoff with jitter, escalate to kill-switch) — never busy-loop retries.
verdicts.assessment: VERIFIED against Bitso primary sources.
