(1) Rate limit and escalation claim is verbatim-accurate: Bitso docs state private requests allow 300 req/min and "the system locks you out for one minute. Continuous one-minute lockouts might result in a 24-hour block" (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/general-concepts). A runaway retry loop is therefore the genuine ban risk the rec names, and a hard circuit-breaker is the correct mitigation. (2) The "distinct, loud error paths" are not aspirational — Bitso exposes exact codes: 0213 "IP address is not allowed to access this resource" and 0211 "Device authentication failed (Invalid IP or expired Token)" (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/authentication-error-02-http-401). The executor can map these precisely, so the design is implementable as written, even stronger than the rec claims. (3) The underlying security stance is real on Bitso: granular permissions ("Place orders" separate from "Make withdrawals") plus IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses, so TRADE-ONLY + NO-withdrawal + IP-allowlisted keys are achievable. (4) No regulatory blocker for an MX individual: Bitso is a CNBV-registered ITF under Ley Fintech, operational in 2026; 300 req/min requires completed KYC, which the stated path covers. Current code is SimExecutor-only (executor.py), so this is correctly-scoped forward guidance for the BitsoExecutor to be written. No primary-source tax/legal claim is made by THIS rec, so no SAT downgrade is triggered.
verdicts.caveats: Three operational cautions, none fatal: (1) IP allowlisting against a residential MX IP is fragile — Christian's own memory documents dynamic/unstable residential IP behavior. A changing IP triggers 0213 and the fail-closed executor will correctly refuse to trade, but he must plan for static egress (VPS) or manual re-allowlisting; the rec's diagnosable-0213 design is exactly what makes this survivable. (2) The 300 req/min figure is the post-KYC verified-user limit; pre-verification limits are lower (not an issue for the stated self-registration path). (3) This rec does NOT cover Phase-3 tax/legal treatment (SAT/ISR on crypto gains) or fee/spread drag at ~11 USD — those are separate Phase-3 items and must each be verified against SAT/CNBV primary sources independently; do not let this security rec's solidity imply the whole Phase-3 path is cleared.

verdicts.recommendation: Safe API Validation Sequence — refactor Executor into two-phase place_order(intent)->oid + reconcile(oid)->Fill for first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso
verdicts.assessment:
The load-bearing technical claim is TRUE and CURRENT (verified June 2026 against primary sources). Bitso POST /orders returns only {success, payload:{oid}} with no fill data — confirmed at docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/place-an-order. The current Protocol in treasuryforge/executor.py:20-21 (execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill) does assume an atomic synchronous fill, which is structurally impossible against this API. So splitting place/reconcile is genuinely necessary and correctly motivated.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS — none broke the core claim, but I found ONE real understatement that needs fixing:
1. reconcile(oid) -> Fill as specified is INSUFFICIENT. Look Up Orders (the cited reconcile source) returns status {queued/open/partially filled/cancelled/completed}, original_amount, unfilled_amount, and the order's LIMIT price — but NOT the actual execution/fill price. To populate a real Fill (executed price + fee), you need a THIRD call: List Order Trades (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/list-user-trades / list order trades). For a tiny taker market order this is minor (you can approximate from ticker), but the recommendation's literal reconcile(oid)->Fill signature cannot return a true fill price from the cited endpoint alone. The interface should be place_order -> reconcile(status/amounts) -> fetch_trades(oid) for execution price, OR reconcile internally calls both.

VIABILITY for a Mexico individual (not blocked): Individual (non-institutional) Bitso accounts CAN generate API credentials with granular permissions — "Place orders" (trade) can be enabled WITHOUT "Make withdrawals," and "Restrict use of API addresses" supports an IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs). This exactly matches the required trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted security stance — confirmed at docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials. No Mexico-specific API blocker found in the docs.

COST/SIZE sanity: Live btc_mxn minimum_value = 10 MXN (api.bitso.com/v3/available_books), so 200 MXN clears the minimum 20x over — feasible. BUT cost drag is material and the recommendation doesn't surface it: base taker fee on MXN books is 0.65% (maker 0.50%) per bitso.com/fees. A round-trip (buy+sell) market order at base tier costs ~1.3% in fees alone (~2.60 MXN) plus spread — meaningful at 200 MXN. This is a cost-gate concern for Phase-2's cost-aware backtest, not a defect in THIS interface recommendation.

REGULATORY/TAX/SECURITY: This recommendation is a PURELY INTERNAL code-interface change (refactoring a Protocol). It creates NO new regulatory, tax, or custody exposure by itself — those attach to the live-trade decision, not to the interface shape. The security posture it depends on (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlist) is actually SUPPORTED by Bitso, which strengthens rather than weakens the blast-radius containment stance. No primary-source tax/legal claim is made here, so nothing to downgrade.
verdicts.caveats: Fix before implementing: reconcile(oid)->Fill cannot get the true execution price from Look Up Orders alone (it returns only limit price + unfilled_amount); add a List Order Trades fetch (third call) or document that fill price is approximated. Bitso fills are async/polled, so the executor must handle partial fills, 'open' (unfilled) limit orders, and timeouts — the synchronous SimExecutor adapter should still route through reconcile() but will trivially report 'completed'. Secondary (not this recommendation's job but flag for Phase-3): 0.65% taker / ~1.3% round-trip fee drag at 200 MXN is large vs any mechanical edge; confirm the strategy's net-of-fee Deflated Sharpe survives. Tax/legal treatment in Mexico (SAT/CNBV) was NOT in scope of this code recommendation and remains UNVERIFIED here — do not treat this verdict as clearing the tax/legal path; that needs separate primary-source (SAT/CNBV) validation before going live.

verdicts.recommendation: do-with-care
verdicts.assessment:
The CORE idea is sound and current: gating all live orders behind a passing signed GET /api/v3/balance canary is the right zero-risk validation. Verified against Bitso's official docs (2026):
CONFIRMED CORRECT:
- Auth scheme: HMAC-SHA256 over nonce+HTTPmethod+requestPath+JSONpayload, hex-encoded, header `Bitso key:nonce:signature`. Matches the rec. requestPath must include the query string (getPathWithQuery), so _sign(nonce,method,path,body) is fine provided `path` carries any query.
- GET /api/v3/balance is a PRIVATE signed endpoint -> a genuine zero-risk canary that exercises signature/nonce/key/IP-allowlist without moving money.
- Bitso supports TRADE-ONLY keys with WITHDRAW disabled, an Allowed IPs field, and per-asset withdrawal address allowlists. The security stance (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted) is fully achievable. Good.
- Stdlib-only (hmac/hashlib/urllib) reusing the existing audit.py HMAC-SHA256 pattern is real (audit.py lines 36-37 already do hmac.new(key, msg, hashlib.sha256)).
- Platform legality: Bitso is a CNBV-regulated Financial Technology Institution (ITF) under Mexico's 2018 Ley Fintech -> legal for a Mexican individual. No regulatory blocker.

TWO MATERIAL DEFECTS (why it does not hold up as written):
1) NONCE SCHEME IS STALE/WRONG. The rec pins `nonce = max(prev+1, time.time_ns())` -- a v1-style monotonic-integer construction. Bitso DEPRECATED Nonce v1 in November 2025 and now REJECTS all v1 validations. As of June 2026 you MUST use Nonce v2: 14-19 digits = a 13-digit epoch-MILLISECONDS timestamp concatenated with a 1-6 digit random salt (6-digit recommended). time.time_ns() is nanoseconds, not millis+salt; the formula does not implement v2 and risks live rejection. Source: docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/nonce-v2-rollout.
2) THE 'DOCUMENTED EXAMPLE VECTOR' DOES NOT EXIST. The rec says to unit-test the signer offline against 'Bitso's documented example vector' BEFORE any live call. Bitso publishes only a shell SCRIPT using live credentials -- there is no worked numeric test vector with an expected signature output. So the central de-risking step is not executable as described. The realistic substitute is to generate your own vector and byte-compare your Python signer against Bitso's reference shell script, then rely on the live GET /balance canary (which is the rest of the rec) as the real proof.

CONTEXT (not blockers, not claimed by the rec): ~11 USD (200 MXN) sits right at Bitso's ~$10 minimum order size -- a single validation buy works but round-trip taker fee (~0.65% BTC/MXN) plus MXN-market spread is heavy drag, fine for a canary trade not for P&L. SAT treats crypto disposal as enajenacion de bienes; the ~128k MXN annual exemption means no practical tax on a 200-peso trade, though it remains a reportable disposal.
verdicts.caveats: Tax/legal items are sourced to secondary guides citing Ley Fintech Art.30 and SAT enajenacion rules, plus CNBV/ITF status from legal-insight and Bitso sources; for the user's actual filing, confirm the 2026 UMA-based exemption figure and the April-2026 platform real-time-reporting rule against a primary SAT/CNBV publication before relying on them. These tax points do not affect this code recommendation's verdict. The Nonce v2 deprecation date (Nov 2025) and format are from Bitso's own docs; whether v1 throws hard or soft-fails in 2026 was not explicitly restated for 2026, but docs say v1 is rejected after Nov 2025 -- treat v2 as mandatory.

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the Bitso API-key scoping as written (Place orders + View balances ON; Make withdrawals + Perform security actions OFF; Restrict use of API addresses ON with the agent host's IP; 2FA). The recommendation is factually correct and current for a Mexico individual. Add one operational guardrail and one tax note that the recommendation omits.
verdicts.assessment:
I tried to refute this and could not break the core claim. Every concrete assertion verifies against Bitso's own cited docs page (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials), fetched directly:

VERIFIED EXACT:
- The five permission labels are verbatim correct: "Place orders", "View balances", "View account information", "Perform security actions", "Make withdrawals". The recommendation's spelling matches Bitso's UI exactly.
- "Restrict use of API addresses" is the exact IP-allowlist option name; max 5 IPs (the rec says "add the host's current public IPv4" — consistent).
- 2FA is genuinely mandatory: Bitso blocks key creation without it. So "complete 2FA" is not optional advice, it's a hard gate the rec correctly surfaces.
- Permission scoping is real and granular (not all-or-nothing), so trade-only + no-withdrawal + no-security-actions is actually achievable, which is the whole premise of the blast-radius containment.

REGULATORY: Not a blocker. Bitso operates as a CNBV-recognized entity in Mexico (its NVIO subsidiary holds an IFPE authorization under Ley Fintech). A Mexican individual can legally register and self-KYC. No regulatory blocker to the API-key action.

WHY IT HOLDS: The recommendation is a pure owner-action config change with zero code dependency and zero withdrawal capability. It cannot create a tax, custody, or solvency problem by itself — it only narrows what a leaked key can do. It directly implements the Phase-3 security stance (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted). On its own terms it is correct, current, and safe.

WHERE I PUSHED BACK (caveats, not refutations):
1. IP-allowlist + residential dynamic IP = self-inflicted outage risk. The user's own memory shows the agent host is a residential MX connection (the same one that 403s googlevideo, plays on cellular, goes silent on WiFi). Residential ISP IPs rotate. When the public IPv4 changes, the IP-restricted key silently 401/403s and the agent stops trading until the owner re-adds the new IP in the Bitso console. This is a feature working as intended, but the rec presents IP-pinning as pure upside with no mention of the operational cost. For a tiny first trade this is fine (manual, supervised), but do NOT wire IP-pinning into an unattended autonomous loop without a "stale-IP / auth-fail" alarm in the policy engine, or it will look like a kill-switch trip. Recommend: pin the IP for the supervised first trade, AND add detection so an off-IP 401 is logged distinctly from a policy denial.
2. "Use a separate key for stage testing" is correct and good practice, but note the SimExecutor/PaperExecutor in this architecture need NO Bitso key at all — only the live Executor does. So the separate-key advice only applies once you reach a real read-only probe against Bitso, not to paper testing.
3. Tax/cost is out of scope of THIS recommendation but worth flagging since the task asked: at 11 USD on BTC_MXN, Bitso taker fee is ~0.65% and maker ~0.50% (per Bitso fee docs, MXN pairs), so a round-trip is ~1–1.3% plus bid/ask spread — material drag at this size, but it does not affect the key-scoping decision. On tax: secondary sources (not primary) claim Bitso does not withhold ISR and that selling crypto with ANY gain triggers a filing obligation for individuals; I could NOT confirm this from a primary SAT source in this pass, so treat the tax framing as UNVERIFIED and do not rely on the "3 UMA exemption" figure without a SAT/LISR art. 93/141 primary check. This caveat does not touch the recommendation, which is tax-neutral.

CORRECTNESS OF THE CONDITIONAL CLAUSE: The rec says add "View account information" only if reconciliation endpoints need it. Verified: that permission exposes account details / transaction limits, which is plausibly what a reconciliation/limits check would hit. Reasonable and correctly scoped as opt-in.
verdicts.caveats: PRIMARY-SOURCE GAP: The tax claims (Bitso doesn't withhold ISR; any-gain triggers filing; ~128k MXN / 3-UMA exemption) come from secondary blogs, NOT a CNBV/SAT/LISR primary source — downgrade and verify before relying. This does not affect the API-key recommendation itself, which is tax-neutral. OPERATIONAL: IP-allowlisting a residential dynamic IP will silently break the key when the ISP rotates the IP; the policy engine should distinguish an off-IP auth failure from a policy denial so it isn't mistaken for a kill-switch trip. SCOPE: separate-key advice only matters at the real-Bitso probe stage; Sim/Paper executors need no key.

verdicts.recommendation: Add a one-time validate_no_withdraw() pre-flight gate that signs a deliberately-poisoned withdrawal request and asserts HTTP 401 / error.code=='0202' before arming the live Bitso executor.
verdicts.assessment:
PARTIALLY TRUE, but the mechanism is the wrong (and riskiest) one. CONFIRMED against Bitso primary docs (docs.bitso.com): error 0202 = "API key is not authorized to execute the requested method" (HTTP 401), 0201 = "Check your credentials", 0215 = "Incorrect request signature" — so the code the recommendation keys on is real and the 0201-vs-0202 distinction is genuine. Also confirmed: Bitso supports a true trade-only key ("Place orders" + "View balances", withdrawal disabled) with IP-allowlisting and destination-address whitelisting, so the TRADE-ONLY / NO-withdrawal / IP-allowlist stance is fully achievable for a Mexico individual, and there is genuinely no permission-introspection endpoint.

REFUTATIONS: (1) Source misattribution — cited source #2 (ccxt #20924) does NOT support the claim; it is a generic question about testing TRADE permissions and actually proposes place-then-cancel an order as the safe test, i.e. it points to a different, safer method. (2) The load-bearing assumption — that 0202 fires deterministically and BEFORE payload validation — is undocumented by Bitso. General REST practice (401 before 400) makes it plausible but Bitso never commits to checking method-permission before parsing the body. A poisoned payload (sub-minimum amount, empty/non-allowlisted destination) is exactly what trips HTTP-400 validation: 0343 "Incorrect amount", 0315 "Invalid address", 0303 "Required field missing". If Bitso validates payload first, a perfectly-good trade-only key returns 400, and asserting ==0202 treats that as FAIL. That direction is fail-safe (refuses to arm), so not dangerous, but it can falsely block a good key — the "safe-by-construction" claim only holds if 401-precedes-400 holds on Bitso, which is unverified. (3) A strictly safer canonical test exists: confirm the key DOES work for methods it should have (place+cancel a tiny order, read balances) and rely on scope-at-creation (don't tick "Make withdrawals" + IP allowlist + whitelisted/empty destinations). (4) Wrong layer: scope is set in the dashboard at key creation by the owner himself; a runtime withdrawal probe is at best defense-in-depth, and this is its riskiest form — it manufactures failed-withdrawal-attempt signals against the single highest-blast-radius endpoint on EVERY startup, where Bitso has lockout/review machinery (0330 "Withdrawals locked", 0216 account-restricted, transaction-review).

COST/LEGAL (flagged, not the rec's focus): Bitso minimum trade is $10, so an ~11 USD test order barely clears it; taker BTC/MXN fee ~0.65%/side (~1.3% round-trip) + ~0.5% half-spread => ~2% drag — acceptable for a one-time validation. Tax: crypto taxed under ISR as enajenacion de bienes (disposal = sell/swap), progressive 1.92-35%, holding/buying not taxable — but NO primary CNBV/SAT source was obtained (only secondary tax-prep blogs), so DOWNGRADE: verify with SAT/a contador. A single un-sold ~200 MXN buy creates no disposal event, so near-zero immediate tax exposure.</assessment>
<parameter name="caveats">Keep the GOAL (prove the live key cannot withdraw before arming), replace the MECHANISM. Recommended substitute for validate_no_withdraw(): (a) create the key in the Bitso dashboard with "Make withdrawals" UNCHECKED + IP allowlist + empty/whitelisted destination list; (b) at startup, run POSITIVE-scope checks only — read balances (proves read scope) and optionally place+immediately-cancel a sub-fillable tiny limit order far from market (proves trade scope), never touch the withdrawal endpoint; (c) treat the absence of withdrawal scope as established at creation-time, not probed at runtime. If a runtime negative-proof is still desired, do it ONCE manually during setup and log it, not on every startup self-check. The 401-before-400 precedence on Bitso is UNVERIFIED — do not ship an assertion that hard-fails on !=0202 without first confirming live what a real trade-only key returns to that poisoned payload (it may legitimately return a 400 code). Tax/legal statements lack a primary CNBV/SAT citation and must be downgraded to "confirm with SAT / contador."

verdicts.recommendation: Fix the Fill fee-denomination model before any live Bitso reconciliation: generalize Fill to carry fee_currency, debit the fee from the correct leg (base on BUY, quote on SELL), and have the live executor read fees_amount/fees_currency from GET /user_trades after each fill.
verdicts.assessment: The recommendation holds up under adversarial verification. Two independent claims both check out. (1) CODE: treasuryforge/types.py:64-74 does hard-code the fee as quote-denominated — quote_delta returns -(gross + fee) on BUY, and base_delta (lines 77-79) returns +base_amount with no fee deduction. Verified by reading the file. (2) BITSO BEHAVIOR: the cited primary doc (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/user-trades) was fetched twice; its example response shows side:"buy", fees_currency:"btc", fees_amount:"0.00000327", major_currency:"btc", minor_currency:"mxn" — i.e. the BUY fee is denominated in the BASE asset received, exactly as claimed. I also confirmed place-an-order returns only oid (no fee data), so reading /user_trades afterward to get the realized fee is the correct prescription, not optional. The failure-mode direction is mechanically right: the model over-credits base by fee_base, so a live wallet's btc.total will be lower than the model expects by exactly the base-denominated fee. Web-search SUMMARIES were contradictory (one claimed buy fees are in fiat/minor), but those are unreliable paraphrases; the actual JSON example in the primary doc is authoritative and unambiguous. No regulatory/tax/security/cost downside: this is a pure internal-correctness fix touching no KYC, custody, key-scoping, or tax surface — it strictly makes the ledger more faithful before real money moves. Separately confirmed Bitso supports trade-only / no-withdrawal key permissions (View balances + Place orders, Make withdrawals left OFF), so the surrounding security stance is achievable on this platform.
verdicts.caveats: Two scope nuances, neither fatal. (1) The consequence "permanently false-tripping the kill-switch" presumes a live reconciliation layer that compares model balances to exchange balances and trips on mismatch — that layer does not exist yet (executors are the swap-in; only SimExecutor exists today). So this is a fix-before-you-build-live item, not a fix to an actively-firing bug. That is still the correct Phase-3 sequencing and the recommendation frames it as "live data will immediately expose," which is accurate. (2) "Debit the correct leg" is right but should be made explicit for the SELL path too: on a SELL the fee is charged in the QUOTE asset (you keep current behavior), while on a BUY the fee must be subtracted from base_delta and removed from the quote_delta double-charge. A naive fix that only flips BUY to "fee in base" without auditing both legs and the SELL case could introduce a new asymmetry. Also note the doc example only contains BUY trades (no SELL example shown), so the SELL=quote-fee assumption rests on the standard "fee paid in the asset received" convention rather than a doc example — verify it on the first live SELL or a paper trade. None of these block the recommendation; they refine it.

verdicts.recommendation: Implement reconcile() against exchange TOTAL (=available+locked, pending excluded) per currency with per-asset Decimal tolerances, fallback to user_trades for fast full-fills, trip kill-switch on divergence. (Theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso.)
verdicts.assessment:
Tried hard to refute it; the core holds up against primary sources. VERIFIED against Bitso's own docs (primary, current 2026):
1. Bitso returns decimal values as STRINGS and explicitly recommends string handling to avoid float truncation — confirms the float-vs-string mismatch driving the recommendation. (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/get-account-balance)
2. total = available + locked, with pending_deposit/pending_withdrawal explicitly EXCLUDED from total — quoted verbatim from the doc. So reconciling against TOTAL (not available) is the correct anchor and the "park funds in locked" false-alarm concern is real.
3. Look Up Orders returns only open/partial orders; completed/cancelled drop out — and the doc literally states "a one-hour window exists" for retrieving those statuses. The recommendation's "~1h lookup window" is therefore ACCURATE and well-sourced, not hand-waved. user_trades is confirmed as the correct fallback for vanished fast fills. (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/look-up-orders, list-user-trades)
4. CODE CLAIMS VERIFIED IN REPO: wallet.py lives at treasuryforge/treasuryforge/wallet.py; lines 41 and 45 contain the `< -1e-9` guard the rec cites as "wallet.py:41-48" — accurate. SimWallet stores balances as float (lines 22-23). There is NO existing reconcile() and NO per-asset tolerance anywhere, so this is correctly net-new code (owner=codebase).
5. SECURITY STANCE IS SUPPORTED BY THE PLATFORM: Bitso API keys support a Trade permission independent of Withdraw, mandatory IP allowlist, and withdrawal destination-address whitelisting — so trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted keys are achievable, and tripping the kill-switch on divergence keeps blast radius inside the policy engine. Good fit.

REGULATORY/TAX: The reconcile() logic itself creates NO tax/legal/regulatory exposure — it is a read-only balance comparison. Tax (ISR on enajenacion; ~60k MXN annual capital-gains exemption for non-habitual personas fisicas) attaches to the TRADE, not to reconciliation code, and is out of scope for this specific recommendation. I did NOT find a CNBV/SAT primary source in this pass (only secondary tax-guide blogs), so any tax claim elsewhere in Phase-3 should be downgraded until backed by SAT/LISR primary text — but that does not bear on this recommendation.

WHAT NEARLY REFUTES IT (the do-with-care basis): (a) Tolerance too tight — BTC `< 1e-8` is exactly one satoshi; an exact-equality-at-satoshi-scale gate can FALSE-TRIP the kill-switch on legitimate exchange-side rounding/fee dust on the base asset. Prefer `<= 1 quantum` or a small multiple, or reconcile the integer-satoshi ledger exactly only if you control rounding end-to-end. (b) The phrase "blanket 1e-9 clamp" mildly mischaracterizes the code: wallet.py:41-48 is a negative-overdraft GUARD, not a reconciliation tolerance — the substantive point (no per-asset tolerance exists; don't reuse a float epsilon for MXN's 0.01 quantum) is still correct. (c) FEASIBILITY BLOCKER for the wider goal, not this rec: Bitso's btc_mxn minimum order is 0.005 BTC (~500k sat), which at 2026 BTC prices is far above an ~11 USD / ~200 MXN budget — a BTC trade is likely not executable at this size via the trading API; the owner needs a lower-minimum book or the simple-buy path. reconcile() must therefore be tested against whatever asset is actually tradable at 200 MXN, not assumed BTC.
verdicts.caveats: Loosen BTC tolerance from strict `< 1e-8` to `<= 1 quantum` (or a small multiple) to avoid false kill-switch trips on exchange-side base-asset rounding/fee dust; keep MXN at 0.01 (centavo) — that quantum is correct. Reconcile against the ASSET ACTUALLY TRADABLE at 200 MXN: Bitso's btc_mxn minimum is 0.005 BTC (~$500), so BTC is not executable at 11 USD via the trading API — pick a lower-minimum book or the simple-buy path before assuming this reconcile target. Confirm on a live read-only key that pending_* really stay out of `total` for your specific asset before trusting the available+locked identity in code. The "~1h window" is real but is a soft retention window, not a hard SLA — treat user_trades as the authoritative completed-fill source, not a mere fallback. No CNBV/SAT primary source was located this pass; downgrade any separate tax/legal claims in Phase-3 until backed by SAT/LISR primary text (this does not affect the reconcile recommendation itself).

verdicts.recommendation: Make the executor base_url a single config constant (sandbox/stage vs prod) and add a CLI/runtime gate (paper -> prod-ro -> prod-tiny -> prod-live) so each rung of the validation ladder is enforced in code; first live run STOPS after read-only; first real order = exchange minimum fetched from available_books, not the full 200 MXN.
verdicts.assessment:
Core mechanics verified against Bitso's live docs and hold up for a Mexico-based individual in 2026.

1) Base-URL claim TRUE. Bitso docs confirm production = the stage/sandbox URL with the environment word removed (https://stage.bitso.com/api/v3/ -> https://bitso.com/api/v3/). Same code path, env differs by base URL. Confirmed.

2) available_books mechanic TRUE and load-bearing-safe. The endpoint is PUBLIC/unauthenticated and returns minimum_amount AND minimum_value per book (also minimum_price/value, tick_size, fees). For btc_mxn the minimum_value is 5 MXN (minimum_amount 0.000075 BTC). So "first order = exchange minimum, not 200 MXN" bounds first-trade risk to ~5 MXN of capital. The smallest-order-first reasoning is correct and stronger than the recommendation states (~5 MXN, not "a few MXN" hand-wave).

3) Security stance FULLY SUPPORTED natively. Bitso API keys are permission-scoped: enable "Place orders" while leaving "Make withdrawals" DISABLED = trade-only, no-withdrawal. "Restrict use of IP addresses" allows up to 5 allowlisted IPs. Trade-only + IP-allowlist is exactly achievable; the policy engine's blast-radius containment is correctly aligned with what Bitso enforces server-side.

4) Registration/KYC for a Mexican individual is a real, unblocked path. Bitso is CNBV-regulated under the Fintech Law; individuals (personas fisicas) register with country of residence + email, then KYC (official ID + selfie + personal data), minutes-to-hours. No institutional gate. The owner can self-register.

5) Tax: the recommendation makes NO tax claim, so nothing to refute there. For completeness: crypto sells and crypto->crypto swaps are taxable as enajenacion de bienes under ISR, but personas fisicas have a ~128,383 MXN/yr exemption (3x annual UMA), so a ~200 MXN (or ~5 MXN) trade is orders of magnitude below any threshold. This is sourced only to secondary/news/advisory sites, not a SAT/LISR primary citation, so the tax framing is DOWNGRADED to "directionally correct, not primary-sourced" — immaterial at this size, but do not rely on these numbers when scaling up.

REFUTATIONS THAT STAND (why do-with-care, not do-now):
A) "Same code path against every environment / differ only by base URL" is PARTIALLY MISLEADING. Bitso docs explicitly state stage/sandbox requires a SEPARATE account AND separately generated API credentials from production. So the config constant must swap base_url AND key/secret together; treating it as "only the base URL changes" risks signing prod requests with stage creds (or vice-versa) and getting auth failures, or worse, mixing environments. The staged CLI gate should bind {base_url, api_key, api_secret} as one atomic profile.
B) Sandbox/stage liveness in 2026 is UNCONFIRMED. No outage/deprecation notice was found, but no positive proof the testing server is currently funded/working either (exchange sandboxes are frequently stale, and the docs no longer clearly distinguish sandbox vs stage). The 4-rung ladder depends on a working non-prod rung; if stage is dead, the practical path collapses to paper -> prod-ro -> prod-tiny, which is still safe but changes the design.

No regulatory, security, or cost blocker for a first tiny real trade. Cost note: at the 5 MXN minimum the taker fee (~0.65%) + spread make it near-pure-loss, but the recommendation correctly frames this as a VALIDATION trade, not an investment, so that is consistent, not a defect.
verdicts.caveats: Two corrections before shipping: (1) The env switch must be an atomic profile {base_url, api_key, api_secret} — Bitso requires SEPARATE accounts and SEPARATE credentials for stage vs prod, so "only the base URL changes" is wrong and could cross-wire creds. (2) VERIFY-LIVE that Bitso stage/sandbox is actually up and fundable in 2026 before committing to a prod-tiny rung that assumes a working non-prod rung; if it's dead, drop to paper -> prod-ro -> prod-tiny. Minor: confirm IP allowlist works under the owner's residential MX IP (dynamic IPs will break a 5-slot allowlist). Tax framing is secondary-sourced only — fine at 200 MXN, re-source from SAT/LISR before scaling.

verdicts.recommendation: Read minimum_value AND minimum_amount live from GET /api/v3/available_books at startup and reject any book where max(minimum_amount*price, minimum_value) > order_notional; never hardcode btc_mxn minimums; mirror as a policy-engine floor.
verdicts.assessment: VERIFIED and current for a Mexico individual in 2026. (1) Endpoint fields confirmed: Bitso docs (list-available-books) return minimum_amount, maximum_amount, minimum_price, maximum_price, minimum_value, maximum_value as decimal-strings; example btc_mxn shows minimum_amount=0.000075, minimum_value=5. (2) Source disagreement is REAL: an older spec shows minimum_amount=0.003 / minimum_value=25, and 0.003 BTC at any 2026 price (>>200 MXN) would hard-reject the order. So runtime-fetch genuinely resolves a contradiction that would otherwise silently sub-minimum-reject and leave a position un-flattened — the why is sound. (3) Hardcoding is independently wrong because a blog-stated 'flat $10 minimum across all pairs' contradicts the API's own btc_mxn minimum_value=5 MXN; trusting live API values over blogs is the correct stance, which strengthens the recommendation. (4) Security stance holds: Bitso API keys expose granular permissions ('Place orders' separate from 'Make withdrawals') and an IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs), so trade-only + no-withdrawal + IP-allowlist is achievable exactly as required; the policy engine still contains blast radius. (5) Platform is viable: Bitso is CNBV-registered as an ITF in Mexico with active KYC; an individual can self-register. REFUTATION ATTEMPTS THAT FAILED: the '$10 universal minimum' blog claim is contradicted by the API and does not block a 200 MXN order; no regulatory/tax problem is created by this code change. The change itself is code, not a live trade, so risk is low. CAVEATS the recommendation omits: (a) selecting a book purely by 'smallest minimum that fits' can route into an illiquid/wide-spread pair — the selector should also weight liquidity/spread, not just minimum-fits, especially at ~11 USD where MXN-market taker fees (~0.60%) plus spread are a heavy round-trip drag; (b) IP allowlist + a residential/dynamic Mexican home IP (a documented pain point for this user) will break the key when the IP rotates — operationally real; (c) tax framing for completeness only: SAT treats crypto disposal as enajenacion de bienes (LISR art. 141), swaps are taxable, and 2026 CARF gives SAT real-time platform data, but 200 MXN sits far under the ~128,383 MXN UMA-based exemption and the recommendation makes no tax claim, so no downgrade — though any future tax assertion must cite LISR/SAT primary text, not the blogs found. Net: technically true, current, and a genuine safety improvement; downgraded from do-now only because the book-selection heuristic and IP-allowlist/dynamic-IP interaction need care before live reliance.
verdicts.caveats: Book selection should weight liquidity/spread, not only 'smallest minimum that fits' (illiquid-pair routing + ~0.60% MXN taker fee/spread drag at 11 USD). IP-allowlisted key will break on a dynamic residential MX IP. Tax claims (SAT/LISR art.141, CARF 2026) come only from secondary blogs — demand LISR/SAT primary source before any tax assertion; not blocking at 200 MXN (under ~128,383 MXN exemption). The change is code, not a live trade, so verify the fetched btc_mxn (or chosen-book) minimums against a live API response before the first real order.

verdicts.recommendation: Use origin_id as a deterministic idempotency key derived from the policy-approved intent's audit/journal decision id (<=40 chars in [A-Za-z0-9_-]); persist it in the crash-safe journal BEFORE the HTTP POST; on timeout/5xx check if the order landed before re-POSTing with the SAME id; gate retries on journal state, not exchange rejection.
verdicts.assessment:
The core claim is TRUE and current (2026), confirmed verbatim against the primary source docs.bitso.com. (1) origin_id uniqueness is scoped to ACTIVE orders only — Bitso changelog 2025-05-27: "Order origin_id uniqueness is now scoped to active orders... removing the prior requirement for unique origin_id across all active and traded orders." (2) Character set is exactly [A-Za-z0-9_-] (dash added 2025-01-08) with max length 40 — matches the recommendation's constraint precisely. (3) A re-POST matching an active origin_id is REJECTED; a re-POST after the order has FILLED creates a NEW order — this is the exact correctness trap the recommendation is designed around, and its fix (journal as at-most-once source of truth, gate retries on journal state not on exchange rejection, mark terminally-executed on fill, add a retry-after-fill test) is sound and strictly safer than naive retry. So the recommendation's thesis holds.

However, adversarial refutation surfaced one real GAP in the proposed retry SEQUENCE, not the thesis: the recommendation says to GET /orders?origin_ids=<id> to confirm whether an order landed before re-POSTing. The origin_ids query param IS real (Look Up Orders endpoint), but that endpoint returns ONLY open or partially-filled orders, with just a ~1-hour window where completed/cancelled orders remain visible before permanent removal. A ~11 USD market order will typically fill instantly, so this GET can return EMPTY for an order that actually executed — and a re-POST off that empty result would double-spend the 200 MXN. The journal-state gate the recommendation also mandates is what actually prevents this, but the specific GET /orders landed-check it proposes is necessary-not-sufficient and must be backed by a fill/balance check (List User Trades or balance delta) for the already-filled case. Author should close this.

Minor: the "420s" reference is not Bitso's actual rate-limit semantics (Bitso documents 60 req/min public / 300 req/min private with a 1-minute lockout, not an HTTP 420). Cosmetic; retry/backoff should target Bitso's real lockout behavior. No tax/legal/custody claim is made here, so no primary-source (SAT/CNBV) downgrade applies to this recommendation.

Net: the idempotency design is correct, current, and safe for a first tiny live trade — do it, but harden the landed-check to handle instantly-filled orders (the GET /orders window misses them) and fix the rate-limit detail.
verdicts.caveats: GET /orders?origin_ids= returns only open/partial orders (filled orders drop out after ~1h), so for an instantly-filled ~11 USD market order the landed-check can return empty even though it executed — back the journal gate with a fill/balance/List-User-Trades confirmation, not GET /orders alone. The "420" rate-limit code is wrong: Bitso uses 60/min public, 300/min private with a 1-minute lockout. Tax/custody not addressed by this claim (correctly out of scope). All technical facts verified against primary source docs.bitso.com (place-an-order + history-of-changes + look-up-orders), changelog dated 2025-05-27 and 2025-01-08.

verdicts.recommendation: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual): complete Bitso KYC before generating a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key; budget for fee/spread drag; keep funds custodial on Bitso; withdraw only manually via web UI.
verdicts.assessment: Core claims verified against primary Bitso docs and Mexican tax sources, and they hold for a 2026 Mexico-based individual. (1) Trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlist key is a REAL Bitso feature: "Place orders" is separable from "Make withdrawals," and the API key IP allowlist supports up to 5 addresses (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). (2) KYC IS a hard prerequisite for the 300 RPM private tier — Bitso general-concepts states public=60 RPM, private=300 RPM "for verified users who have fully completed the KYC process," so signed private calls require full KYC. (3) Fee math is correct: MXN-market entry tier (under ~1.5M MXN 30-day volume) is 0.50% maker / 0.65% taker, so round-trip taker = 2 x 0.65% = 1.30%. (4) Bitso is CNBV-supervised/legal for a Mexican individual; self-registration KYC is the normal path; no platform-level blocker. (5) Custodial-at-this-size with the no-withdrawal key as the blast-radius control is sound; self-custody at 11 USD adds gas/key risk for no benefit. WEAKNESSES (downgrades, not kills): (a) The 1.3% figure UNDERSTATES true cost because it excludes the bid-ask SPREAD, which on MXN pairs adds materially on top of taker fees — real round-trip is likely >1.3%, plausibly 1.5-2.5%+ on a thin book with a market order; the recommendation says to confirm the live order book first, which partly mitigates this, but it frames 1.3% as the expectation rather than a floor. (b) Many secondary reviews cite a "0.1% fee cap" that applies only to USD/stablecoin markets, NOT MXN — the recommendation correctly uses 0.65% MXN, but cross-checkers will be misled. (c) Tax (not an explicit claim in the rec, but in project scope): crypto gains for individuals fall under LISR Art. 93 Fr. XIX(b) enajenacion de bienes muebles, exempt up to 3x annual UMA ~128,383 MXN for 2026 — an 11 USD trade's gain is trivially exempt; this is well-supported by Mexican tax-advisory sources but the SDV primary-text fetch only surfaced the casa-habitacion fraction, so treat the 3-UMA movable-property exemption as well-supported-secondary, not a verbatim primary quote. CARF data collection began Jan 1 2026 — Bitso reports to SAT regardless. (d) Minimum order ~1 USD/~100 MXN; 200 MXN clears it but with thin headroom — don't split.
verdicts.caveats: Treat 1.3% as a FLOOR, not the expected drag — it excludes bid-ask spread. To minimize total cost, use the tightest-book pair (USDT/MXN or USDC/MXN, not a thin alt/MXN), and place a LIMIT order rather than a market order to avoid eating the spread. The "0.1% fee cap" cited in some reviews is the stablecoin/USD market, NOT the 0.65%-taker MXN market — do not plan around 0.1%. Tax 3-UMA (~128,383 MXN) movable-property exemption (LISR Art. 93 Fr. XIX-b) is from tax-advisory secondary sources, not a verbatim primary LISR quote — directionally correct and the trade is far under the threshold, but confirm with the actual LISR text or an accountant if scaling up. Bitso reports to SAT under CARF (effective Jan 1 2026), so exemption does not mean invisibility — keep cost-basis records from trade #1. Verify the chosen pair's LIVE minimum and current fee tier on the order book immediately before the first order, as the recommendation already instructs.

verdicts.recommendation: Owner: register on Bitso (CURP/INE Mexican-resident KYC) and generate a TRADE-ONLY API key — check ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (+ 'View account info' if the executor reads limits); leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' UNCHECKED; add the builder's static/egress IP to the allowlist (up to 5 IPs). Store key/secret outside the repo.
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED AND CURRENT (2026). I tried to refute the core security claim and could not — it is accurate against Bitso's primary documentation. The five permission labels in the recommendation match Bitso's docs VERBATIM ('Place orders', 'View balances', 'View account information', 'Perform security actions', 'Make withdrawals'), they ARE independent toggles, the IP allowlist max IS five addresses, and a trade-only/no-withdrawal key is genuinely supported. The blast-radius logic is sound: a key without 'Make withdrawals' physically cannot move funds out, so a leak is contained to in-policy trades. This is the single best security control and the recommendation states it correctly. Source: https://docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials (primary).

WHAT THE RECOMMENDATION OMITS OR UNDERSTATES (the Phase-3 goal explicitly asked for these; the recommendation text is silent on them):

1) MISSING PREREQUISITE — 2FA. Bitso BLOCKS API-key creation unless 2FA is enabled on the account first. The recommendation jumps to key generation without this gate. Add: enable 2FA before attempting key creation. (Primary docs, confirmed.)

2) FEE DRAG IS SEVERE AND THE WRONG NUMBER IS EASy TO ASSUME. Bitso's headline 0.15% maker / 0.19% taker is the USD/crypto-pair rate. For MXN spot pairs the lowest-volume tier (<20,000 MXN/30d — exactly where an 11-USD trader sits) is 0.600% MAKER / 0.780% TAKER (https://bitso.com/fees). A market-order round-trip (buy+sell) costs ~1.56% in fees ALONE, before bid/ask spread, on a ~200 MXN position that is ~3 MXN. This is a meaningful, often-decisive drag at this size and the recommendation does not mention it. Use limit (maker) orders to halve it. The Phase-2 'cost-aware backtest gate' MUST be parameterized with 0.60%/0.78% MXN-pair fees, not 0.15%/0.19%, or the live edge will be overstated.

3) MINIMUM ORDER SIZE ~= USD 10. Bitso's minimum trade is reported at ~USD 10 across pairs. An ~11 USD budget leaves almost zero headroom: after a buy, fees and any price move can drop the sellable notional below the minimum, stranding the position. The 200 MXN size is right at the operational floor — workable but fragile. Verify the exact current per-pair minimum live before trusting partial-fill / re-balance logic.

4) REGULATORY FRAMING. Bitso is NOT a CNBV-authorized ITF (Ley Fintech). Per Global Legal Insights 2026, exchanges like Bitso operate legally but as a 'vulnerable activity' / DNFBP under AML law (SAT-registered, CDD/reporting obligations; July-2025 reform triggers reports at >=210 UMA, ~USD 1,180). A 200 MXN individual trade is FAR below that threshold and fully legal — but the owner is using an AML-registered exchange, so KYC/source-of-funds is normal and his activity is reportable to SAT under CARF (OECD Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework, in force 2026). Not a blocker; just be aware the trade is visible to tax authorities.

5) TAX CLAIM NEEDS A PRIMARY SOURCE — DOWNGRADED. Secondary sources cite a SAT '20% provisional ISR payment on the total amount of each disposition' (one attributes it to Prodecon). I could NOT confirm this in a primary CNBV/SAT/DOF source; the authoritative legal review (GLI 2026) describes individual ISR at 1.92%–35% on gains with NO mention of a 20% per-transaction withholding. TREAT THE 20% AS UNVERIFIED. What IS well-supported: gains from disposing crypto are taxable; a ~128,383 MXN annual exemption (3x UMA) for sale-of-asset gains likely zeroes out tax at this size; BUT a MECHANICAL bot generates many taxable dispositions (every sell AND every crypto-to-crypto/stablecoin swap is a disposition), creating a record-keeping burden far out of proportion to the profit. Keep a full trade ledger from trade #1. Confirm the 20% provisional rule and any per-disposition obligation with a Mexican accountant / primary SAT source before scaling beyond the test trade.

6) CUSTODY. At ~200 MXN, leaving funds custodied on Bitso is the correct, pragmatic choice (the recommendation implies this and that's fine) — self-custody overhead is not worth it at this size, AND it's actually required since the API key is deliberately no-withdrawal. Just don't accumulate meaningful balance on the exchange long-term.

NET: The literal recommendation (scoped no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted, off-repo key) is TRUE, current, safe, and should be done. The verdict is do-with-care only because the surrounding Phase-3 deliverables it sits inside (fees, minimum size, tax) have gaps/one likely-wrong number that would bite at execution time if not corrected.
verdicts.caveats: Two items genuinely block-or-bite if ignored: (a) the cost model must use Bitso's MXN-pair low-tier fees (0.60%/0.78%), not the 0.15%/0.19% USD-pair rate the recommendation's silence invites; round-trip taker ~1.56% on a 200 MXN trade. (b) The 20% SAT provisional-withholding figure is NOT confirmed by a primary CNBV/SAT/DOF source and conflicts with the authoritative legal review — treat as unverified pending an accountant. Also unstated prerequisite: 2FA must be enabled before any API key can be created. Minimum order ~USD 10 leaves near-zero headroom at an 11 USD budget — verify live.

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt it — do-now — with two refinements: (1) enforce the floor on minimum_value but ALSO validate price against tick_size and base against minimum_amount before submitting (the live btc_mxn book has tick_size=10 on price and minimum_amount=0.0000006; an order that clears the value floor can still be rejected for a non-tick-aligned price); (2) treat the fee parenthetical as the BASE tier only — the live API returns a tiered fees.structure (9 volume bands), so load the per-book fee from the flat_rate field at startup rather than assuming a single static 0.78%.
verdicts.assessment: I refuted the recommendation against the live source of truth and it holds. LIVE VERIFICATION (fetched https://api.bitso.com/v3/available_books today, 2026-06-16): btc_mxn returns minimum_value="10", tick_size="10", minimum_amount="0.0000006", minimum_price="100000", and fees.flat_rate.taker="0.0078" (0.78%) and maker="0.006". usd_mxn likewise minimum_value="10". Both numeric claims in the recommendation (minimum_value=10 MXN, taker=0.78%) are CURRENT and CORRECT. The "two cross-agent contradictions prove static values go stale" rationale is itself validated by the fact that the live taker is tiered (fees.structure has 9 volume bands), not the flat single number any doc would freeze — so "read live, never hardcode" is the right call and is actually understated. ARCHITECTURE: confirmed against the codebase — policy.py:108-113 already enforces max_notional_per_tx as a notional CAP, so extending the same notional() computation to a FLOOR is a minimal, sound change; executor.py:20 defines the Executor Protocol as the documented swap point, so a Bitso executor fetching /available_books on startup fits cleanly. SECURITY: the GET /available_books call is public/unauthenticated, so it needs NO API key and introduces ZERO blast radius — fully compatible with the trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted stance (Bitso confirmed: 'Place orders' permission separable from 'Make withdrawals', plus up to 5 IPs in the allowlist). At ~200 MXN the order clears every minimum comfortably (200 >> 10 MXN floor), so this prevents the documented failure mode (rejected first order) without creating new risk. TAX/REGULATORY: the recommendation makes NO tax claim, so nothing to downgrade — it is pure code mechanics. (Separate context note, not part of this rec: SAT treats crypto as activo virtual / bien mueble intangible under Ley Fintech art. 30; disposal is a taxable enajenación under LISR; the bien-mueble exemption is 'tres veces el salario minimo/UMA anual' per LISR art. 93 — secondary sources disagree on the peso figure (~60k vs ~128k), which is exactly why any tax number must come from CNBV/SAT primary text, not an agent. A 200 MXN trade is far under any threshold.)
verdicts.caveats: minimum_value alone is necessary but not sufficient: also enforce tick_size price-alignment and minimum_amount on the base leg, and load the per-book fee from fees.flat_rate (not a hardcoded 0.78%) since the real schedule is tiered. The 'live values' are a snapshot — they were verified 2026-06-16 and the whole point of the rec is to re-fetch at runtime, so do NOT also freeze these numbers into tests as expected constants. The available_books call should be defensive (timeout + cached-last-good fallback) so a Bitso API blip on startup does not hard-block the executor. Tax exemption peso figure is unverified against SAT primary source (secondary sources conflict), but the recommendation itself makes no tax claim so this does not affect the verdict.

verdicts.recommendation: Build a PaperExecutor as an intermediate tier (Sim -> Paper -> live), but do NOT make the Bitso sandbox a hard mandatory gate. Use the sandbox for what it reliably gives (real auth-signing + request/response contract against api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/), treat its testnet FUNDING and order-FILL path as unreliable/optional, and validate fills/slippage/fee-debits/permission-enforcement against PRODUCTION read-only endpoints plus ONE tiny real ~11 USD order with a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key.
verdicts.assessment: The recommendation is directionally correct but materially overstated on its load-bearing dependency. VERIFIED TRUE: (1) The sandbox exists at https://api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/ and the two cited Bitso docs are genuine and on-point. (2) The 3-tier ladder is sound and the existing codebase already anticipates it — executor.py defines an `Executor` Protocol with a single `execute(...)` signature and explicitly names PaperExecutor as the swap-in, so adding the tier is low-cost and architecturally clean. (3) The live-tier security stance is fully verifiable on production Bitso: API keys support granular scopes ("Place orders" = trade-only, "Make withdrawals" can be left OFF) plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses — exactly the trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted posture the project requires. REFUTED / OVERSTATED: (a) "FREE Bitso sandbox with testnet funding" is misleading. The funding docs cite Ethereum's Ropsten testnet (permanently shut down since 2022) and BTC testnet3 (chronically unreliable), indicating the sandbox docs are stale and unmaintained; testnet funding cannot be assumed to work. (b) Sandbox access is NOT frictionless self-serve: onboarding requires emailing developersupport@bitso.com to verify your phone (no SMS in test env), obtain a transaction PIN, and request funds + Wallets-API activation by sending your user ID — a manual, support-gated, indeterminate-latency process with no guarantee an individual (vs. a business/CaaS partner) is serviced promptly. (c) Calling this a "MANDATORY tier" before any real pesos is therefore risky: a broken/slow sandbox could block the first 11 USD trade indefinitely, and the recommendation itself concedes the real value (real fills, slippage, fee debits, production permission enforcement) lies in what the sandbox CANNOT test. ADJACENT CONSTRAINT (not claimed by the rec but relevant): Bitso minimum trade size is ~10 USD and base taker fee is 0.19% (maker 0.15%); at ~11 USD the owner can place essentially ONE order at the floor, leaving almost no room for the policy engine to subdivide notional. TAX/LEGAL (downgraded — no primary source): crypto are "Activos Virtuales" under the Ley Fintech and gains are taxed by SAT as enajenacion de bienes (ISR 10-37%, with a ~3x-UMA annual exemption), but every source found was a secondary blog, not a primary CNBV/SAT bulletin, so any tax figure must be treated as unverified. The recommendation makes no tax claim, so this does not change its verdict.
verdicts.caveats: Sandbox testnet funding is likely broken (Ropsten dead, BTC testnet3 unreliable) and access is support-gated via email, not self-serve — so the sandbox cannot be a hard prerequisite. Build PaperExecutor and exercise the sandbox auth/contract opportunistically, but gate the go-live decision on production read-only validation + one tiny real order, not on sandbox completion. Confirm sandbox liveness with a real curl before committing to it. Tax/legal treatment cited from secondary blogs only — get a primary SAT/CNBV source or a Mexican tax advisor before reporting gains. Watch the ~10 USD minimum + 0.19% taker fee: at 11 USD you effectively get one floor-sized trade.

verdicts.recommendation: Wire the live Bitso per-book taker/maker fee into the Phase-2 cost-aware Deflated-Sharpe / purged-CV gate and the solvency/edge check, rejecting intents whose expected edge < round-trip cost. CORRECTION REQUIRED: read the AUTHENTICATED /v3/fees/ (List Fees) endpoint per-account, NOT the public /v3/available_books/ flat_rate.
verdicts.assessment:
CORE THESIS HOLDS, BUT THE FACTS ARE INVERTED. True/current parts: (1) Fee drag IS the dominant cost at ~11 USD — SPEI MXN deposits are free on Bitso and the 1 USD min order does not bind at 11 USD, so per-trade % fees decide net edge. (2) Wiring the live per-book fee into the Deflated-Sharpe/purged-CV gate + solvency edge-check is correct and important: an understated fee would green-light a net-losing strategy. (3) The security stance is fully achievable on Bitso — API keys have SEPARATE 'Place orders' vs 'Make withdrawals' permissions, an IP allowlist (up to 5), and per-crypto withdrawal-address allowlists; a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-locked key is real and recommended.

DECISIVE REFUTATION — the numbers are backwards. I fetched both endpoints live (2026): the PUBLIC /v3/available_books/ flat_rate for btc_mxn returns maker 0.006 / taker 0.0078 (0.60%/0.78%) and its structure array's lowest tier matches that. The AUTHENTICATED /v3/fees/ (List Fees) endpoint returns the customer's ACTUAL active tier = maker 0.50% / taker 0.65%, dropping to 0.49%/0.637% at 1.5M MXN 30-day volume. Bitso docs, BrokerRank, and FXEmpire all confirm 0.50/0.65 as the CURRENT entry tier. So the recommendation has it inverted: it calls 0.50/0.65 the 'stale doc sample' and 0.60/0.78 the 'LIVE numbers,' and tells him to read the WRONG endpoint (available_books flat_rate). 0.60/0.78 is the public DEFAULT reference rate, not the account-applied rate.

WHY NOT 'AVOID': wiring the higher 0.78% number is CONSERVATIVE — it overstates cost, so it can only make the gate stricter, never green-light a loser. Safety direction is correct; only the framing + endpoint choice are wrong. Round-trip cost is real either way: ~1.30% taker / ~1.00% maker (real rates) up to ~1.56% / ~1.20% (public default) on an 11 USD trade.

OTHER FLAGS: Legal — Bitso operates legally in Mexico via CNBV-authorized subsidiary NVIO (IFPE); crypto trading runs under the AML 'vulnerable activity' scheme, not a blocker for an individual. TAX (DOWNGRADED): convergent secondary sources describe ISR progressive 1.92-35%, ~60k MXN annual asset-gain exemption, crypto-to-crypto as a taxable disposal, INPC inflation-adjusted cost basis, and CARF/real-time SAT reporting from 2026 — but I could NOT confirm these against a PRIMARY SAT/DOF/LISR document (the 403-blocked and blog sources are secondary explainers). Per the primary-source demand, the tax specifics must be downgraded to 'verify with SAT/accountant.' At ~200 MXN the realized gain is negligible and almost certainly inside any exemption, so tax is not a blocker for the FIRST trade, only a record-keeping habit to start.
verdicts.caveats: 1) FACTUAL INVERSION must be fixed before coding: 0.50%/0.65% is the REAL account entry tier (authenticated /v3/fees/), 0.60%/0.78% is the PUBLIC available_books flat_rate default. Read the authenticated /v3/fees/ endpoint per-account at runtime; do not hardcode either, and do not treat 0.50/0.65 as 'stale.' 2) Fees are volume-tiered and decay — re-pull /v3/fees/ periodically so the gate tracks the real applied rate. 3) Add a spread term to the round-trip cost model; btc_mxn is liquid but spread is non-zero and the recommendation only counts fees. 4) Tax/legal specifics are from secondary sources — verify against SAT/CNBV/DOF or a Mexican accountant before relying on the ~60k MXN exemption or CARF claims. 5) Security stance verified achievable on Bitso (no-withdrawal + IP allowlist) — adopt it.

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the validation-ladder largely as written (read-only assert no-withdrawal -> ~10-20 MXN post-only test order -> real ~195-200 MXN buy), with two corrections: (a) treat the 0.60%/0.78% fee figures as UNVERIFIED — read the authenticated /fees endpoint on first connect to get your actual tier (likely ~0.50% maker / ~0.65% taker base); (b) decide post-only vs marketable-limit deliberately — post-only guarantees maker fee but can FAIL TO FILL if marketable (it refuses to take liquidity), so for the real buy either accept it may rest unfilled and re-quote, or use a price-capped marketable limit (taker, but deterministic fill). The 10 MXN floor and post-only support are verified live/current.
verdicts.assessment: Core mechanical claims survive refutation against LIVE 2026 sources. (1) 10 MXN floor: VERIFIED via live Bitso public API /v3/available_books -> btc_mxn min_value=10, min_amount=0.0000006 (a web snippet claiming 25 MXN was stale; live API is authoritative). (2) post-only LIMIT: VERIFIED — Bitso docs list time_in_force=postonly for limit orders. (3) Trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted key: VERIFIED — Bitso exposes separate permission checkboxes (Place orders / View balances / View account info / Make withdrawals), so the blast-radius-containment stance is fully implementable; this is the strongest part. (4) Bitso is a CNBV/Banxico-regulated MX entity; self-KYC registration by an individual is real and not a blocker. DEFECTS FOUND: (a) Fee figures 0.60% maker / 0.78% taker are NOT corroborated — maker/taker are absent from available_books (tier/authenticated), and secondary sources report MXN base tier nearer 0.50%/0.65%; the exact numbers look stale and should be downgraded and read live from the authenticated /fees endpoint. (b) Conceptual conflation: 'marketable/post-only' are mutually exclusive — a post-only order that would be marketable is rejected/repriced, so it can fail to fill; fine for a place/sign TEST, but the real 200 MXN buy needs an explicit fill-vs-fee decision. (c) Minor: there is no self-describing endpoint that returns a key's own permission scopes; 'assert NO withdrawal' is enforced by config discipline + confirming a withdrawal call is rejected, not a read. None of these break the ladder; they are precision fixes. Tax/legal: the recommendation makes no tax claim so nothing to refute there, and a single BUY is not a taxable disposal under MX ISR anyway; the broader crypto-tax treatment (enajenacion de bienes, progressive ISR, 2026 CARF reporting) rests on secondary sources only and should carry a confirm-with-SAT/contador caveat before any SELL.
verdicts.caveats: Fee figures 0.60%/0.78% are unverified and likely stale — confirm via authenticated /fees on first connect (do not hardcode). Post-only is not guaranteed to fill; pick post-only (maker, may rest) OR price-capped marketable limit (taker, deterministic) for the real buy with eyes open. Key-permission self-assertion is by discipline, not a scope-read endpoint — verify by confirming a withdrawal call returns rejected. Tax treatment on eventual SELL rests on secondary sources (beincrypto/contarito/etc.), not a SAT primary page I could pin this pass — confirm with SAT/contador before disposing; a BUY alone is not taxable. At ~11 USD, even ~0.65% taker is ~1.3 MXN round-trip plus spread — material drag but acceptable for a one-time validation trade.

verdicts.recommendation: Owner + Codebase: for trade #1 do ONE single post-only maker buy of ONE top-cap allowlisted asset (btc_mxn or eth_mxn) directly in its MXN book, then HOLD. Do NOT split across assets, do NOT route MXN->USDT->asset, do NOT wire a rebalancer. Pin the allowlist to BTC/ETH only.
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation survives adversarial verification and is current for a Mexico-based individual in 2026. CONFIRMED: (1) Bitso is the correct platform - its subsidiary NVIO Pagos Mexico is CNBV-authorized as an IFPE under the Ley Fintech, so a solo individual can legally register/KYC and trade his own funds. (2) Native btc_mxn / eth_mxn MXN books exist (the cited API doc is genuine); btc_mxn minimum order value is 5 MXN, so a 200 MXN order clears the floor ~40x. (3) The single-leg cost thesis is right: Bitso's own docs confirm the simple app applies a hidden SPREAD while Bitso Alpha / the API books use maker-taker, so MXN->USDT->BTC genuinely pays the book fee twice - direct btc_mxn is correct. (4) Post-only is executable: the place-order API supports time_in_force=post_only (default goodtillcancelled). (5) Trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted keys are fully supported (grant Place Orders without Make Withdrawals + set Allowed IPs), so the policy-engine blast-radius stance is implementable as written. (6) BUY-then-HOLD is also tax-optimal: under LISR, buying/holding crypto is NOT a taxable event - only enajenacion (sell/swap/pay) is - so avoiding the USDT hop also avoids a needless crypto-to-crypto taxable disposition, and Article 93 LISR exempts ~128,383 MXN/yr of disposition gain (3x annual UMA) for individuals, dwarfing an 11 USD position.

CORRECTIONS / DOWNGRADE REASONS (why do-with-care, not do-now): (A) The codebase understates fees by ~5x. SimExecutor uses fee_rate=0.001 (0.10%), but real tier-0 maker on btc_mxn is 0.50% (taker 0.65%); 0.10% maker only exists at high 30-day volume. Round-trip cost at 11 USD is ~1.0% maker / ~1.3% taker plus spread. The backtest cost gate (Deflated Sharpe) and SimExecutor must be re-parameterized to 0.005 maker BEFORE any live go-decision, or the strategy is being validated against a fantasy cost model. The recommendation's direction is right; the magnitude in code is wrong. (B) Tax/legal framing rests on consistent SECONDARY sources (Article 30 Ley Fintech = virtual assets; enajenacion de bienes treatment; Article 93 LISR exemption; CARF/CRS 2026 exchange reporting to SAT), corroborated by Bitso's own blog and the Diputados PDF of the Ley Fintech, but not a live SAT primary ruling on crypto. The frequently-cited 20% provisional withholding on the operation amount is secondary-sourced and should be confirmed with a contador - though it applies to a SELL, so it does not block trade #1 (a buy). (C) Operational gap: a post-only buy may never fill (it rests on the book); that is SAFE but means trade #1 may require re-pricing or a deliberate taker fallback - the recommendation should state this.
verdicts.caveats: Before going live: (1) Re-parameterize SimExecutor fee_rate to 0.005 (0.50% tier-0 maker) and re-run the cost-aware backtest gate; the current 0.001 understates real Bitso fees 5x. (2) Add post-only no-fill handling (re-price or explicit taker fallback) - a resting maker buy can silently never execute. (3) Tax claims are secondary-sourced (Bitso blog, Diputados Ley Fintech PDF, multiple guides) - confirm the 20% provisional-withholding-on-sale detail with a contador/SAT before the FIRST SELL (does not affect the buy). (4) CARF/CRS means SAT receives Bitso balances in 2026 - keep records even below the Article 93 exemption. (5) Use a TRADE-only, NO-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key (Bitso supports all three) - never a full-permission key.

verdicts.recommendation: Build a single stdlib-only BitsoExecutor implementing the existing Executor Protocol; sign with hmac+hashlib (nonce+method+path+body, hex, "Bitso key:nonce:signature"); no CCXT. Sound in principle, but verify the exact API host and the stage/paper path live before coding.
verdicts.assessment: The core engineering claim survives adversarial scrutiny. VERIFIED TRUE: (1) Bitso auth is plain HMAC-SHA256 over the concatenation nonce + HTTP method + requestPath + JSON body, hex-encoded, header "Bitso {key}:{nonce}:{signature}" — fully implementable with Python stdlib (hmac, hashlib, json, urllib), no third-party crypto needed. (2) "Serialize the JSON body ONCE and sign the exact bytes sent" is not just nice-to-have, it is REQUIRED — the signed body must byte-match the sent body or Bitso rejects it. (3) The security stance is achievable on Bitso: API key permissions are granular — "Place orders" is independent of "Make withdrawals", withdrawals can be address-allowlisted, AND there is an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses ("Restrict use of API addresses"). So TRADE-ONLY + NO-WITHDRAWAL + IP-ALLOWLISTED keys are real and the right configuration. (4) The Executor Protocol in the repo is execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill (verified in C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\executor.py) so a BitsoExecutor is a genuine drop-in. (5) "Do NOT add CCXT" is defensible for a stdlib-only/local-first/auditable invariant: for one signed POST, CCXT's requests+async+compiled-wheel dependency tree is a disproportionate, harder-to-audit blast radius. REFUTATIONS / WHY NOT do-now: (A) URL hardcoding is unsafe. Bitso's own docs conflict — the testing-setup page documents production https://bitso.com/api/v3/ and testing https://stage.bitso.com/api/v3/, but general-concepts and the wider ecosystem use https://api.bitso.com/api/v3/ (prod) and https://api-sandbox.bitso.com/api/v3/ (sandbox), NOT stage.bitso.com. Because the signed requestPath must include /api/v3/... and the host must be the one the keys actually validate against, the recommendation's hardcoded base_url=https://bitso.com / https://stage.bitso.com must be curl-probed live first; do not trust either constant blind. (B) "Same class = PaperExecutor(stage)" overstates frictionlessness: the stage/testing environment has no SMS, requires contacting Bitso's Developer Support Desk for phone verification and PIN, and docs reference dead testnets (Ropsten). The class-reuse design is correct; the claim that paper-mode is a trivial URL swap is optimistic — it needs a manually provisioned testing account. (C) This pivots away from the repo's own executor.py docstring, which names Kraken paper mode + Coinbase AgentKit as the planned swap-ins. Bitso is actually the better choice for a Mexico-resident individual (MXN-native; regulated via affiliate NVIO Pagos Mexico, a CNBV-authorized IFPE), but it supersedes a previously stated plan and should be acknowledged. (D) Cost drag context the executor must encode: Bitso MXN-market taker fee at the lowest-volume tier is 0.65% (maker 0.50%) PER SIDE, so a round trip is ~1.3% in fees before spread. SimExecutor's default fee_rate=0.001 (0.1%) understates this ~6.5x; the BitsoExecutor/paper config must use 0.65% taker or paper results will mislead. Also order minimums: general $10 minimum per order, BTC/MXN minimum_value 5 MXN — feasible at 200 MXN but a real constraint the policy/agent must respect. (E) The recommendation makes NO tax/legal claim, so nothing to refute there directly; but for the broader Phase-3, all Mexican tax claims I could find come from SECONDARY sources only (bleap/mexc/beincrypto/cronista) — crypto as "Activo Virtual" under Ley Fintech, gains trigger ISR up to 35%, crypto-to-crypto/stablecoin = taxable enajenacion, ~3x-annual-UMA enajenacion exemption. I could NOT confirm these from SAT/CNBV primary text, so any tax statement must be downgraded to "verify with SAT/a contador."
verdicts.caveats: Before writing code, curl-probe to settle two things: (1) which production host validates your trade-only key — https://bitso.com/api/v3/ vs https://api.bitso.com/api/v3/ — and whether the sandbox is stage.bitso.com or api-sandbox.bitso.com; make base_url a config constant set from that probe, not a hardcoded literal, and ensure _sign() signs the path WITH the /api/v3 prefix exactly as sent. (2) Confirm the stage/testing account is actually obtainable (it needs Bitso Developer Support contact, no SMS) before promising PaperExecutor=stage as a clean swap; if stage is impractical, fall back to a PaperExecutor that uses live read-only endpoints (public order book) for fills without placing real orders. Set the Bitso key to Place-orders + View-balances ONLY (no Make-withdrawals, no Security-actions), enable the IP allowlist, and run the read-only sequence (balance, available_books, ticker) before any test order. Configure the executor fee at 0.65% taker (not 0.1%) and enforce book minimums (>= $10 / >= minimum_value). Validate end-to-end on stage/read-only first; the first real order should be a single tiny market BUY near the book minimum, manually observed. Treat all Mexican tax handling as unverified until checked against SAT primary guidance or a contador — do not bake any tax assumption into code or claims. Confirm KYC tier on the live Bitso account permits API trading at this size.

verdicts.recommendation: Create the Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' (+ 'View balances') enabled; leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' OFF; add the executor host's fixed egress IP to the 5-IP allowlist. Then deliberately trigger 0202 (attempt a withdrawal call) and 0213 (call from an off-allowlist IP) once during read-only validation to PROVE the blast radius holds.
verdicts.assessment:
CORE CLAIMS VERIFIED AGAINST PRIMARY SOURCE (Bitso docs, 2026-current). The five API-key permissions are independent toggles exactly as claimed: Place orders, View balances, View account information, Perform security actions ("modifying your account settings"), Make withdrawals (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). IP allowlist ("Restrict use of API addresses", up to 5 IPs) is a separate independent control. Error 0202 = "API key is not authorized to execute the requested method"; error 0213 = "IP address is not allowed to access this resource" (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/authentication-error-02-http-401). The blast-radius logic is SOUND and actually understated: a withdrawal-enabled key with NO destination allowlist can withdraw to ANY address, so leaving Make withdrawals OFF is the correct containment. Bitso is CNBV-registered (ITF/IFPE via NVIO) and crypto trading is legal for Mexican individuals (Ley Fintech "activos virtuales"). The 0202/0213 trigger-to-prove tactic is legitimate positive containment verification, not assumption.

THREE PROBLEMS, ONE BLOCKING. (1) BLOCKER for THIS owner: the recommendation assumes a "fixed egress IP" exists. Project memory repeatedly shows his backend runs on a RESIDENTIAL Mexican IP (home PC), which is typically DYNAMIC. A dynamic egress IP will intermittently throw 0213 and silently kill the executor mid-run. The recommendation does not flag this. He must egress through a genuinely static IP — he already owns the Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28) which is the natural fix — before the allowlist is safe. (2) Cost drag under-acknowledged: ~$11 USD barely clears Bitso's ~$10 minimum trade; taker fee on MXN pairs is 0.65% plus spread (~1.3%+ round-trip). Harmless for a path-validation trade (loss is cents) but useless as a profitability signal — acceptable since Phase-3 only validates the path. (3) The 0202 test must confirm the response is genuinely 0202 (permission) and not masked by 0213 (off-allowlist) or a 0400 validation error, or the containment proof is hollow. Tax is not claimed by the recommendation so not refuted, but the Phase-3 framing's tax figures (esp. a "20% provisional withholding") are secondary-source and conflict with the 2.5% digital-platforms regime; downgrade any tax claim to verify-with-accountant. At $11 tax is immaterial, but CARF reporting began 2026-01-01.
verdicts.caveats: VERIFY LIVE before relying on the allowlist: confirm the executor's actual egress IP is STATIC (route through the Netcup VPS 152.53.167.28, not the residential home IP) or the key will intermittently 0213 and the agent will silently stop trading. Sequence the validation so the 0213 test (off-allowlist call) is run knowingly and the 0202 test (withdrawal call from an ON-allowlist IP) returns a clean permission error, not an IP error — otherwise the containment proof is ambiguous. Tax treatment (ISR enajenacion de bienes, ~128k MXN/3x-UMA exemption, the disputed 20% vs 2.5% withholding) is from secondary sources only; do not present as settled — confirm with a Mexican accountant / SAT primary text before any real-money scale-up. Fees (0.65% taker + spread) make the $11 trade economically meaningless except as a plumbing test, which is fine for Phase 3.

verdicts.recommendation: Extend Intent with order_type/limit_price/post_only/quote_amount and add a min-notional policy check sourced from GET /available_books.
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation holds up on its core claims. VERIFIED IN CODE: Intent (treasuryforge/types.py) carries only symbol/side/base_amount/reason — no order_type, limit_price, post_only, or quote_amount; and policy.py (treasuryforge/policy.py) has a per-tx notional CAP (upper bound, lines 108-113) but no MIN-notional rule. So the stated gap is real: the system cannot today express a maker order or a fixed-MXN buy that a live exchange requires. MARKET default + base_amount preserves the existing SimExecutor.execute signature and the 55-test surface, so the "keeps tests valid" claim is credible.

VERIFIED AGAINST BITSO (current 2026): POST /orders supports type=market|limit and side=buy|sell; for MARKET orders you may specify amount via major OR minor (not both); GET /available_books returns minimum_amount (major units) and minimum_value (minor units). Both cited source URLs are real and accurate. A ~200 MXN trade clears Bitso's ~1 USD platform minimum, so the min-notional rule is genuinely useful, not theoretical. Security stance is fully supported: Bitso keys separate "Place orders" from "Make withdrawals" and offer an IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs), so trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted is achievable and matches the policy engine's blast-radius intent.

DEFECTS FOUND (refutation, non-blocking): (1) The proposed standalone post_only: bool does NOT map to a Bitso parameter — Bitso has no post_only field; maker-only is time_in_force="postonly" (set alongside goodtillcancelled/fillorkill/immediateorcancel). The bool is a slightly leaky abstraction the executor must translate. (2) The rec says quote_amount maps to Bitso minor, which is correct ONLY for market orders — a LIMIT order requires major+price, so quote_amount+LIMIT is an illegal combination the data model should reject/guard. (3) Tax/legal context (SAT ISR on sells/trades, CNBV/Ley Fintech) is sourced only to secondary blogs here, not a primary CNBV/SAT citation — but this recommendation makes no tax claim and is pure code, so that does not downgrade it.

SCOPE: This is a code-only data-model + policy-rule change. It touches no live money, no API key, and no network call, so it is safe to implement now. It is NOT itself the live trade and must not be conflated with it.
verdicts.caveats: do-with-care, not do-now, for two reasons: (a) model post_only as time_in_force (or have the executor translate post_only->"postonly"), since a raw bool does not exist in the Bitso API; (b) add a guard rejecting quote_amount combined with LIMIT (Bitso minor is market-only; limit needs major+price). Also keep the min-notional rule reading minimum_value live from GET /available_books rather than hardcoding, since those values change. Verify the post_only/time_in_force and minor-vs-major mapping against a live read-only API probe before the first real order. Bitso requires a ~100 USD initial deposit to open/fund the account even though the per-trade minimum is ~1 USD.

verdicts.recommendation: Executor Implementation: 3-state place() (CONFIRMED/REJECTED/UNKNOWN) with reconcile-before-retry; journal origin_id before POST; on UNKNOWN reconcile against trade history before any bounded retry; halt to UNRESOLVED if inconclusive; never blind-retry; never rely on origin_id alone.
verdicts.assessment: Every load-bearing factual claim verified against Bitso's current (2026) PRIMARY docs and holds for an individual in Mexico. (1) origin_id uniqueness is scoped to ACTIVE orders only — confirmed verbatim in Bitso changelog dated 2025-05-27, so a near-instant fill frees the id and a timeout+blind-retry CAN double-buy. (2) Look Up Orders (GET /orders) returns ONLY open/partial orders, with merely a ~1-hour window to see terminal ones — confirmed verbatim, so 'not found' does NOT prove non-execution; cross-checking trade history is mandatory. (3) Place an Order returns only an oid with NO documented idempotency/timeout guarantee — confirmed, which is exactly why a client-side reconcile is required (strengthens the rec). (4) The security stance is feasible: a 'Place orders' (trade-only) permission also grants reading your trades and open orders, withdrawals are a SEPARATE toggle (leave OFF), and an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses is supported — so reconciliation works on a no-withdrawal, IP-pinned key, exactly as the policy-engine blast-radius design wants. This is correctly the single most important correctness/safety control. BUT adversarial review found two real defects in the rec AS WRITTEN. Defect A: it lists GET /orders?origin_ids as a co-equal reconcile source; that endpoint is the WEAK one (loses terminal orders after ~1h).
