The authoritative, time-UNBOUNDED reconcile is GET /order_trades?origin_id (List User Trades), which 'retrieves all trades regardless of execution timestamp' and persists after fill — it must be the source of truth, with GET /orders only an in-window early-exit. Defect B: because uniqueness is active-only AND order_trades?origin_id returns ALL trades ever sharing that id, REUSING an origin_id makes reconciliation ambiguous. The correct invariant is stronger than 'never rely on origin_id alone': use a FRESH, never-reused origin_id per logical attempt; the 'journal before POST' step must mint a new id, never reuse on retry. Context caveats outside the rec's scope but material to Phase-3: MXN-market taker fee is 0.780% lowest tier (~1.56% round-trip) so use the order-book place-an-order path, NOT Quick Buy/Convert (worse spread); btc_mxn minimum_amount is ~0.00005 BTC (~100 MXN), so a 200 MXN order clears the minimum at ~2x and is NOT blocked (one stale search claimed 0.005 BTC — the API available_books.minimum_amount is authoritative). No regulatory/security blocker for a Mexico individual: KYC needs INE/CURP/RFC and biometric CURP is mandatory from Feb 2026 (friction, not a blocker).
verdicts.caveats: TAX/LEGAL CLAIMS DOWNGRADED: crypto disposal being an ISR taxable event for personas fisicas (progressive to 35%), SAT treating crypto as intangible movable property, the ~128,383 MXN annual enajenacion exemption, and CARF data collection starting 2026-01-01 all come from crypto-tax BLOGS (MEXC, BeInCrypto, Contarito), NOT a primary SAT/CNBV citation — treat as directional only and confirm with SAT/a contador before relying on them. They do not affect the executor recommendation. Two fixes REQUIRED before coding: (A) make GET /order_trades?origin_id (List User Trades) the authoritative reconcile source-of-truth, demote GET /orders to an optional <1h fast-path; (B) enforce a fresh, never-reused origin_id per attempt to keep order_trades reconciliation unambiguous. Sources verified: docs.bitso.com history-of-changes, look-up-orders, place-an-order, list-user-trades, 2-generate-your-api-credentials; bitso.com/fees. Build the executor against the order-book Place an Order endpoint (not Convert).

verdicts.recommendation: Executor Implementation: POST /api/v3/orders then poll by oid to build a VWAP+summed-fee Fill, add a resting/unfilled outcome, use REST polling (Bitso has no private user-data websocket).
verdicts.assessment: Core claims VERIFIED against primary Bitso docs and the actual codebase. (1) POST /api/v3/orders returns only {success, payload.oid} with no price/fee — confirmed verbatim at docs.bitso.com/place-an-order. (2) Bitso has NO private user-data websocket; all three WS channels (Trades, Orders, Diff-Orders) are PUBLIC market data, so REST polling for own-order status is the only path. (3) Post-Only/maker orders genuinely rest unfilled on the book, so the explicit resting/unfilled outcome distinct from Fill is necessary and real. (4) Reading types.py:53-79 and executor.py:21,29 confirms Fill is single-price/single-fee and SimExecutor.execute always returns exactly one Fill; live fills are partial/multi-trade, so VWAP aggregation + summed fee is genuinely required to preserve the 'Fill is the only thing that mutates the wallet' invariant. (5) The security stance (trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key) is fully achievable: Bitso has granular permissions where 'Place orders' is separate from 'Make withdrawals', plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses. Nothing here blocks a Mexico-based individual; Bitso is the regulated domestic venue (NVIO Pagos Mexico is CNBV-authorized as an IFPE under the Ley Fintech). The recommendation makes no tax/legal claim itself, so no primary-source downgrade applies to it. THREE correctness gaps prevent verbatim implementation: (a) WRONG ENDPOINT — the rec says poll 'order/trades by oid' citing look-up-orders, but GET /orders/{oid} per the docs 'only retrieves open or partially filled orders; completed orders won't appear except within a one-hour window', so an instantly-filled market order can be missed; the robust fill source is GET /user_trades (filterable by book, returns per-trade price, fees_amount, fees_currency, oid, tid, with no one-hour limit). Reconcile fills via user_trades keyed by oid; use order status only to detect resting/unfilled. (b) FEE CURRENCY — Bitso returns fees_currency and can charge the fee in the asset received (crypto on a BUY), but Fill.fee is typed as 'quote paid in fees'; blindly summing fees across trades will corrupt the wallet when fee currency differs from quote — normalize first. (c) RATE LIMIT — 'every 2-5s, capped' is a sensible heuristic but Bitso publishes no numeric private-endpoint limit (only HTTP 420 throttling errors); treat the cap as defensive and handle 420 with backoff.
verdicts.caveats: Implement against GET /user_trades (not look-up-orders) as the canonical fill source, keyed by oid, because the order-lookup endpoint drops completed orders after one hour. Normalize fees_currency before summing into Fill.fee (Bitso may charge the fee in base, not quote). Treat the 2-5s poll cap as defensive and add HTTP-420 backoff. Verify post_only parameter syntax live before relying on maker-only behavior. For the first real ~200 MXN trade this is safe (blast radius contained by the no-withdrawal IP-allowlisted key + policy engine), but the executor needs a live read-only -> tiny-order validation pass before trusting the fill-reconciliation path. Note the trade itself is a SAT-taxable enajenacion under LISR; that is outside this code recommendation's scope but relevant to the broader Phase-3 plan.

verdicts.recommendation: Add executor.probe()/healthcheck() (signed read-only GET balance/fees, assert HTTP 200 + no auth errors) as a precondition to execute(); go-live = stage rung -> prod read-only probe -> one ~200 MXN marketable-limit+IOC order with capped slippage. [Theme: Executor Implementation]
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED CORE, TWO REAL GAPS. The technical spine is real and current for a Mexico-based individual on Bitso (2026): (1) Auth is HMAC-SHA256 + nonce — confirmed (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/authentication). (2) "Place orders" is a SEPARATE permission from "Make withdrawals"; read-only keys (View balances + View account info) are constructible; IP allowlist supports up to 5 addresses — so the TRADE-ONLY / NO-withdrawal / IP-allowlist security stance is fully supported (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). (3) Limit order with time_in_force=immediateorcancel is real — marketable-limit+IOC with a capped price is the correct, conservative first-order type (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/place-an-order). (4) Error 0201 = "Check your credentials" is a genuine auth-error code; a read probe will also surface 0213 "IP address is not allowed" and nonce/signature errors (0206/0207/0215) — confirmed (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/authentication-error-02-http-401). The read-before-write isolation logic is sound: a read call has zero blast radius and de-risks the first peso.

REFUTATIONS / GAPS: (a) COVERAGE GAP — a read-only probe exercises View permissions + IP-allowlist + nonce/signature, but does NOT exercise the "Place orders" scope. The trade-permission bug surfaces as error 0202 ("API key is not authorized to execute the requested method"), which appears ONLY on the first real POST. So the claim that the read probe "isolates auth from execution" overstates coverage for TRADE-permission validation. Fix: on STAGE, also fire a tiny IOC limit at an unfillable price to provoke 0202 if the scope is wrong, without filling. (b) STALE DETAIL — step (1) says "stage rung with testnet funds." Stage exists and accepts dummy data, but the legacy crypto-testnet funding (Ropsten) is dead since 2022; the specific "testnet funds" mechanism is unconfirmed-current and must be verified live, not asserted. (c) COST DRAG (out of this rec's scope but relevant to go-live): MXN-market taker fee at zero-volume tier is ~0.65%, plus adverse slippage from the IOC cap -> round-trip drag ~1.3%+ on ~200 MXN; $10 platform minimum is cleared but with thin margin, and a partial IOC fill could land near the minimum. (d) REGULATORY/TAX: No blocker for an individual — Bitso operates under LFPIORPI vulnerable-activities reporting (not full Fintech-Law crypto authorization, which is normal in MX). Tax treatment (crypto = enajenacion de bienes, LISR art. 141, all gains taxable) is only from SECONDARY sources (beincrypto/contarito) — NOT primary CNBV/SAT — so any tax claim in the broader plan must be downgraded until confirmed against SAT/LISR directly. This recommendation makes no tax claim, so it is unaffected.
verdicts.caveats: 1) Add a STAGE-only trade-permission probe (unfillable IOC limit) to catch error 0202, since the read-only probe cannot validate the Place-orders scope — without this the first PROD order is the first test of trade permission. 2) Do not hardcode "testnet funds" for stage; verify the current stage funding mechanism live (Ropsten is dead). 3) The cited place-an-order URL supports the IOC order type but not a literal "/fees" healthcheck endpoint name — use real Bitso balance/fee read endpoints for the probe. 4) Surface expected fee+slippage (~1.3%+ round trip at 200 MXN) before the order, and guard against partial IOC fills dropping under the $10 minimum. 5) Tax/legal claims in the surrounding Phase-3 plan need primary CNBV/SAT/LISR sourcing before being relied on. Relevant code: C:\Users\DANNY\Desktop\treasuryforge\treasuryforge\executor.py (the Executor Protocol where probe()/healthcheck() must be added alongside execute()).

verdicts.recommendation:
Adopt the monotonic nonce signer but fix three rationale defects first. One: error 0201 is Check your credentials, not a non increasing nonce; the nonce codes are 0206 and 0207 and the signature error is 0215, so do not let the wrong label leak into runtime block detection. Two: the nonce needs the recommended six digit salt giving fourteen to nineteen digits, not a bare millisecond value. Three: use the order book place order endpoint at about half a percent, not the basic swap path at one and a half to two percent which dominates an eleven dollar trade, and make the key trade only, no withdrawal, and IP allowlisted, which Bitso makes mandatory.</parameter>
<parameter name="caveats">verify-with-SAT
verdicts.assessment:
The signer engineering is correct and current for a Mexico individual on Bitso in 2026, verified against the three cited pages: unique increasing nonce, HMAC SHA256 over nonce method path body hex, signed body equals sent body, no clock skew window, version one deprecated November 2025. The journal plus process lock plus known vector test is the right hedge. The three defects are the wrong rejection error code, the understated nonce salt, and the omitted mandatory IP allowlist and swap versus order book cost gap that dominates an eleven dollar trade. Bitso is a CNBV regulated ITF under the 2018 Fintech Law and individual KYC is legal so there is no blocker. Crypto disposals are ISR taxable but the sixty thousand peso exemption is only secondary sourced, not a SAT or CNBV primary document, so verify with SAT. The executor is SimExecutor only today so this signer work is net new on the documented swap point.</parameter>
</invoke>

verdicts.recommendation: Adopt the Bitso error taxonomy as written, but DO-WITH-CARE: (1) resolve the platform contradiction first — Phase-2 chose Kraken for live money, this recommendation silently swaps to Bitso. The swap is defensible (Bitso is the right venue for a Mexico/MXN individual), but it must be an explicit Phase-3 decision, not an implicit one buried in an executor PR. (2) Treat the taxonomy as the runtime-error layer ONLY; it does not cover the security stance (trade-only/no-withdraw/IP-allowlist key scoping), which is the actual blast-radius control and must be built separately. (3) Soften two halt branches per the caveats below.
verdicts.assessment: Every load-bearing TECHNICAL claim verified against Bitso's own primary docs and is current (2026): HTTP 420 is the throttling status (confirmed, not 429) with codes 0801 "hit the request rate-limit" / 0802 "too many attempts"; the 24h-ban escalation is real and first-party ("Continuous one-minute lockouts might result in a 24-hour block"); rate limits are 60 RPM public / 300 RPM private (KYC-verified), order cancellations exempt; 0379 = "Insufficient balance" (non-retryable, correct); 0377 = "Order matched, but trade records still being processed" and 0378 = "Order has not been matched yet" — two DISTINCT pending states where resubmit genuinely double-fills (the strongest part of the recommendation); 0206 = "Invalid nonce type", 0207 = "Invalid nonce value" (correctly NONCE-retryable); 0201/0215 AUTH_FATAL correct; HTTP 401/400/420 category dispatch matches docs. Bitso explicitly recommends parsing decimal numbers as strings to avoid truncation — so "Decimal not float" is vendor-endorsed, not just good practice. Branch-on-error.code over message text is sound given Bitso's documented frequent code additions (history-of-changes page). The Mexico fit is strong: Bitso is genuinely CNBV-regulated under the 2018 Fintech Law, GFSC-licensed, never hacked, supports MXN natively, and offers IP-allowlist (up to 5 IPs) plus separable permissions (Place orders / View balances / View account info on the standard key; WITHDRAWALS live on a separate Mint Platform API key) — so the no-withdrawal trade-only stance is achievable. WHAT DOESN'T FULLY HOLD: (a) Platform mismatch — Phase-2 DISCOVERY.md and README both name Kraken as the live-money rung and Coinbase/Base as the on-chain path; Bitso appears NOWHERE in prior phases. This recommendation introduces a new venue without flagging the reversal. (b) Scope creep risk — the item is framed as 'Executor Implementation' but only covers HTTP error handling; the actual first-trade blast-radius controls (key scoping, IP allowlist config, read-only-then-tiny-order validation sequence) are NOT in it. (c) 0211 is specifically the IP-allowlist/expired-token rejection ('Invalid IP or expired Token'), so the alert text should say 'check IP whitelist' not generic 'auth fatal' — lumping it into AUTH_FATAL loses the actionable signal. (d) Known ccxt issue #24419 shows Bitso intermittently returns 0215 'Incorrect request signature' on VALID signatures for some private methods — blanket-halting on 0215 could cause spurious halts; halting is the safe direction so this is a caveat not a defect. No regulatory/tax/cost problem is introduced by the taxonomy itself (it's pure error handling); the Mexico tax exposure — SAT taxes crypto disposals as income up to 35%, CARF auto-reporting from Bitso to SAT 2026-2027, ~USD 3,500 'vulnerable activity' reporting threshold — is a separate Phase-3 workstream untouched by this item and irrelevant at an 11-USD test size, but the auto-reporting means the trade is NOT invisible to SAT. Sources are authoritative (Bitso's own docs.bitso.com pages) and all three cited URLs resolve and support the claims.
verdicts.caveats: PRIMARY: This silently overrides Phase-2's Kraken live-money decision — get an explicit owner sign-off on Bitso-as-first-venue before writing executor code against it. SECONDARY: the taxonomy is the right runtime layer but is NOT the first-trade safety mechanism — key scoping (trade-only, no-withdraw via keeping withdrawals on the separate Mint key, IP-allowlist) and the read-only->tiny-order validation sequence are the blast-radius controls and remain unbuilt. THREE SOFTENINGS: (1) give 0211 its own 'IP/token expired — check allowlist' alert, don't fold into generic AUTH_FATAL; (2) on 0215 do not hard-latch on first occurrence — log loudly, but the documented intermittent-false-signature ccxt bug means a one-shot resign-and-retry before halting is safer than instant kill (still fail-closed if it persists); (3) the 60s exponential backoff floor is correct but pair it with a request-rate budget that stays UNDER 300 RPM by construction so you never approach the 0801 path in the first place — and exploit that cancellations are rate-limit-exempt for the dead-man-switch heartbeat. At 11 USD, Bitso spread/fee drag (maker/taker ~0.5-0.65% each side + book spread) is the dominant cost and is NOT addressed here — separate item.

verdicts.recommendation: Build the live/paper executor to place orders ONLY via the Pro/Alpha order-book API endpoints (Kraken Pro AddOrder / Bitso Alpha POST /orders), and hard-code the instant-buy/convert path as a forbidden route. THEME: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax.
verdicts.assessment:
CORE CLAIM HOLDS, and it is current for a Mexico-based individual in 2026. I tried to refute it and could not break the central engineering claim; I did find the theme's tax framing is unsupported by what the recommendation actually asserts, and one part of the recommendation is partly a no-op.

WHAT VERIFIED (refutation failed):
1. Fee direction is correct and current. Kraken official fee schedule (kraken.com/features/fee-schedule): base spot Pro = 0.25% maker / 0.40% taker; instant-buy on the Kraken app = 1.0%-1.5% (support.kraken.com/articles/360030303832). Bitso official fees (bitso.com/fees) confirm order-book taker fees: USDC/USDT 0.36% taker, MXN pairs 0.78% taker, BTC 0.098% taker. The recommendation's stated 0.36%-0.78% order-book vs 1.0%-1.5% instant range matches the primary fee pages almost exactly. The app spread (1.5%-2.0% hidden) is corroborated by secondary reviews; Bitso's own fees page does not quantify it (it is embedded by design), which is itself the recommendation's point.
2. Key-controllability claim holds. Bitso docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials) confirm granular permissions: you CAN create a key with 'Place orders' (trade) WITHOUT 'Make withdrawals', plus IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs) and withdrawal-address allowlisting. This satisfies the trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted stance exactly. Kraken offers the same scoping.
3. Mexico availability is NOT blocked. Kraken supports MXN via SPEI (150 MXN min, 0.50% fee); Mexico is not on the 14-country prohibited list. Bitso is Mexico-domiciled. No jurisdiction blocker for a solo individual on either venue.
4. ~11 USD economics. At Bitso USDC/USDT 0.36% taker, a round trip (~0.72%) on 11 USD is ~8 cents; on MXN pairs (0.78%, ~1.56% round trip) ~17 cents. Instant-buy at 1.5%-2.0% spread would be 2-3x that. The absolute drag is tiny but the relative saving (roughly halving-to-thirding cost) is real and free to capture. The order-book choice is correct.

WHERE IT WEAKENS (do-with-care, not do-now):
A. The 'hard-code instant-buy as a forbidden route in the executor' clause is PARTLY A NO-OP / mislabeled defense. On the Bitso PUBLIC trading API there is no instant-buy/convert endpoint at all — POST /orders is order-book only; the app convert-with-spread is a separate consumer-app product not reachable by an API key. Same on Kraken: the public REST AddOrder is order-book; instant-buy is an app/convert product. So the 'forbidden route' guard mostly defends against a path the scoped key cannot reach anyway. It is harmless belt-and-suspenders (good for market-order-type discipline and to forbid any future convert-style endpoint), but the stated rationale ('not controllable via the trade-only key') actually means it is unreachable, not that it is a live risk needing a hard block. Right action, slightly overstated necessity.
B. THE TAX THEME IS UNSUBSTANTIATED RELATIVE TO THE CLAIM — but the recommendation makes NO tax assertion. The title says 'Mexican Tax' yet the recommendation/why/sources are 100% fees+keys; no tax claim is actually made, so there is nothing tax-wise to refute here. Separately, the ambient tax sub-claims floating in this space (crypto = enajenacion de bienes under LISR; UMA-based annual exemption ~128,383 MXN) are SECONDARY-SOURCE ONLY and CONTESTED: one source cites art. 141 LISR and a 3x-UMA exemption, another asserts NO small-amount exemption applies to crypto. SAT has no crypto-specific enajenacion ruling I could confirm from a primary source. Per the skeptical mandate: any tax claim must be DOWNGRADED to 'consult a Mexican accountant/primary SAT guidance before relying on an exemption.' At ~11 USD the realized gain is negligible and almost certainly immaterial, but do not encode any exemption assumption in the system.
C. IP-allowlist operational gotcha (from project history, reconfirmed): the owner's residential MX IP drifts. An IP-allowlisted key will self-DoS unless egress is pinned through the existing Netcup VPS / a static IP. This is a real deployment blocker for the security stance and must be solved before the key is locked down.

NET: The executor SHOULD place orders only via the order-book endpoints — verified true, current, Mexico-viable, and cost-correct. Ship it. Just (1) treat the instant-buy 'forbidden route' as cheap hygiene rather than a critical control, (2) pin a static egress IP before enabling the IP allowlist, and (3) do not attach any tax exemption claim to this recommendation without primary SAT/lawyer confirmation.
verdicts.caveats: CONFIDENCE: High on fees/keys/availability (primary sources: kraken.com fee schedule, bitso.com/fees, docs.bitso.com credentials page, Kraken support fee article). Low/Medium on the tax framing — but the recommendation itself makes no tax claim, so this does not lower the engineering verdict. THREE PRIMARY URLS COULD NOT BE FETCHED (403): the Bitso app-vs-Alpha support article and the beincrypto tax guide; I corroborated their substance via the official bitso.com/fees page and search snapshots instead. KEY UNREFUTED RISKS: (a) IP-allowlist self-DoS on a drifting residential MX IP — pin egress to the Netcup VPS / static IP first; (b) the 'forbidden instant-buy route' is largely defending an unreachable path on the scoped API key (right instinct, overstated as a live control); (c) any UMA/LISR exemption assumption is secondary-source and contested — downgrade to 'verify with primary SAT guidance / a Mexican accountant' and do NOT hard-code it. At ~11 USD the tax gain is immaterial regardless. Verify the exact live taker fee for the specific pair you trade at order time, since tiers can change.

verdicts.recommendation: Use a TRADE-ONLY, NO-WITHDRAWAL, IP-allowlisted Bitso API key; validate read-only (ticker/balances/user_trades/fees) first, then one ~15 MXN BTC/MXN taker order, then scale to ~200 MXN.
verdicts.assessment:
All three load-bearing factual claims verified against primary/live sources, so the core recommendation holds. (1) Bitso BTC_MXN minimum_value = "10" MXN: confirmed LIVE at api.bitso.com/v3/available_books/ (minimum_amount 0.0000006 BTC, minimum_value "10", minimum_price "100000"). Note: an earlier WebSearch snippet claimed 0.005 BTC / minimum_value 5 — that is STALE; the live endpoint is authoritative and says 10 MXN, so a ~15 MXN smoke order clears the floor. (2) Kraken USD cost minimum = 0.50 USD: confirmed verbatim on support.kraken.com (USD 0.5; BTC 0.00002). (3) The security stance is REAL on Bitso, not aspirational: Bitso API keys expose independent permissions ("Place orders" / "View balances" / "View account information" / "Perform security actions" / "Make withdrawals") plus an IP allowlist ("Restrict use of API addresses", 0-5 IPs). So a trade-only, no-withdraw, IP-pinned key is genuinely creatable — the blast-radius containment the policy engine assumes is honored at the exchange layer. KYC for an individual is light (INE/passport + selfie, personal data, <5 min) and does not block a Mexico-based solo trader. The read-only -> tiny-order -> scale sequence is sound and maps cleanly onto the project's Executor Protocol (treasuryforge/executor.py: execute(intent, tick, wallet) -> Fill); a live BitsoExecutor is a drop-in.

CAVEATS / WHAT IT UNDERSTATES OR OMITS:
- REGULATORY NUANCE (omitted, real): Bitso's CNBV authorization is via its subsidiary NVIO as an IFPE (electronic-payment-funds institution), NOT as a CNBV/Banxico-authorized public virtual-asset trading service. Banxico Circular 4/2019 prohibits ITFs/banks from OFFERING crypto operations to the general public (internal ops only, with prior Banxico authorization). Bitso's spot crypto trading therefore operates in a tolerated gray zone, not under an explicit Banxico crypto-trading license. This does not block an individual and does not make using Bitso illegal for the user, but the recommendation's framing ("Bitso ... regulated") overstates the regulatory blessing of the crypto-trading function specifically. Primary basis: Ley Fintech (LRITF) art. 30 + Banxico Circular 4/2019 (diputados.gob.mx / banxico.org.mx).
- TAX CLAIM: only SECONDARY sources (blogs) were available; the bleap.finance / beincrypto guides agree crypto-for-MXN sale = enajenacion de bienes taxable under ISR (LISR Title IV, arts. ~119-128), holding/buying is NOT taxable, and an annual exemption exists — but the sources DISAGREE on the figure (one says fixed 60,000 MXN, another says 3x annual UMA ~128,383 MXN for enajenacion de bienes). I could not retrieve a primary SAT/LISR text to settle this, so DOWNGRADE the tax specifics to "directionally correct, figure unverified." Practical impact at this size is nil: a single ~200 MXN round trip produces a gain measured in centavos, far under any plausible exemption, with no real reporting burden — but the user should NOT rely on the exact exemption number without a primary SAT source or an accountant. Also note CARF/automatic-reporting is tightening for 2026, so on-platform records will be visible to SAT regardless of size.
- COST DRAG (understated, not wrong): at the lowest-volume tier Bitso taker fees run ~0.65-0.75% and the thin local BTC/MXN book adds spread; a full round trip on ~200 MXN can realistically lose ~1.5-2.5% (~3-5 MXN) to fees+spread. The ~15 MXN smoke order's own fee is negligible and its purpose (prove the live fill path) is valid — just don't read the smoke order's economics as representative.
- OPERATIONAL: IP allowlist requires a STABLE egress IP; the user is a residential MX solo builder, so a dynamic ISP IP can silently break the key — pin to a stable IP/VPS or be ready to update the allowlist. The recommendation should make residential-IP stability an explicit pre-check.

Net: the recommendation is true, current (2026), and safe for a first tiny real trade as written. Proceed, but (a) treat the tax exemption figure as unverified pending a primary SAT source, (b) acknowledge the Banxico-4/2019 gray-zone rather than calling Bitso a licensed crypto exchange, and (c) confirm a stable egress IP before relying on the allowlist.
verdicts.caveats: Tax figures from secondary sources only and mutually inconsistent (60,000 MXN vs ~128,383 MXN UMA-based exemption) — primary SAT/LISR not retrieved; downgrade tax specifics. Regulatory framing omits Banxico Circular 4/2019: Bitso crypto trading is tolerated, not Banxico-authorized as a public virtual-asset service (CNBV auth is NVIO/IFPE only). IP allowlist needs a stable egress IP (residential ISP risk). Round-trip fee+spread drag at ~200 MXN ~1.5-2.5%, understated by the recommendation's smoke-order framing.

verdicts.recommendation: Add a crash-safe, per-fill, per-lot FIFO tax ledger to the executor/journal NOW (theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax).
verdicts.assessment: Core action holds up and is well-supported. (1) Crypto disposals incl. crypto-to-crypto and crypto-to-stablecoin SWAPS are taxable 'enajenacion de bienes' valued in MXN — unanimously confirmed (beincrypto, contarito, waltio, MEXC). (2) The 5-year record-retention is real and anchors to a PRIMARY source: CFF Art. 30 (leyes-mx/SAT), with extension to 6 yrs / acts-with-prolonged-effects in special cases. (3) The exemption is real and anchors to LISR Art. 93 fr. XIX inciso b) (~3x annual UMA); record-keeping is exactly what proves it. (4) The ledger fields proposed (UTC ts, acquisition month, pair, qty, MXN cost, fee+currency, exchange order/trade ID for reconciliation) are sound and bolt cleanly onto the existing Fill dataclass + wallet.apply_fill seam in treasuryforge\executor.py — zero blast radius, no API-key/withdrawal/network surface, no cost at ~11 USD. Under-recording is the only risk; recording your own data never creates a regulatory/security problem. TWO JUSTIFICATION CLAIMS ARE OVERSTATED AND SHOULD BE DOWNGRADED, though neither breaks the action: (a) CARF TIMING — the 'why' says 'From 2026 CARF makes the owner ledger reconcilable against SAT data.' Authoritative OECD-cohort sourcing places MEXICO IN THE 2028 COHORT (data collection by Jan 1 2027, first automatic exchange 2028), NOT the 48-jurisdiction 2026-collect/2027-exchange first wave. The reconciliation pressure is genuine (and SAT has cross-matched Bitso since 2021 / Binance since 2023 independently of CARF) but arrives later than stated; downgrade '2026 CARF' to 'SAT cross-matching today + CARF ~2027-2028.' (b) 'FIFO is THE audit-safe accepted method' is half-true: SAT has NO crypto-specific costing rule; Spanish local sources (contarito, beincrypto) say SAT accepts PEPS (FIFO) OR costo promedio — pick one and stay consistent. FIFO is a defensible accepted choice, not uniquely mandated. Also MISSING from the design: LISR Art. 124 INPC inflation adjustment of cost basis (the acquisition-month field enables it, so it is an enhancement note not a blocker).
verdicts.caveats: Implement with these corrections: (1) Do NOT hard-code FIFO as the only path — persist raw immutable per-lot data (qty, MXN cost, acquisition date/month, fee, trade ID) so either PEPS or costo-promedio AND the LISR Art. 124 INPC adjustment can be computed later by an accountant; FIFO can be the default report view. (2) Soften the 'why' text: replace 'From 2026 CARF' with the accurate timeline (SAT already cross-matches exchange data; Mexico CARF first exchange ~2028, collection from 2027). (3) Treat the exemption peso figure as UMA-derived and recompute yearly (sources cited both 105,360.90 and 128,383 because UMA moves) — store value-in-MXN-at-disposal so the threshold test is reproducible. (4) Primary-source legal claims that survived verification: CFF Art. 30 (5-yr retention), LISR Art. 93 fr. XIX (exemption), LISR Art. 124 (inflation adjustment); secondary-only claim that did NOT verify to a primary source: 'real-time SAT platform access April 1 2026' and 'CARF 2026' — treat as journalism, not law. This is a logging-only change: safe for the first ~200 MXN real trade and consistent with the trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlist security stance. None of these caveats block a Mexico-based individual.

verdicts.recommendation: Set the cost-aware backtest gate's per-side cost to the real low-volume taker rate of the chosen venue/book (0.78% Bitso MXN, 0.40% Kraken Pro, 0.36% Bitso USDC) plus a live half-spread estimate, and require expected edge > 2x round-trip cost before going live. Do NOT use the stale 0.15%/0.36% figures.
verdicts.assessment:
COST CLAIMS: VERIFIED AND CURRENT (June 2026), against primary sources, not the recommendation's secondary cites.
- Bitso MXN book taker 0.78%: confirmed BOTH on the live primary API https://api.bitso.com/v3/available_books (btc_mxn "taker":"0.0078" at entry tier) AND on bitso.com/fees (0.780% taker at <20,000 MXN). This is the strongest, hardest-to-dispute number and it is exactly right.
- Bitso USDC book taker 0.36%: confirmed on bitso.com/fees (0.360% taker at <1,000 USD). Note: the live /available_books exposes this as the usd_mxn/USD ("Digital Dollars") book, not a literal "usdc_mxn" symbol — a naming nuance, not an error.
- Kraken Pro entry taker 0.40%: confirmed on kraken.com/features/fee-schedule (0.40% taker / 0.25% maker at $0+ 30-day volume).
- Round-trip drag 0.7%-1.6% and "stale 0.15%/0.36% are wrong" warning: both justified. Third-party aggregators returned inconsistent/stale figures (0.25%/0.30%, 0.36-0.60%), exactly the trap the recommendation warns against.

SAFETY: This is a SAFE change for a first tiny trade because it only makes the gate STRICTER. It tightens a backtest filter; it cannot itself trigger or worsen a live order. Worst case it blocks an otherwise-marginal strategy — which at 11 USD is the correct bias. The code defect it fixes is real: SimExecutor hardcodes fee_rate=0.001 (0.10%), 7-8x below the real Bitso MXN taker rate, so the current gate would green-light an uneconomic live strategy. Valid, code-level, high-impact.

DEDUCTIONS (why do-with-care, not do-now):
1. TAX GAP — the theme is "Fees, Spread & MEXICAN TAX" but the recommendation body contains ZERO tax content and cites only fee pages. The tax angle is material and specific to an automated swapping agent: SAT treats every crypto disposal — including crypto-to-crypto swaps — as enajenacion de bienes, taxable under ISR at progressive 1.92%-35%, requiring inflation-adjusted cost-basis per disposal, with an enajenacion exemption near 3x annual UMA (~128k MXN) and CARF/platform real-time reporting to SAT phasing in during 2026. None of this is asserted or sourced here. Any tax claim must carry a primary SAT/CNBV citation; the recommendation provides none, so the tax half of its own theme is unaddressed.
2. The "2x edge over round-trip cost" multiplier is a sensible conservative heuristic, not a derived threshold — fine at this size, but label it as a chosen safety margin, not a calculated one.
3. "Live half-spread estimate" is named but the capture method is unspecified (top-of-book snapshot vs realized slippage). Implementable but needs a concrete spec.
4. At 11 USD, exchange minimum-order-size and rounding can push effective drag ABOVE the percentage estimate; treat 0.7-1.6% as a floor.
verdicts.caveats: Cost figures verified current as of 2026-06-16 and will drift — re-probe /v3/available_books and the fee pages at go-live since they are volume-tiered and venue-policy-dependent. The recommendation is a gate-tightening code change only; it executes nothing. Do NOT treat it as covering the tax half of its stated theme — the SAT/ISR treatment (disposal = taxable event incl. swaps, inflation-adjusted basis, UMA exemption, 2026 CARF reporting) is real and material for an automated agent but is neither asserted nor primary-sourced here; require a separate CNBV/SAT-cited tax item before any live MXN trade. Half-spread capture method and the 2x multiplier are underspecified/heuristic.

verdicts.recommendation: First live ~200 MXN (~11 USD) trade should use Bitso exchange custody; do NOT buy a hardware wallet or build any self-custody/withdrawal path for Phase-3.
verdicts.assessment:
I tried to refute this and the core custody claim survives. CONFIRMED against primary/near-primary sources (2026): (1) Bitso is CNBV-registered as a Financial Technology Institution (ITF) under Mexico's Ley Fintech, MXN-native, SPEI deposits free — real and current for a Mexico individual. (2) Bitso's API supports exactly the security stance required: a key with "Place orders" enabled and "Make withdrawals" DISABLED, plus IP allowlist (up to 5 IPs), up to 6 keys per account (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). This is what makes a trade-only/no-withdrawal key sufficient and is the precise mechanism that lets the policy engine contain blast radius. (3) "One personal account per person/CURP" is corroborated (a second personal account triggers block/review; personal+business is the only legitimate dual case). (4) The economic-rationality argument is correct and decisive: a 54-249 USD hardware wallet is 5x-22x the position, and an offline cold wallet is structurally INCOMPATIBLE with an autonomous API agent that needs venue-side, programmatically-tradeable balance. Exchange custody is the only coherent choice at this size. The recommendation's central thesis is true and current.

WHERE IT NEEDS CARE (not refutation, but the rec understates these): (a) COST DRAG is the real risk, and the rec's "free SPEI" framing hides it. Bitso MXN-market fees are MAKER 0.600% / TAKER 0.780% at <20,000 MXN volume (bitso.com/fees, fetched directly) — so a market-order round-trip is ~1.56% in fees plus spread, i.e. roughly 0.17-0.20 USD of a 11 USD position evaporates on a single buy+sell before any market move. SPEI being free does not offset this; the cost-aware backtest gate (Deflated Sharpe) must use ~0.78% taker, not the 0.36-0.60% some secondary summaries cite. (b) ORDER MINIMUMS: the executor must read the per-book `minimum_amount` from the List-Available-Books endpoint and pick a pair whose minimum fits ~11 USD; a BTC-pair minimum may exceed the position. This is an executor-design item, not a custody flaw. (c) CARF/SAT: as of 2026 Mexican-operating platforms report balances and gross operations to SAT automatically (OECD CARF), so this trade is NOT invisible — fine for a compliant individual, but worth knowing. (d) Tax specifics (ISR 1.92-35%, ~128,383 MXN annual enajenacion exemption = 3x UMA) come only from SECONDARY sources (tax blogs, exchange blogs), NOT a direct SAT/DOF citation — I downgrade any reliance on exact tax thresholds until verified against SAT/DOF, but note the custody recommendation ITSELF makes no tax claim, so this does not weaken it.

One source-quality note: the rec cites tangem.com and aminagroup.com (vendor/marketing) and an rwatimes Substack; the load-bearing facts, however, are independently confirmed by Bitso's own docs and fee page, so the conclusion stands regardless of those weaker citations.
verdicts.caveats: Verdict is do-with-care (not do-now) ONLY because of cost framing, not custody soundness. Before the first live trade: (1) Hard-code the cost model at TAKER 0.78% one-way (round-trip ~1.56% + spread) in the backtest/policy gate; the per-tx notional cap and budget rule should account for this drag so the agent does not churn a $11 position to death. (2) Have the executor fetch per-book minimum_amount and choose a pair (e.g., a USDT/USD-MXN or low-minimum book) where ~11 USD clears the minimum; do not assume BTC. (3) Treat exact Mexican tax thresholds (ISR brackets, the ~128,383 MXN exemption, CARF reporting dates) as UNVERIFIED against primary SAT/DOF text — confirm with a SAT/DOF source or an accountant before relying on them; the custody decision does not depend on them. (4) Keep the API key strictly trade-only + no-withdrawal + IP-allowlisted, and store balance at venue with NO on-chain withdrawal path in the executor — exactly as the rec states; this is the correct blast-radius posture and is fully supported by Bitso's permission model.

verdicts.recommendation: {"recommendation":"Provision the agent's Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' enabled; leave 'Make withdrawals' AND 'Perform security actions' OFF; enable IP allowlist and pin it to the agent's egress IP (prefer the stable VPS IP 152.53.167.28 over a rotating residential IP — fail-closed if it changes). Store the once-shown secret encrypted at rest, never in git/plaintext config.","verdict":"do-now","holds_up":true}
verdicts.assessment:
CORE CLAIMS VERIFIED AGAINST PRIMARY SOURCE (Bitso official docs, docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials):

1. EXACTLY 5 separable permissions exist, names verbatim: "Place orders", "View balances", "View account information", "Perform security actions", "Make withdrawals". CONFIRMED.
2. Up to SIX API keys per account. CONFIRMED.
3. IP allowlist: "none up to five addresses". CONFIRMED.
4. The fail-open claim is TRUE and confirmed by Bitso's own docs: when the withdrawal destination whitelist is empty, "any address can receive funds." So the reasoning "simply not enabling withdrawals is the safe path" is correct and the more important guardrail. A trade-only, no-withdrawal key cannot move funds off-exchange — blast radius is bounded to fee/spread churn, exactly as claimed. CONFIRMED and security-sound for a first $11 trade.

PLATFORM/REGULATORY: Bitso is the correct platform for a Mexico individual. It operates a CNBV-authorized subsidiary (NVIO Pagos Mexico, IFPE/electronic-payments license under Ley Fintech, authorized Jan 2020) and is the dominant MX-domiciled exchange. An individual can self-register and KYC. No blocker found for a Mexico-based individual.

WEAKNESSES / OVERSTATEMENTS (do not invalidate the custody decision, but the 'why' oversells):
- "at zero functional cost" is misleading. Live Bitso MXN fees at the lowest tier (<20k MXN/30d) are 0.60% maker / 0.78% taker (bitso.com/fees, verified live — NOT the 0.50/0.65 some secondary pages cite). A round-trip taker fill is ~1.56% plus bid-ask spread; on ~200 MXN that is real drag (~3+ MXN round trip before spread). The custody choice is zero-cost; trading is not. Phase-3's fee/spread analysis should use 0.78% taker, not lower.
- Min order is fine: btc_mxn minimum_value = 5 MXN, well under 200 MXN. No blocker.
- IP-pinning nuance: pinning to the VPS 152.53.167.28 is sound ONLY if the agent's outbound traffic actually egresses from that IP. If the executor runs on the residential PC (much of this owner's stack does) and reaches Bitso directly, pinning to the VPS IP will fail-CLOSED and block all orders — correct security posture, but operationally the owner must confirm WHERE the executor runs and pin the ACTUAL egress IP. 'Fail-closed if it changes' is the right default; just verify the egress before locking.

TAX/LEGAL (DOWNGRADED — no primary source obtained): The SAT treatment (crypto = enajenacion de bienes / ISR, integrated into progressive personas-fisicas income, reported in the Declaracion Anual by Apr 30, MXN conversion at DOF rate) is consistent across multiple secondary guides but I did NOT confirm it against a primary SAT/CNBV publication. The recommendation itself makes NO tax claim, so this does not weaken it — but Phase-3's separate tax conclusion must be validated against an official SAT source before being relied on. At a single $11 gain the practical tax exposure is negligible, but the reporting-obligation claim needs a primary cite.

NET: The custody recommendation is true, current (2026), safe, and the single best first move. Adopt it as written. The only edits are to (a) fix the 'zero cost'/fee figures elsewhere in Phase-3 and (b) verify the real egress IP before locking the allowlist.
verdicts.caveats: Tax claims unverified against primary SAT source — downgrade any standalone tax conclusion to 'verify-live'. The recommendation itself makes no tax claim, so it is unaffected. Fee 'zero cost' framing is wrong for the trading layer: use live 0.60%/0.78% (maker/taker) MXN tier, not 0.50/0.65. IP-allowlist pin must target the executor's ACTUAL egress IP — confirm whether the agent runs on the VPS or the residential PC before locking, or orders fail-closed.
