PRE-TRADE custody/runbook for first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso: save 2FA emergency restore code offline, keep 2FA on a separate device, KYC docs ready; add docs/incident_response.md to treasuryforge with recovery steps (change password -> triggers Bitso 24h withdrawal freeze; change PIN; change email password; contact Bitso support; delete leaked API key in web UI with 2FA); store runbook off the trading PC.
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation's load-bearing claims are TRUE and CURRENT (2026), verified against Bitso's own documentation:

CONFIRMED:
- 24h automatic withdrawal freeze after a password change is real and current, verbatim from Bitso's "Why are my withdrawals disabled?" article ("Withdrawals are temporarily disabled for 24 hours after a password update... a buffer of 24 hours to keep your account protected"). The "change password first as an anti-takeover move" logic is valid.
- Suspect-login recovery steps (change Bitso password -> change PIN -> change email password if compromised -> contact support) are confirmed verbatim from Bitso's suspect-login article.
- Emergency restore code exists at 2FA setup; Bitso recommends saving it offline; losing BOTH the 2FA device AND the code forces the slow, support-ticket-gated recovery. Confirmed exactly as stated.
- Trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted API keys are fully supported: Bitso permissions are "Place orders" / "View balances" / "View account information" / "Perform security actions" / "Make withdrawals" (leave the last OFF), IP allowlist supports up to 5 addresses, 2FA is required to create keys. The security stance is correct and actually understated — there is also a "Perform security actions" permission that can block withdrawals. A leaked trade-only key is revoked instantly in the web UI with 2FA, exactly as claimed.
- Bitso is the correct platform: CNBV-regulated ITF in Mexico, a Mexico individual can self-register/KYC (INE + proof of address), ~$10 USD minimum trade (the ~$11 trade clears the floor).

REFUTED / WEAK POINTS (why "with care," not "do-now"):
1. The hard-coded support email "customersupport@bitso.com" could NOT be confirmed from any authoritative source. Bitso's canonical recovery channel is a support TICKET (support.bitso.com/hc/en-us/requests/new); the general email surfacing across sources is info@bitso.com. A runbook that instructs emailing an unverified/likely-wrong address is a real defect — replace the literal email with "open a support ticket at support.bitso.com/hc/en-us/requests/new" (the official 2FA/account-recovery path), and verify the exact contact in-account at setup time.
2. The "why" overstates "no phone support / text-only recovery." Bitso publishes phone lines (e.g., 800 479 6666 for cards, +52 lines). Account/2FA recovery is genuinely ticket-gated and slow, but the blanket "no phone support" framing is inaccurate.
3. Minor scenario conflation: for a leaked TRADE-ONLY key the correct first move is simply revoking that key (instant, blast-radius already contained by the no-withdrawal scope + IP allowlist + the policy engine). The password-change/24h-freeze sequence is for FULL account compromise, not a leaked trade-only key. The runbook lists both correctly but should label which scenario each path is for.

TAX/LEGAL: The recommendation makes no specific tax claim, so nothing to refute there. Context only: a ~11 USD trade is far below Mexico's ~128,383 MXN (3x annual UMA) gain exemption for personas fisicas, so it creates no practical ISR liability — but that came from commercial guides, not primary SAT, so treat as orientation, not a filing instruction.

NET: No regulatory, tax, security, or cost problem is created by this recommendation. It is a low-cost, defensive, do-before-trading custody step that correctly pressure-tests the security stance. The single actionable fix is the support contact (use the official ticket URL, drop the unverified email).
verdicts.caveats: Verify-live at setup: (1) Replace "email customersupport@bitso.com" in the runbook with "submit a support ticket at https://support.bitso.com/hc/en-us/requests/new" and confirm the exact in-account contact/email shown during YOUR 2FA setup — the hard-coded email is unverified. (2) Confirm the 24h withdrawal-freeze-on-password-change behavior is still active in-account before relying on it as your incident-response trigger (it is documented and current as of this check, but it is Bitso policy that can change). (3) Distinguish in the runbook between "leaked trade-only API key" (revoke the key instantly in web UI -> done; no password change needed) and "full account compromise" (change password to trigger the 24h freeze -> change PIN -> contact support). (4) Sources for the 24h freeze, suspect-login steps, API permission model, and emergency code are Bitso's own help center (primary for platform behavior); tax exemption context is from commercial guides, not primary SAT/CNBV — do not treat the tax figures as a filing instruction.

verdicts.recommendation: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso): mint two trade-only, no-withdrawal Bitso API keys, IP-allowlisted, secrets outside repo.
verdicts.assessment:
The spine of the recommendation is VERIFIED TRUE and current for a Mexico individual in 2026, but it contains one factual error and omits material cost realities.

VERIFIED against Bitso's official docs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials):
- Real, independently-toggleable permissions: "Place orders", "View balances", "View account information", "Perform security actions", "Make withdrawals". So an Agent key = Place orders + View balances, with "Make withdrawals" left OFF, is exactly buildable as described.
- "Make withdrawals" is a separate, independent toggle (off-able) — the no-withdrawal blast-radius bound is real; worst case is churning the 200 MXN, never withdrawing it. This is the single biggest and correct control.
- IP allowlist ("Restrict use of API addresses") exists, up to 5 IPs per key. Up to 6 keys per account, so the two-key scheme is feasible. Secrets shown once / store outside repo: sound.
- Bitso is a CNBV-regulated Financial Technology Institution (ITF) under the 2018 Ley Fintech; a solo MX individual can self-register, complete KYC, and trade. No regulatory blocker for an 11-USD trade.

REFUTED (factual error): the recommendation's "Watchdog/monitor key (cancel-only or read-only)". Bitso has NO cancel-only permission. Order cancellation is bundled inside "Place orders" — a key that can cancel can also place orders, so it is NOT a reduced-blast-radius key. The only true low-privilege watchdog on Bitso is READ-ONLY ("View balances" + "View account information"). The cancel-only option as written is impossible; if implemented literally you'd hand the watchdog full trade power, defeating the two-key isolation rationale.

MATERIAL OMISSIONS (Phase-3 cost reality the "first trade" framing should flag): BTC/MXN taker fee is 0.65%/side, maker 0.50%/side (MXN pairs 0.36-0.60% range) — round-trip ~1.0-1.3% in fees alone, ~2-2.6 MXN per round trip on 200 MXN, plus MXN-pair spread. Bitso minimum order is ~10 USD; 200 MXN (~11 USD) is barely above the floor, so any order-splitting or partial sell risks sub-minimum rejections. These don't invalidate the security stance but should be surfaced before the first real trade.

TAX (downgraded per instruction): no specific tax claim is made in THIS recommendation, so it isn't refuted here. But the common "crypto = bienes muebles, 3x-UMA annual exemption (~128k MXN), Art. 93 LISR" treatment is PRACTITIONER INTERPRETATION, not an explicit SAT crypto ruling — primary SAT/LISR sources cover bienes muebles generically, not crypto by name. Treat any tax sub-claim as verify-with-a-Mexican-accountant, not settled. Note also CARF/SAT data collection began Jan 1 2026.
verdicts.caveats: Fix the error before acting: change the watchdog key to READ-ONLY (View balances + View account information); drop "cancel-only" — Bitso cannot express it. Keep both keys' "Make withdrawals" OFF and run from the fixed-IP Debian VPS with that IP allowlisted (residential home IP is dynamic/blockable per project memory, so allowlisting it is fragile). Before the first live order, accept ~1-1.3% round-trip fee drag plus MXN spread on 200 MXN, and ensure the executor never emits sub-10-USD orders. Validate read-only first (fetch balances) then a single tiny test order. Confirm crypto tax treatment with a Mexican contador, not a blog, before any gain-realizing sell.

verdicts.recommendation: Codebase: add a BitsoExecutor implementing the existing Executor Protocol with (a) live available_books loader, (b) pre-flight min-notional reject, (c) journaled origin_id, (d) cancel_all() = DELETE /v3/orders/all. Theme: Risk & Ops for first ~200 MXN trade on Bitso, MX individual.
verdicts.assessment:
Core claims verified TRUE and current (2026), but the recommendation is incomplete on the hardest part and silent on two real-money issues.

VERIFIED:
- Executor Protocol execute(intent,tick,wallet)->Fill exists exactly as stated (treasuryforge/executor.py:20-21). Swap point is real.
- Live /v3/available_books returns minimum_value=10 MXN for btc_mxn and usdt_mxn. A ~200 MXN order is ~20x the floor (comfortable). Reading it live genuinely moots the 5-vs-10 doc conflict.
- origin_id uniqueness across ACTIVE orders confirmed in Bitso docs; <=40-char [a-zA-Z0-9_-] idempotency rationale is sound.
- DELETE /v3/orders/all is a real documented endpoint (plus single/multi variants).
- BONUS (unstated dependency, now confirmed): Bitso supports TRADE-only, NO-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted keys (up to 5 IPs), so the security stance is achievable.

REFUTATIONS / GAPS:
1. SYNC-FILL MODEL MISMATCH (biggest gap): real Bitso orders return an oid immediately; fills are async and can be partial. A synchronous execute()->Fill that mutates SimWallet in-place (wallet.py apply_fill) cannot represent a real fill without a status-poll/reconciliation loop. The four sub-items cover order PLACEMENT but omit fill RECONCILIATION entirely.
2. FEE REALISM: SimExecutor default fee_rate=0.001 (0.10%) understates Bitso MXN-market taker (~0.60-0.65%). Round-trip drag at 200 MXN ~= 1.3% + spread. Material, not blocking, but the executor reads live minimums while leaving fees mis-calibrated.
3. TAX (primary-source demand only partly met): every crypto->stablecoin/crypto swap is an enajenacion under LISR Art. 141 -- a per-swap taxable event with inflation-adjusted cost basis, a latent landmine for an autonomous multi-swap agent the recommendation never mentions. De minimis at 200 MXN (below ~128,383 MXN annual gain exemption). Bitso does NOT withhold 20% on individual sales (that 20% is the personas-morales-without-RFC / digital-platforms regime, a conflation in some blogs); self-declaration is the user's duty. All tax specifics rest on SECONDARY sources (EY bulletin, PRODECON via press, tax blogs) -- no direct SAT/CFF ruling quoted -- so downgrade and confirm with a MX fiscal advisor before scaling.
4. CUSTODY/REGULATORY: Bitso is NOT a CNBV-authorized IFPE for client-facing crypto; no MX entity offers retail crypto under the formal Ley Fintech perimeter (Banxico restricts crypto to internal ops). Legitimate and dominant, but regulated-adjacent, not licensed crypto custody. Fine at 200 MXN; unmentioned.
verdicts.caveats: Tax and custody/regulatory claims are sourced from secondary references (EY MX bulletin, PRODECON-via-press, tax blogs, CNBV IFPE registry summary), NOT direct primary SAT/CFF/CNBV text; treat as directional and verify with a Mexican fiscal advisor before scaling beyond a single tiny trade. Fee figures (~0.60-0.65% MXN taker) are from exchange-review aggregators, not a live authenticated fee-tier read on the user's own account. The async-fill reconciliation gap is inferred from the codebase's synchronous execute()->Fill signature vs. Bitso's oid-then-poll order model; the exact partial-fill behavior should be confirmed against a live read-only probe before the first real order.

verdicts.recommendation: Risk & Ops: add a STARTUP + PRE-ORDER reconciliation gate (query Bitso open_orders + recent user_trades, treat EXCHANGE as ground truth, trip kill-switch on divergence, never blind-retry a submit).
verdicts.assessment:
The recommendation survives adversarial scrutiny on substance, but one of its three citations is fabricated/misattributed and the design under-specifies a real Bitso-specific gotcha.

WHAT HOLDS UP (verified):
1. Bitso API endpoints are REAL and current (2026), verified directly against docs.bitso.com:
   - GET /open_orders and "Look Up Orders" return open/partially-filled orders only (matched or cancelled orders are excluded from the response).
   - GET /user_trades lists ALL recent trades across books (limit default 25 / max 100, marker pagination, desc sort), returning tid, oid, book, major/minor, price, side, fees_amount/fees_currency, created_at, and origin_id. This is exactly the data a reconciliation gate needs.
2. The security stance the project demands is supported: Bitso API keys support a Trade permission independent of Withdraw, and an IP allowlist ("Allowed IPs") is MANDATORY at key creation. Trade-only + no-withdrawal + IP-allowlisted is achievable, matching the policy-engine blast-radius design.
3. Bitso is legal for a Mexico-based individual and is CNBV-regulated under the Ley Fintech as an ITF; individuals must complete Bitso's own KYC. No regulatory blocker for a ~200 MXN first trade. (This is a CODEBASE change, so tax/legal is background, not load-bearing.)
4. The engineering core is genuinely best practice and genuinely a LIVE-ONLY failure the SimExecutor never exercises: industry consensus is exactly reconcile-via-REST-snapshot, exchange-as-ground-truth, idempotency/client-order-id, and never blind-retry. State drift after a watchdog/crash restart is a real, high-severity failure mode. This is a high-value first live change, consistent with the project already being crash-safe/journaled (journal.py checkpoints policy+wallet atomically via os.replace; the runner has no boot-time exchange reconciliation today, so the gap the recommendation fills is real).

WHAT FAILS / NEEDS CARE:
A. CITATION FRAUD: the third source, markrbest.github.io/paper-vs-live/, does NOT support the quoted claim. That article is a slippage sim-vs-live comparison; it never mentions state drift, reconciliation, exchange-as-ground-truth, or restart recovery, and contains no phrase like "the single most dangerous failure mode." The dramatic quote in the "why" is unsourced. The claim is still TRUE on independent best-practice grounds, but the attributed evidence is bogus — downgrade trust in that one citation.
B. CITATION SLUG MISMATCH: the second cited URL (list-user-trades) is actually Bitso's "List Order Trades" (GET /order_trades/, per-order), not the account-wide GET /user_trades the recommendation's prose relies on. For broad "recent fills since outage" you must hit /user_trades. Minor but the source link points at the narrower endpoint.
C. DESIGN GOTCHA the recommendation glosses: per Bitso's History of Changes, origin_id uniqueness is now scoped to ACTIVE orders only (reusable after an order completes). Matching historical fills by origin_id can therefore COLLIDE if the agent ever reuses an origin_id. Reconciliation must key on Bitso's server oid (or guarantee globally-unique-per-submit origin_ids, e.g. UUIDs) — not on origin_id alone — or it can mis-attribute a fill. Also: a fully-filled order vanishes from open_orders/look-up-orders, so detecting a fill that completed DURING the outage REQUIRES /user_trades (or /order_trades) — which is precisely the crash-restart case this gate targets, so the open_orders query alone is insufficient and the trades query is the load-bearing half.

NET: the recommendation is directionally correct, safe for a first tiny real trade, and creates no regulatory/tax/security/cost problem (it is conservative — it fails CLOSED into the kill-switch). It does not over-trade or widen blast radius. Implement it, but (i) drop/replace the markrbest citation, (ii) key reconciliation on server oid not origin_id, (iii) make /user_trades (account-wide) the primary fill-detection call, and (iv) define epsilon in quote terms accounting for fees/rounding so normal fee dust does not false-trip the kill-switch on every boot.
verdicts.caveats: One of three citations (markrbest paper-vs-live) is fabricated/misattributed — it does not discuss reconciliation or "the single most dangerous failure mode"; the claim is independently true but that evidence is bogus. Second citation slug points to per-order /order_trades, while the design needs account-wide /user_trades. Bitso-specific risk: origin_id is now unique only across ACTIVE orders (reusable after completion), so reconcile on server oid, not origin_id, to avoid mis-attributing fills; and because filled orders disappear from open_orders, the /user_trades query is the load-bearing half for detecting fills that completed during an outage. Define epsilon to absorb fee/rounding dust so the kill-switch does not false-trip on every boot. Regulatory/tax not independently re-verified against SAT/CNBV primary sources here because this is a codebase change, not a tax claim — Bitso CNBV/ITF status confirmed via secondary 2026 sources only.

verdicts.recommendation: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso): instantiate PolicyConfig for the 200-MXN rung (max_notional_per_tx=25, max_notional_per_window=100, max_tx_per_window=4, max_drawdown_pct=0.05, fee_rate=0.0078), add a startup assertion max_notional_per_tx >= live minimum_value, and bind tick-windows to 24h wall-clock in live mode.
verdicts.assessment: Codebase claims are TRUE and verified against source. Every cited PolicyConfig field exists and behaves as described (C:/Users/DANNY/Desktop/treasuryforge/treasuryforge/policy.py:37-49) — no schema change needed, confirmed. The headline prerequisite is the strongest and most correct point: MarketTick.ts is an integer step with NO wall-clock (types.py:23) and PolicyEngine._prune does now_ts - window arithmetic (policy.py:178-185), so window_steps/spend_window_steps are tick units and the "daily" caps are genuinely inert in live mode until ts maps to real seconds/ns. That binding is a real, load-bearing prerequisite, not boilerplate. The startup assertion is a sound proposed addition (none exists today); 25 >= minimum_value gives a valid order-size band. External facts check out: fee_rate=0.0078 = Bitso's entry-tier MXN-market taker fee (bitso.com/fees, <20,000 MXN); btc_mxn minimum_value = 5 MXN (confirmed in Bitso API docs, so 25-MXN cap clears it — the floating "$10 minimum" is wrong for this book); trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted keys are explicitly supported by Bitso. No regulatory blocker for a Mexican individual (CNBV-regulated, INE/CURP+selfie KYC, ~10 min). REFUTATION POINTS (all tuning/wording, not blockers): (1) "outside BTC's ~2%/day noise" is OPTIMISTIC — V-Lab GARCH shows ~52% 1-day annualized vol in June 2026, i.e. ~2.7% daily sigma, so a 5% breaker is only ~1.85 sigma and can trip on an ordinary bad day, not just genuine failures; the recommendation honestly labels these medium-confidence, but the framing understates trip frequency. (2) "conservative top-tier taker" mislabels 0.78%, which is the WORST (entry) tier and applies only to *_mxn books; a btc_mxn bot actually pays 0.098%, so 0.0078 is conservative for the solvency buffer but the rationale conflates books/tiers. (3) OMISSION: PolicyConfig.fee_rate only widens the solvency buffer (policy.py:137); SimExecutor carries its own independent fee_rate (executor.py:25,38), so the live Executor must ALSO be set to the real fee or actual fee drag is not modeled — setting it on PolicyConfig alone is insufficient. (4) Fee drag at ~11 USD is brutal: at 0.78% a buy+sell round-trip is ~1.56% + spread, ~3 MXN each — correctly flagged in spirit. Tax (LISR enajenacion de bienes; Art. 93 fr. XIX-b movable-property exemption ~3x annual UMA likely covers gains at this size) is a project-goal item, not a claim the recommendation itself makes, so it is context not a refutation target.
verdicts.caveats: Do-with-care, not do-now, because: (a) the live Executor's own fee_rate must be set to the real Bitso fee independently of PolicyConfig.fee_rate or fee drag goes unmodeled; (b) the 5% breaker sits at ~1.85 sigma of real ~2.7% daily BTC vol and will trip on ordinary down-days — accept that or widen to ~7-8% if you want it to fire only on genuine failures; (c) verify-live the exact minimum_value via a live GET /available_books call at startup (the assertion should read the live value, not a hard-coded 5) and confirm whether the bot trades btc_mxn (0.098% fee) vs an *_mxn alt book (0.78%) since that 8x fee difference changes the drag math; (d) tax exemption and CARF reporting (SAT data collection began 2026-01-01) should be confirmed with a primary SAT/Art.93 source or a Mexican tax pro before scaling beyond this tiny rung — not a blocker at ~11 USD but a real obligation.

verdicts.recommendation: Codebase: ACTIVATE the inert staleness gate by passing a real data_age_ns into PolicyEngine.evaluate() in live mode; add freshness fields; set max_staleness_ns to ~2-3x poll interval; propose no-op when stale.
verdicts.assessment:
The central code claim is VERIFIED and accurate, and survived adversarial checking against the live source. In treasuryforge/policy.py:97 the staleness gate fires only when BOTH cfg.max_staleness_ns is not None AND data_age_ns is not None; evaluate() (line 73) defaults data_age_ns=None; and the live driver treasuryforge/runner.py:107 calls evaluate() WITHOUT data_age_ns. So the gate is genuinely inert in every current code path. The change is also low-risk because the rule's internal comparison logic is ALREADY unit-tested with data_age_ns supplied (tests/test_policy_phase2.py:83-98) -- only the live wiring is missing. The motivation is sound: a frozen-but-open feed is the deadliest unattended failure, and Bitso is a REST/poll exchange (confirmed 60 RPM public / 300 RPM private rate limits), exactly the model where stale-price trading is a real loss vector. This is a local-only code change with zero on-chain/money/tax/regulatory/cost surface; it strictly reduces blast radius (fail-closed). No tax/legal claims are made in THIS recommendation, so no CNBV/SAT primary-source demand applies. Separately confirmed that the broader Phase-3 security stance is achievable on Bitso for a MX individual: API keys support 'Place orders' WITHOUT 'Make withdrawals' plus an IP allowlist of up to 5 addresses -- the trade-only / no-withdrawal / IP-allowlisted posture the project demands.

WHY do-with-care, not do-now: (1) The recommendation OMITS a load-bearing implementation warning that already lives in the repo at PHASE2_DISCOVERY.md:78 -- do NOT compute data age from MarketTick.ts (it is an integer step counter with no wall-clock meaning); the live feed adapter must stamp a real received-at time and compare via time.monotonic_ns(). The recommendation's wording ('freshness fields last_market_ts/last_account_ts', 'exceeds ~2-3x the poll interval') invites re-introducing the exact tick.ts mistake if last_market_ts is derived from the tick rather than from wall-clock receipt. Get this wrong and the gate is silently miscalibrated -- worse than inert because it looks active. (2) The two cited sources are NOT load-bearing: the Bitso 'general-concepts' URL is a real page but covers REST format and rate limits, NOT staleness/heartbeat/freshness or key permissions (verified by fetch); the Medium link is a personal blog, not authoritative. The claim's real evidence is the codebase itself, which is strong -- the citations add nothing and should not be leaned on.
verdicts.caveats: Implementation must use wall-clock/monotonic receipt time (time.monotonic_ns()) for data age, NOT MarketTick.ts (integer step, no wall-clock) -- see PHASE2_DISCOVERY.md:78; the recommendation's 'last_market_ts/poll-interval' phrasing risks reintroducing that bug. Also note account-data staleness (last_account_ts) is a separate concern from market staleness and may need a separate budget. Sources cited do not substantiate the staleness claim (Bitso page = REST/rate-limits only; Medium = personal blog); rely on the verified code, not the citations. Not a financial-effecting change, so no tax/regulatory exposure for the trade itself.

verdicts.recommendation: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso): smallest safe E2E validation sequence + live secret loader (keyring-then-env), making trade #1 a cost-measurement experiment with a sub-200-MXN smoke order.
verdicts.assessment:
The core thesis survives adversarial scrutiny and is current for a Mexico-based individual in 2026, but two concrete sub-steps are wrong and must be fixed before executing.

VERIFIED / HOLDS UP:
- Legality for an INDIVIDUAL: Banxico's virtual-asset restriction targets REGULATED FINANCIAL INSTITUTIONS (banks/IFPE), NOT retail. Individuals may still buy/sell crypto via non-financial exchanges like Bitso "at their own risk" (IMCP, imcp.org.mx). Bitso operates legally; its IFPE fiat rail is NVIO Pagos. No block for the owner.
- Security stance is genuinely achievable on Bitso: the API offers granular permissions ("Place orders" + "View balances" enabled, "Make withdrawals" left OFF) and an IP allowlist of up to 5 IPs (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials). The TRADE-ONLY, NO-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted key the policy-engine stance demands is real.
- Live minimums ARE machine-readable via the books endpoint: btc_mxn minimum_value is currently 5 MXN (older docs showed 25). Step (2) "fetch live minimums" is the correct guard; 10-20 MXN clears btc_mxn but other books vary, so fetching is load-bearing. SPEI deposit minimum is 100 MXN, so funding 200 MXN is fine.
- cancel_all() exists: DELETE /api/v3/orders/all.
- The cost-measurement premise is CORRECT and is the strongest part: real Bitso MXN-market taker fee at the entry tier is 0.65% (support.bitso.com), while SimExecutor hardcodes fee_rate=0.001 (0.10%) + 5bps slippage. The sim understates round-trip cost ~6.5x on fees alone -- exactly the paper-to-live divergence the rec says to measure. Premise validated against the actual executor.py.
- keyring is correctly flagged as NON-stdlib (confirmed: not in sys.stdlib_module_names); .env+gitignore fallback is honest, and the repo .gitignore already excludes .env/*.key/secrets/.

REFUTED / FLAWS:
1. WRONG SOURCE + WRONG MECHANISM for the clock-drift step. The cited recvWindow source is Binance/Bybit. Bitso does NOT use a recvWindow clock-tolerance window; it authenticates with a MONOTONICALLY-INCREASING NONCE (HMAC-SHA256, docs.bitso.com/.../understand-bitsos-auth-mechanism). The real failure mode is nonce reuse/regression (clock stepping backwards, parallel requests), not "timestamp outside recvWindow." The NTP/drift<1-2s check is low-value busywork here; the actual guard needed is a strictly monotonic nonce generator. Downgrade/replace this sub-step.
2. KILL-PATH TEST IS LOGICALLY BROKEN. Step 3 places a MARKET buy, which fills instantly; step 4's cancel_all() then cancels nothing (Bitso docs: won't cancel already-filled orders). It proves nothing about the kill path. To genuinely exercise cancellation, place a RESTING LIMIT order far from market (won't fill), confirm it is open, then cancel_all() and confirm it's gone -- THEN do the tiny market buy + reconcile. Reorder accordingly.
3. SCOPE/CONTEXT (non-blocking): tax omitted. For a ~200 MXN trade, any gain is far below the ~128,383 MXN (3x annual UMA) enajenacion exemption for personas fisicas, so effectively zero ISR; IVA does not apply to buy/sell. CARF auto-reporting to SAT is REAL but Mexico is OECD SECOND-WAVE (reporting ~2028, not 2026 as the memory note implied) per OECD monitoring docs -- so 'all trades reported in 2026' is overstated, though trades are traceable regardless. Keep records.

Net: the ramp (read-only -> live-minimums -> tiny order -> reconcile sim-vs-actual) is the right shape, the platform/permissions/legality are correct and current, and the cost-experiment framing is well-founded. Two sub-steps (clock check, cancel test) are technically wrong and would either waste effort or give false confidence in the kill path. Fix those two and it is safe to execute the first tiny real trade.
verdicts.caveats: Do-with-care, not do-now, because of two concrete fixes required before the first live order: (1) Replace the NTP/recvWindow step (Binance-specific, inapplicable to Bitso) with a strictly-monotonic-nonce generator and a nonce-regression guard -- that is the actual Bitso auth failure mode. (2) Fix the kill-path test: a market buy cannot be cancelled, so test cancel_all() against a RESTING LIMIT order placed far from market (place -> confirm open -> cancel_all -> confirm gone) BEFORE the tiny market buy. Also: model real fees correctly in the live executor -- entry-tier MXN taker is 0.65%, ~6.5x the SimExecutor's 0.10%; use the spot/trading API (maker-taker) path, NOT the Bitso App 'instant buy' which carries a larger hidden spread. Verify the live book's minimum_value at runtime (5 MXN for btc_mxn today) since a 10-20 MXN smoke can fail on other books. keyring being non-stdlib is acceptable as the rec itself flags it. Tax is effectively zero at this size (well under the ~128,383 MXN exemption), but keep cost-basis records as CARF reporting reaches Mexico (~2028). Sources: docs.bitso.com (API credentials, auth mechanism, books, cancel), support.bitso.com (maker-taker fees, SPEI minimum), imcp.org.mx (Banxico scope), OECD CARF monitoring update (Mexico second-wave), pypi.org/project/keyring (non-stdlib).

verdicts.recommendation: Proceed with the ~200 MXN first trade as a persona física on Bitso, with two corrections to the recommendation: (1) Bitso's CNBV authorization is via NVIO as an IFPE (e-money license covering the MXN/SPEI wallet), NOT an ITF license for crypto — crypto trading itself is legal-but-unlicensed and not IPAB/CNBV-protected; and (2) the "no anti-lavado filing" claim is correct but the recommendation must also state that ISR (income tax) applies to each crypto disposal/swap as an enajenación — exempt at this size (annual gains exemption ~128,383 MXN = 3x UMA) but the owner should retain cost-basis records, and CARF/CRS exchange reporting to SAT begins in 2026. Use the API order-book path (taker ~0.30%) not the App instant-buy (wider ~1%+ spread); create the API key trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted (Bitso supports all three).
verdicts.assessment: The core legal conclusion is correct and primary-source-backed: SAT's own page confirms the "actividad vulnerable de activos virtuales" obligated subjects are platforms/wallet providers/exchanges, explicitly NOT individuals trading own funds, so no personal license, CNBV/Banxico/AMIB registration, or SPPLD anti-lavado filing is required. Bitso allows individual KYC (INE/CURP+selfie), SPEI funding, $10 min trade, and the API supports trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlist keys — the security stance is achievable. Two flaws: (a) "CNBV-supervised ITF" is imprecise — Bitso's CNBV cover is an IFPE e-money license via NVIO for the MXN rail, not an ITF crypto license; no institution holds an ITF for retail crypto, so the crypto balance is unlicensed/uninsured (legal but not protected). This doesn't block the trade but overstates the regulatory shield. (b) The recommendation conflates "no anti-lavado filing" with "no obligations" and is silent on ISR: every sale/swap is a taxable enajenación; at ~200 MXN it is comfortably under the gains exemption so zero tax is owed and nothing must be filed, but cost-basis records should be kept and CARF/CRS auto-reporting to SAT starts 2026. Cost drag is trivial in absolute terms (cents) if the API order book is used instead of the wide-spread instant-buy. No hard blocker for a Mexico-based individual.
verdicts.caveats: Refute/refine before acting: (1) "CNBV-supervised ITF" is wrong as stated — it is an IFPE (e-money) authorization via NVIO covering only the MXN wallet; crypto is legal-but-unlicensed (no IPAB/CNBV protection on the crypto balance). (2) Tax gap: recommendation omits ISR — disposals/swaps are taxable enajenaciones; exempt at this size (~128,383 MXN 3x-UMA annual gains exemption) but keep acquisition records; CARF/CRS reporting to SAT begins 2026. (3) UMA threshold for the provider's reporting is disputed across sources (210 vs 645 UMA) but irrelevant since it binds Bitso, not the individual, and ~200 MXN is far below either. (4) Use API order book (taker ~0.30%) not App instant-buy (~1%+ spread); round-trip fee ~0.6%, immaterial in absolute terms for validation. (5) Sources are mostly secondary/SEO blogs; only the SAT page and the diputados/CNBV pages are primary — the obligated-subject claim IS primary-source confirmed (SAT consulta 79706); the IFPE-not-ITF distinction is from a secondary source and should be confirmed on the CNBV Padrón de Entidades Supervisadas before relying on the regulatory characterization.

verdicts.recommendation: Create the Bitso API key with ONLY 'Place orders' + 'View balances' (and optionally read-only 'View account information') ENABLED; leave 'Make withdrawals' and 'Perform security actions' DISABLED; set the IP allowlist to the agent host's static IP; enable 2FA. Store key/secret outside the repo. Validate read-only first.
verdicts.assessment:
VERIFIED CURRENT (2026) and SAFE. Confirmed against the primary source (docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials): Bitso exposes exactly five INDEPENDENT permission toggles — Place orders, View balances, View account information, Perform security actions, Make withdrawals — each individually enable/disable. The IP allowlist supports "none up to five addresses," so the "up to 5" claim is correct. Disabling 'Make withdrawals' is precisely what enables/disables fund egress, so a withdrawals-disabled key is structurally incapable of sending funds to any external (incl. SDN) address — the blast-radius/containment logic is sound and reinforces the policy engine. REGULATORY: no blocker for a Mexico-based individual — Bitso is registered as an Institucion de Fondos de Pago Electronico (IFPE) under the Ley Fintech, CNBV-supervised; a solo individual can self-register with INE + selfie/liveness KYC. 2FA-to-create-key and off-repo secret storage match Bitso's own API security guidance. The recommendation makes no tax/legal claim that could be false; its claims are all confirmed.

REFUTATION ATTEMPTS that did NOT break the core recommendation but surface adjacent gaps it omits: (1) TRADE-SIZE FLOOR — Bitso minimum trade size is ~$10 USD and the target is ~$11 USD (~200 MXN), leaving almost no headroom; after taker fees (~0.65-0.75% all-in standard tier, worse in thin USD/MXN books) plus spread, a buy+sell round-trip at this size is fee/spread-dominated. The key-scoping advice is unaffected, but a reader should know ~200 MXN sits at the executability floor. (2) READ-ONLY VALIDATION — ticker is a PUBLIC endpoint (no key); only balance reads need 'View balances'. The recommendation's sequence works. (3) TAX — every crypto disposal (sell/swap) is taxable in Mexico as enajenacion de bienes under ISR (SAT); immaterial at 200 MXN (under the ~128k MXN UMA-based 2026 exemption per secondary tax-advisory sources) but cost-basis tracking obligation begins at trade #1. I could NOT confirm the exact exemption figure against a primary SAT bulletin in this pass (only secondary advisory sources) — verify before relying on the number. None of these refute the recommendation; they are caveats on claims it does not itself make.

Sources: https://docs.bitso.com/bitso-api/docs/2-generate-your-api-credentials (primary, permission toggles + 5-IP allowlist), https://support.bitso.com/hc/en-us/articles/4414985686036 (maker-taker fees), https://bitso.com/fees, https://www.lightspark.com/knowledge/is-crypto-legal-in-mexico + https://www.ccn.com/education/crypto/crypto-legal-mexico-banks-ban-crypto-2026/ (Ley Fintech / CNBV / IFPE status — secondary), https://plisio.net/tax/mexico-crypto-tax (SAT ISR treatment — secondary; primary SAT source still needed).
verdicts.caveats: The recommendation itself is do-now: the key-scoping action is verified, current, regulation-compliant, and the safest possible first step with no downside. Two adjacent items the recommendation does NOT state but the Phase-3 owner must know: (1) ~200 MXN (~$11) is right at Bitso's ~$10 minimum-order floor — after fees+spread a round-trip is heavily cost-dragged; this is a sizing reality, not a key-scoping flaw. (2) Crypto disposals are ISR-taxable in Mexico from trade #1 (immaterial at this size but track cost basis); the specific 2026 exemption figure (~128k MXN) is from secondary sources and should be confirmed against a primary SAT publication before being relied upon. The 'View account information' toggle is genuinely optional (only needed for KYC/limit data).

verdicts.recommendation: Keep treasuryforge strictly single-user / own-funds; add a SCOPE.md / README legal note declaring third-party funds, pooling, custody-of-others, and selling signals/management-as-a-service OUT OF SCOPE pending Mexican counsel + (if ever pursued) the correct license; optionally encode an own-account-only assertion in policy/config.
verdicts.assessment:
VERDICT: do-now on the ACTION, but the stated LEGAL RATIONALE is anchored to the wrong statute and must be corrected before it is treated as authoritative.

What holds up (primary-source confirmed):
1. Art. 225 LMV verbatim DOES require "de manera habitual y profesional" services "a nombre y por cuenta de terceros" — so the habitual/professional/third-party triggers are real (leyes-mx.com/ley_del_mercado_de_valores/225.htm; diputados.gob.mx LMV ref06). An individual trading his OWN funds trips NONE of them. Conclusion is correct and safe.
2. The "even registered advisors are barred from custody of clients' money" claim is TRUE, but it lives in Art. 226 / the CNBV General Dispositions (advisors may not receive in deposit, administration, custody or guarantee clients' money or securities), NOT Art. 225 as the why-text implies. Substance correct, citation loose.
3. The action is documentation-only: zero regulatory, tax, security, or cost risk. Purely protective, auditable, reversible. No downside at any trade size.

STRONGEST REFUTATION (recommendation overlooks this): treasuryforge trades CRYPTO. In Mexico, crypto = "activos virtuales" under the Ley Fintech (LRITF) regime, and crypto is explicitly NOT a "valor"/security. The LMV (incl. Art. 225/226) governs VALORES. So citing LMV Art. 225 as "THE single bright line" for a crypto agent is a category mismatch — wrong statute for the asset class. The binding regime for any future third-party-CRYPTO activity is more likely LRITF (ITF / exchange authorization, which is gated by Banxico and effectively unissued to date) plus AML/PLD, which is arguably MORE restrictive than LMV advisor registration. Net effect: the practical conclusion (own-funds only) is even more strongly supported, but the legal reasoning as written is imprecise and would mislead if relied on verbatim.

TAX (adjacent, SAT-confirmed, supports own-account framing): own-funds crypto gains are taxed as "enajenacion de bienes" under ISR, with an annual exemption around 3 UMA; at ~200 MXN it is immaterial. Crypto-to-crypto is a taxable permuta. CARF reporting begins 2026. None of this blocks a Mexico-based individual.

BLOCKERS for a Mexico individual: none from this recommendation. (The real Phase-3 blockers live in the platform/KYC/API-key-scope research, not here.)
verdicts.caveats: Do the action now, but fix the rationale: (a) re-anchor the legal note to BOTH regimes — LMV Art. 225/226 (securities-advice/management + custody bar) AND, more importantly for crypto, the Ley Fintech/LRITF (activos virtuales, ITF authorization, Banxico gate) plus AML/PLD; state plainly that crypto is NOT a "valor". (b) Move the custody citation from Art. 225 to Art. 226 / CNBV Disposiciones Generales. (c) Frame the triggers correctly: habitual AND professional applied to third-party advice/management — for own-funds trading none apply regardless. (d) Add the SAT/ISR own-account note (enajenacion de bienes, ~3 UMA exemption, permuta on crypto-to-crypto, CARF 2026) so the boundary is tax-aware. The "high impact" rating is overstated for a tiny own-funds trade — its value is as a future-proofing guardrail, not a precondition for the first 200 MXN trade. None of these corrections change the decision to ship the SCOPE.md; they prevent the note from being cited as authoritative-but-wrong later. For any real third-party plans, this still needs a Mexican abogado — the note is a boundary marker, not legal advice.

verdicts.recommendation: Extend the executor/journal to log every fill (timestamp, side, pair, qty, price, fee, MXN-equivalent value in/out) as a tax-grade ledger with CSV export, capturing per-disposal MXN cost basis from trade #1.
verdicts.assessment:
The ENGINEERING action holds up and is well-founded; two TAX CLAIMS in the rationale are overstated and must be corrected.

VERIFIED TRUE (individual, Mexico, 2026):
1. Crypto-to-crypto swaps and sell-to-MXN are taxable disposals (enajenacion) under ISR for personas fisicas. Corroborated across sources; Bitso's own help center confirms users are fully self-responsible (Bitso does not withhold ISR nor issue CFDI for crypto trades).
2. CORE PREMISE CONFIRMED: Bitso provides only an annual transaction report / raw history export, NOT cost basis and NOT per-disposal MXN gain/loss. The user therefore MUST build their own reconcilable cost-basis ledger; the exchange will not supply it. This is the strongest, load-bearing part of the recommendation.
3. The gap is real in code: treasuryforge/treasuryforge/types.py Fill is quote-only (price/fee) with an integer ts (no wall-clock) and no MXN field or cost-basis tracking; executor.py writes no fiat-economic detail. The work is additive, append-only, and never touches the policy engine or execution path -> zero blast radius for the first tiny trade. Building now is genuinely cheaper than reconstructing later for a mechanical strategy that emits many small taxable events.

OVERSTATED (downgrade the rationale, not the action):
A. CARF timing is wrong for Mexico. Recommendation says ~2026-2027; KPMG/PwC/OECD-commitments place Mexico in the SECOND wave -> data collection from 1 Jan 2027, first exchanges/reporting commencing 2028. The 2026 batch of 48 jurisdictions excludes Mexico.
B. "Assume full SAT visibility" in 2026-2027 is premature. The actual 2026 instrument (Resolucion Miscelanea Fiscal 2026 real-time digital-platform reporting, effective 1 Apr 2026) per KPMG does NOT specifically cover crypto exchanges (targets goods/services intermediation), and Bitso confirms it does not currently auto-report crypto to SAT. Full automatic crypto visibility is a ~2028 reality. The DIRECTION (design for reconcilability) is sound; the dating is wrong.

OMISSION that reframes urgency: at ~200 MXN the trade is ~0.16% of the annual disposal exemption (3x annual UMA ~128,383 MXN for 2026, Art. 93 LISR per cited guides), so a first tiny trade's gain is almost certainly exempt. BUT the exemption REQUIRES keeping records to prove you are under the threshold -- which still justifies the ledger, just reframes its near-term purpose as proving exemption rather than computing tax owed.

SOURCE-QUALITY CAVEAT: all three cited sources are secondary (beincrypto, contarito, mexc blog). I could not parse the OECD primary PDFs nor reach SAT/CNBV primary text (403s / binary). Per the demand-a-primary-source rule I down-weight legal precision accordingly; the direction is corroborated by KPMG/PwC and Bitso's official help center, but exact ISR mechanics (20% provisional, Art. 93 threshold applying to crypto) rest on secondary guidance and a CPA should confirm before relying on them for filing.
verdicts.caveats: Ship the ledger now (do-now on the code), but fix the justification before treating it as fact: (1) change CARF to "Mexico ~2028 (data from 2027)," not "2026-2027"; (2) soften "assume full SAT visibility" to a forward-looking design assumption, not a 2026 reality; (3) add the real near-term driver -- record-keeping to PROVE the 3x-UMA disposal exemption (~128,383 MXN/yr), under which a 200 MXN first trade is almost certainly exempt. Capture wall-clock UTC timestamp (current Fill.ts is an integer sim step), store MXN value at fill time from a recorded rate source, and prefer Decimal over float for money fields to keep the ledger tax-grade and reconcilable against Bitso's annual report. Do NOT rely on the cited 20% provisional / Art. 93 specifics for an actual filing without a Mexican CPA confirming against LISR primary text. None of this blocks the first ~200 MXN trade.

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: NO HARD BLOCKER FOUND for a Mexico-based individual on Bitso. The first ~200 MXN trade is unblocked end-to-end (KYC via CURP/INE -> free SPEI funding -> trade-only IP-allowlisted key -> POST /orders). The items below are soft/conditional blockers to clear, not stoppers.

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: IP ALLOWLIST NEEDS A STATIC IP (conditional). The trade-only key's IP allowlist is the outer security wall, but the owner's backend runs on a residential MX IP (zen.chernandez.dev) that can rotate, which would break the allowlist and (on Binance) auto-expire the key. The executor must run from a host with a stable egress IP — the Netcup VPS (152.53.167.28) in memory is the right home. Until a static IP is pinned, either run from the VPS or accept no-IP-lock + rely on no-withdrawal + the policy engine (weaker).

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: KYC IS A PRECONDITION (owner gate, not a code blocker). Full 300 req/min rate limit AND MXN/SPEI funding both require completed KYC (CURP/INE + selfie + proof of address). Trivial for a Mexican resident but must be done before any live trade.

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: TINY-SIZE FEE/SPREAD DRAG IS A SOFT ECONOMIC BLOCKER, not a trade blocker. Round-trip cost is ~0.7% (USD/stablecoin maker) to ~1.6% (MXN taker), and far worse (~3-4%) if the basic-app spread path is hit by mistake. At 11 USD the first trade must be treated as a validation/learning cost, not a profit expectation; the mechanical edge must clear this hurdle in the backtest gate.

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: TAX RECORD-KEEPING OBLIGATION FROM TRADE #1 (not a blocker, an obligation). Every disposal is an ISR enajenación event; the agent must log per-fill timestamp/pair/side/MXN+USD notional/price/fee (and ideally DOF FX rate) so year-end ISR is mechanical. Confirm specifics with SAT/a contador before scaling.

blockers.theme: Platform Selection for Mexico (treasuryforge Phase-3 real-money path, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: UNVERIFIED RELIABILITY/UPTIME (caution, not a blocker). Bitso's never-hacked / monthly zk-proof solvency / CoinCover-insurance claims are largely self-reported; no primary status-page/incident history was located. Build defensively: handle HTTP 429 with backoff (never auto-retry into a lockout), treat 5xx as transient non-fills (re-query order state, do not blindly resubmit), and treat venue outage as a first-class failure path.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: HARD GATE (not a true blocker, but mandatory ordering): the verified KYC tier (INE front+back + live selfie) plus a CURP must be completed BEFORE any SPEI deposit or trade. Budget up to 24h for approval; do not schedule the first trade as time-critical.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: HARD GATE: SPEI funding MUST come from a Mexican bank/fintech CLABE in the owner's OWN legal name matching KYC exactly. Third-party transfers (shared/family/different-name accounts) are rejected/returned. Blocks the timeline if the owner lacks a same-name MX bank account.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: POTENTIAL/UNCONFIRMED 2026 BLOCKER: biometric CURP became the reinforced-identity standard for the Mexican financial sector during early/mid-2026 (press dates disagree; no primary source names crypto exchanges/ITFs explicitly). If Bitso enforces biometric CURP for new account opening and the owner lacks one, he must obtain it at RENAPO/registro civil first. Verify in Bitso's live onboarding + with RENAPO; do NOT treat press dates as binding. LOW confidence.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: IP-ALLOWLIST FOOTGUN (operational blocker if mishandled): a dynamic/CGNAT residential Mexican IP will silently break an IP-allowlisted key. Either confirm a stable egress IP or run the executor from a fixed-IP host (the project already has a Netcup VPS 152.53.167.28). Note the residential MX IP is also the same kind of IP that has caused googlevideo 403s elsewhere — not relevant to Bitso REST but reinforces preferring a fixed VPS IP.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: WITHDRAWAL-WHITELIST FOOTGUN (only if withdrawals are ever enabled later): Bitso's per-key, per-asset withdrawal address allowlist treats an EMPTY list as 'any address allowed' (default-allow, not default-deny). For treasuryforge this is moot because the key is no-withdrawal, but it must never be left empty if withdrawals are ever turned on.

blockers.theme: Registration & KYC (Bitso, Mexico individual, ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: NOT verified / do NOT rely on without primary-source confirmation: (a) Mexican tax treatment (ISR on disposals, ~128,383 MXN enajenacion exemption, CARF reporting) — LOW confidence, confirm with SAT/contador before scaling; (b) Bitso Terms-of-Service stance on bot/automated trading for personal accounts — not confirmed from a primary source, read bitso.com/legal before going live; (c) whether a Bitso ACCOUNT-LEVEL (non-API) withdrawal-address whitelist exists — not clearly documented (verify in-app/with support); (d) the secondary '$200M SIM-swap theft' figure.

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: NONE are hard blockers for a Mexico-based individual on Bitso — the path is open. The items below are gating PRE-REQUISITES and CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY caveats, not blocks.

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: PRE-REQ: 2FA via authenticator app (Authy/Google Authenticator, NOT SMS) MUST be enabled on the Bitso account before the API-key page will issue any credentials. Without it, key creation is impossible.

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: PRE-REQ: KYC. The owner must complete standard individual KYC (full legal name, DOB, address, valid gov ID, likely CURP/proof-of-address for an MX resident) and fund ~200 MXN via SPEI before trading. Self-registerable; not a block, but it gates the first trade.

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (tax/legal): exact MX ISR treatment of crypto gains is UNCERTAIN — there is NO dedicated crypto chapter in the ISR Law. The cited ~60,000 MXN/yr personal-asset exemption and the '20% in some cases' (PRODECON) framing are SECONDARY-source interpretations and must NOT be treated as settled law. Confirm with a primary SAT source or a contador before relying on any rate/exemption. (Amounts at 200 MXN are negligible, but record-keeping is real from trade #1, and CARF/automatic-exchange reporting to SAT begins 2026.)

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (key longevity): Bitso docs are SILENT on whether an un-IP-restricted key auto-expires (unlike Binance/OKX which state limits explicitly). Absence of documentation is not proof of immortality — confirm with Bitso support before depending on indefinite key validity for an unattended agent.

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: CONFIRM-BEFORE-RELY (fees/funding): Bitso fee tiers are 30-day-volume based; verify the live 0.60%/0.78% tier shown in HIS account at trade time. If Kraken is used instead, verify the SPEI minimum (~150 MXN) and any ~0.50% SPEI funding fee on Kraken's own funding page (aggregator figures only).

blockers.theme: API Key Scope & Security (Phase-3 real-money path: MX individual, ~200 MXN / ~11 USD first trade)
blockers.blocker: OPERATIONAL (not a block, but causes silent failure if mishandled): if IP allowlisting is enabled on a residential dynamic MX IP, the key breaks on every ISP IP rotation. Mitigate by allowlisting the static Netcup VPS IP (152.53.167.28) instead, or launch with zero IPs at this size.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: KYC is a HARD prerequisite: the 300-rpm private tier and signed private endpoints require a fully identity-verified Bitso account. The owner must complete Bitso KYC (CURP/INE-based) himself before any live key works. Not a code issue but gates everything.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: No Kraken spot sandbox exists — Kraken's only official demo API is futures-only (demo-futures.kraken.com). This BLOCKS a Kraken-hosted spot-sandbox validation path but does NOT block the project (use Bitso + in-repo PaperExecutor). Do not design a Kraken-spot-sandbox executor.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: Bitso sandbox/stage is UNVERIFIED and shows staleness (deprecated Ropsten testnet, conflicting hostnames, possibly partner-gated). Until live-probed it cannot be relied on as the validation environment; the safe fallback is in-repo PaperExecutor + prod read-only key + one tiny live order.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: btc_mxn minimum-amount (0.003 BTC per one source) may vastly exceed 200 MXN at 2026 prices, which would hard-reject a 200 MXN BTC order. BLOCKS only if BTC is assumed; resolved by selecting the book at runtime from available_books. Do not assume btc_mxn works for an 11 USD order.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: Fee/spread drag is structurally high at 11 USD size (not a hard blocker but a real economic floor): Bitso MXN taker fees reach ~0.65% (maker ~0.50%), so a buy+sell round-trip is ~1.3% (~2.6 MXN) BEFORE bid-ask spread. At 200 MXN this drag plus spread means the mechanical strategy's per-trade edge must exceed ~1.3-2% just to break even — confirm against the Phase-2 cost-aware backtest gate before going autonomous.

blockers.theme: Safe API Validation Sequence — first ~200 MXN live trade on Bitso (Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: Tax/legal certainty is NOT verifiable from the sources gathered (all secondary blogs, not primary SAT text). The first 200 MXN BUY is non-taxable, so the first trade is unblocked — but before the strategy starts SELLING/swapping, ISR computation, the annual exemption figure (cited variously ~60,000 MXN or ~128,383 MXN / 3x UMA), and CARF reporting obligations MUST be confirmed with a contador or primary SAT source. Treat as a record-keeping obligation now, a confirm-before-scaling blocker later.

blockers.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: NO HARD BLOCKER prevents a Mexico-based individual from making the ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso. KYC is the standard Mexican-resident flow (CURP/INE); ~200 MXN clears all live minimums (10 MXN floor) with ~20x headroom; the trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted key model is natively supported.

blockers.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: ECONOMIC (soft) BLOCKER, not regulatory: fee drag of ~1.2–1.6% round-trip (live maker 0.60% / taker 0.78% per side) sets a per-trade hurdle the mechanical strategy MUST clear to be net-positive. A high-frequency strategy at this size is eaten by costs. This blocks PROFITABILITY, not the trade itself — frame trade #1 as a validation rehearsal.

blockers.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: OWNER-ACTION GATE (avoidable trap): do NOT use Bitso's consumer 'Convert'/'instant buy' widget — it bakes in an opaque ~1.5% spread (one agent could not retrieve the exact figure; treat as higher and opaque). Execute only through the Bitso Alpha / API order book. This is a process discipline item, not a platform limitation.

blockers.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: DUST TAIL (avoidable, code-guardable): Bitso has NO public dust-convert/sweep feature (unlike Binance BNB-convert). A residual crypto balance worth <10 MXN after fees/partial-fills/adverse-price would be permanently un-sellable. Mitigate with an all-or-nothing / 'never leave <10 MXN residual' executor invariant. Not a blocker for a single full-exit order.

blockers.theme: Minimum-Capital Mechanics (~11 USD / ~200 MXN first live trade)
blockers.blocker: TAX/LEGAL UNCERTAINTY (does not block 200 MXN, must confirm before SCALING): (a) the exact 2026 3x-UMA exemption peso figure must be confirmed from INEGI/DOF; (b) it is legally UNSETTLED (PRODECON characterization) whether crypto even qualifies for the Art. 93 'bienes muebles' exemption — it excludes titulos valor/inversiones. Do NOT hard-code an 'exempt below X' rule. Confirm with a Mexican contador / primary SAT source (LISR Art. 124, CFF Art. 30) before the FIRST SELL.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: NO HARD BLOCKER prevents a Mexico-based individual from making the trade — every required primitive (self-registration + KYC, trade-only/no-withdrawal/IP-allowlisted key, stdlib HMAC auth, 200 MXN > minimum, SPEI peso funding) is real and currently supported on Bitso. The items below are design gaps and unverified facts that must be closed BEFORE go-live, not legal/access blockers.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: CODEBASE DESIGN GAP (must fix before any live order): Intent has NO order-type, price, or post_only field, and NO quote_amount — verified in types.py (only symbol/side/base_amount/reason). It cannot express a post-only maker order or a 'spend exactly 200 MXN' (minor=200) buy. SimExecutor models a single immediate taker fill. The policy engine (policy.py, verified) sizes notional from base_amount*price and has no min-notional rule. Live orders REQUIRE choosing market vs limit. Must extend Intent with order_type (MARKET|LIMIT), limit_price, post_only, and an optional quote_amount, keeping MARKET default so the 55 existing tests + SimExecutor stay valid.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: FILL-MODEL GAP: live fills are partial/multi-trade and a post-only order may NOT fill at all, but Fill (verified types.py) is single-price/single-fee and SimExecutor always returns exactly one Fill. Executor must VWAP-aggregate executions into one Fill and add an explicit 'resting/unfilled' outcome so only real executions mutate the wallet (preserving the 'Fill is the only thing allowed to mutate the wallet' invariant).

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: IDEMPOTENCY HAZARD (not a blocker but mandatory-to-handle): origin_id uniqueness is ACTIVE-orders-only, so it cannot prevent a double-buy after a fast fill vanishes. Requires the reconcile-before-retry 3-state machine (CONFIRMED/REJECTED/UNKNOWN) cross-checking trade/ledger history, or blind retries can double-spend the treasury.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: TAX/LEGAL — UNVERIFIED, primary-source confirmation required: SAT treats crypto as a virtual asset and disposals (sell/trade/spend) can trigger taxable gains, but NO agent verified the exact 2026 SAT treatment from a primary source — all flagged it explicitly out-of-scope. This is NOT settled enough to assert numbers. The first 200 MXN trade is informational; confirm SAT/Fintech-Law treatment with SAT or a Mexican tax professional before scaling.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: UNVERIFIED-BEFORE-GO-LIVE facts (close each against the live API/docs, do not trust aggregators): (a) exact live btc_mxn minimum_value via GET /available_books; (b) actual account taker/maker fee via GET /api/v3/fees; (c) verbatim time_in_force='postonly' literal; (d) Bitso stage/sandbox account requires emailing Developer Support to clear SMS verification — an onboarding friction step.

blockers.theme: Executor Implementation
blockers.blocker: BINANCE-as-fallback caveat: a 2026-01-15 Binance change requires PERCENT-ENCODING the payload BEFORE HMAC (else -1022), and Binance enforces a clock-skew recvWindow (-1021) needing OS NTP sync — different signing rules than Bitso, so the fallback path is not a free swap.

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: NONE are hard blockers for the ~200 MXN first trade. The following are conditions/drags, not blockers:

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: Economic drag (not a feasibility blocker): round-trip taker fee floor of ~0.7%-1.6% (Bitso MXN book ~1.56%, Kraken ~0.80%, Bitso USDC ~0.72%) plus live spread must be cleared by strategy edge in the cost-aware backtest gate; a mechanical strategy that cannot beat ~1.6% round-trip is uneconomic at this venue/size.

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: Executor-design constraint (must be respected, else cost balloons): the instant-buy/convert path is forbidden — Kraken App instant-buy is 1.0%-1.5% and Bitso app buy/sell embeds an undisclosed spread, and neither is controllable via a trade-only, no-withdrawal, IP-allowlisted API key. The executor MUST use Kraken Pro / Bitso Alpha order-book API endpoints only.

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: Procedural/compliance obligation (cheap to satisfy, but real): record-keeping from trade #1 and likely an annual declaracion even with zero tax due. Flat CFF fines for an unfiled return (~$2,050-$25,360 MXN) or missing accounting records (up to ~$122,440 MXN) can dwarf the trade — neutralized for ~$0 by filing + keeping records.

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: Unverified tax specifics that must be confirmed with a contador/SAT BEFORE relying on net P&L or scaling: (a) the exact 2026 exemption figure and UMA-vs-salario basis; (b) whether any 20%-of-gross provisional ISR is withheld at source on small spot sales; (c) whether an autonomous high-frequency bot reclassifies the owner into 'persona fisica con actividad empresarial' (monthly declarations + electronic accounting + possible 16% IVA exposure).

blockers.theme: Fees, Spread & Mexican Tax
blockers.blocker: CARF/Regla 2.9.21 visibility (not a blocker, a posture change): from 2026 the exchange reports the owner's gross operations to SAT, so 'too small to be seen' is not a valid long-term posture; the owner's ledger must be reconcilable with exchange exports.

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: NO blocker prevents the ~200 MXN first trade. The items below are scoped, mostly self-inflicted-if-skipped, or scaling-gated.

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: SELF-INFLICTED RECOVERY BLOCKER (pre-trade, must do): At Bitso 2FA setup you must SAVE the emergency restore code offline BEFORE depositing. Losing BOTH the 2FA device and that code is the only scenario that forces slow, KYC-gated, text-only support recovery (Bitso has NO phone support; all recovery is Help Center ticket + customersupport@bitso.com).

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: BITSO PLATFORM DEFAULT-UNSAFE behavior (does not block, but must be respected): a key with 'Make withdrawals' ENABLED and an EMPTY whitelist fail-OPENS to ANY address. The platform fallback is permissive, not restrictive. Mitigated entirely by giving the agent a NO-withdrawal key (and no withdrawal code path).

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: ONE personal account per CURP/email: a Mexico individual CANNOT open a second personal Bitso account to physically separate trading vs reserve funds; duplicate-data detection can block one or both. This BLOCKS the 'two separate exchange accounts' separation pattern — replaced by logical separation (scoped keys + policy-engine reserve floor).

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: STRUCTURAL Mexico risks (do not block 11 USD, raise cost at scale): (a) NO IPAB/FDIC-equivalent deposit insurance covers crypto balances; (b) UIF can freeze accounts WITHOUT a judicial order/MP, and the Oct-2025 Amparo reform removes the usual injunction (suspension) defense in laundering-linked freezes — SPEI to/from the venue routes through this same banking surveillance; (c) Mexican law mandates NO client-asset segregation/bankruptcy-remoteness for non-bank crypto exchanges, and banks are barred (Banxico Circular 4/2019) from crypto custody. Mitigation costs nothing: fund via SPEI from the owner's OWN KYC-name-matched bank account in small explainable amounts, never route third-party money, keep the key trade-only so a freeze can't be amplified, and keep on-exchange balances tiny.

blockers.theme: Custody for Small Real Funds (~200 MXN / ~11 USD first live trade, Mexico individual)
blockers.blocker: TAX/LEGAL UNVERIFIED (must confirm before scaling/realizing gains, NOT a blocker for the buy): No agent retrieved a PRIMARY SAT source. Consensus from secondary guides: holding and BUYING are NOT taxable; only enajenacion (sale, crypto-to-crypto swap, or paying with crypto) triggers ISR (progressive up to 35%); wallet-to-own-wallet transfers are generally not a disposal (standard inference, not primary-confirmed). An annual asset-disposal exemption ~3x UMA exists but the exact 2026 figure is UNCERTAIN (one guide ~128,383 MXN, another ~60,000 MXN). At 200 MXN gains fall far below any threshold so likely no ISR is owed, BUT records must be kept from day one. Under OECD CARF (effective April 2026) Bitso reports your balances and gross annual operations to SAT regardless of self-custody. CONFIRM all tax treatment with a Mexican contador or primary sat.gob.mx text before realizing gains.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: KYC IS MANDATORY FROM THE FIRST PESO: Bitso (CNBV-regulated) requires customer identification/KYC before any virtual-asset activity. Not a per-tx constraint, but a hard registration gate the owner must clear before any trade. Cannot be bypassed.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: NO EXCHANGE-SIDE DEAD-MAN-SWITCH ON BITSO: unlike Kraken (cancelAllOrdersAfter), Bybit, BitMEX, Binance Futures, Bitso has NO server-side cancel-on-disconnect/countdown. If BOTH the agent and a client-side watchdog die, resting orders are NOT auto-cancelled. Mitigation: market-only orders at this size leave nothing resting (near-zero residual risk) + build a client-side watchdog. BLOCKS a true server-side DMS but is worked around, not fatal.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: NO REGULATORY RECOURSE FOR SELF-INFLICTED LOSSES: crypto sits outside CONDUSEF/Banxico consumer-finance protection in Mexico; bot-made (authorized) trades cannot be reversed by any regulator. The 200 MXN must be treated as fully at-risk burn-in capital. CONDUSEF's fintech portal covers account-TAKEOVER/unauthorized-access only, not the owner's own key's bad trades.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: TAX/LEGAL TREATMENT IS UNVERIFIED: no agent fetched an authoritative SAT/CNBV primary source. The persona-fisica 2026 gain-exemption figure CONFLICTS across secondary sources (60,000 vs 128,383 MXN). At 200 MXN any gain is immaterial and tax does NOT block the test trade, but the exact exemption, ISR rates, provisional-withholding mechanics, and CARF (operational 2026) reporting MUST be confirmed with SAT or a contador before scaling. Treat all cited tax figures as UNCONFIRMED.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: DYNAMIC RESIDENTIAL IP vs STRICT IP-ALLOWLIST: a residential rotating public IP will 403 the key on change if a strict allowlist is set. This is FAIL-CLOSED (safe) but presents as an outage. Workaround: run from the fixed-IP Debian VPS and allowlist that, OR re-add the IP on change, OR skip the allowlist (Bitso doesn't force it) and rely on trade-only/no-withdrawal scoping for blast radius.

blockers.theme: Risk & Ops for First Real Money (MX individual, ~200 MXN first trade on Bitso)
blockers.blocker: CLOCK SKEW ON THE RESIDENTIAL WINDOWS HOST: Bitso auth signs requests with a nonce/timestamp; local clock drift surfaces as intermittent SILENT auth rejections (order never placed -> agent thinks it's flat). Must verify Windows NTP (w32time) sync and refuse to trade if drift > ~1-2s. NOTE: Bitso's exact HMAC/nonce/timestamp signing rules ('Nonce v2', 2025-03-14) were NOT fully verified in this research and MUST be read from Bitso's auth docs before coding the signer.

blockers.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
blockers.blocker: NONE that legally stop the ~200 MXN first trade. Every agent classifies the regulatory/legal dimension as ENABLES for an own-funds individual.

blockers.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
blockers.blocker: SOFT/PROCEDURAL GATE (not a legal blocker): mandatory exchange KYC must be PASSED before any order — INE/CURP + selfie/liveness, possibly proof of address; budget minutes-to-hours. Trading is gated until verification completes.

blockers.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
blockers.blocker: BRIGHT LINE TO NEVER CROSS (would convert legal hobby into regulated/illegal activity): never accept, pool, hold custody of, or trade ANY third-party funds, and never offer habitual/professional individualized paid recommendations or market the agent as a money-management/advisory service — that triggers CNBV 'Asesor en Inversiones' registration (LMV Art. 225) and/or the LFPIORPI 'actividad vulnerable' provider regime. Keep treasuryforge strictly single-user/own-funds.

blockers.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
blockers.blocker: TAX OBLIGATION (does not block the trade, but is a real individual-side duty): ISR on realized gains; every crypto-to-crypto swap AND every sell-to-MXN is a taxable disposal. Negligible at 11 USD, but the obligation and record-keeping start at trade #1. Exact exemption/threshold figures are UNVERIFIED against primary SAT text — confirm with a contador before any trade large enough to realize meaningful gains.

blockers.theme: Regulatory & Legal (Individual, Mexico) — real-money path for a ~200 MXN first live crypto trade
blockers.blocker: CUSTODY/RECOURSE CAVEAT (not a blocker at this size): exchange balances are UNINSURED — no IPAB coverage for crypto, Bitso does not insure fiat balances (only a private Coincover/Lloyd's crypto-THEFT policy, not insolvency/fiat); CONDUSEF reaches only the CNBV-registered fiat leg (NVIO Pagos Mexico) and is non-binding mediation. Acceptable at 11 USD; re-evaluate before scaling balances.
